subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Parts of Speech Review
subjects.summary: Review nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs — the building blocks of sentences.

quiz.q: In 'The exhausted hikers slowly climbed the steep trail,' which word is the adverb?
options: Hikers
Slowly
Steep
Exhausted

quiz.q: Which sentence uses 'run' as a noun, not a verb?
options: She will run to school.
They run every day.
Dogs run fast.
The team won their morning run.

quiz.q: In 'The curious puppy sniffed the ball with excitement,' which word is the adjective?
options: Sniffed
Puppy
Curious
Excitement

quiz.q: Which word in 'He carefully wrapped the fragile gift' tells you HOW he wrapped it?
options: Carefully
Wrapped
Fragile
Gift

quiz.q: In 'Her quick thinking saved the day,' what part of speech is 'quick'?
options: An adjective describing 'thinking'
A verb showing an action
An adverb describing 'saved'
A noun naming 'thinking'

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Multiplication Facts and Strategies
subjects.summary: Ontario Number strand: by the end of Grade 5, students should have multiplication/division facts mastered. Review times tables and strategies.

quiz.q: A box holds 8 rows of 7 apples. If 2 apples are removed, how many are left?
Which strategy correctly finds 9 × 6 using known facts?
options: Both the first and second

quiz.q: If 6 × n = 48, what is n?
A classroom has 24 students seated in equal rows of 6. How many rows are there?
Which strategy would NOT correctly give you 7 × 8?
options: 7 × 4, then double it
5 × 8, then add 2 × 8
7 × 10, then subtract 7 × 2
6 × 8, then subtract 8

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Forces Acting on Structures
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Science strand: structures must withstand forces like tension, compression, and load to stay stable.

quiz.q: An engineer designs a bridge that must support heavy trucks. Which forces matter most?
options: Neither — bridges don't experience forces
Only tension
Only compression
Both tension and compression

quiz.q: Why do bridges and towers often use triangular shapes instead of squares?
options: A triangle holds its shape better
Squares aren't allowed in construction
Triangles always use less material
Triangles are cheaper to build

quiz.q: A tall tower has a wide base that narrows toward the top. How does this help?
options: It only changes how the tower looks
It lowers the centre of gravity and improves stability
It only makes the tower lighter
It has no effect on stability

quiz.q: If you press down on both ends of a sponge, which force are you applying?
options: Gravity only
Tension
Compression
Torsion

quiz.q: A rope holding up a swing is pulled tight by a child's weight. Which force acts on the rope?
options: Tension
Friction only
Compression
No force at all

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Early Encounters: First Peoples and New France
subjects.summary: Ontario Heritage & Identity strand: explore the diverse First Nations societies in what would become Canada, and early encounters with French settlers before 1713.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Early Encounters: First Peoples and New France

quiz.q: Why is it important to consider BOTH First Nations and European perspectives on early encounters?
options: First Nations perspectives aren't needed
Only European accounts are historically accurate
Each group experienced these events differently
There was only one shared perspective

quiz.q: Which of these was a direct RESULT of contact between French settlers and First Nations peoples?
options: Complete isolation between the two groups
Immediate agreement on all matters
No changes to either group's way of life
Trade networks and alliances

quiz.q: Before European contact, First Nations societies across what became Canada were best described as...
options: Diverse nations with many languages
Entirely nomadic with no settlements
A single unified nation with one language
Without any form of trade or governance

quiz.q: New France was established primarily to...
options: Isolate France from other European powers
Return First Nations peoples to Europe
Provide a vacation destination for French royalty
Expand French trade and territory

quiz.q: A historian studying this period would find which question MOST useful?
options: How did different groups record events differently?
Which version can be ignored?
None of these are useful questions
What is the single correct version of events?

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Sentence Types
subjects.summary: Sentences can be declarative (statement), interrogative (question), imperative (command), or exclamatory (exclamation).

quiz.q: Which of these is an imperative sentence that also shows urgency?
options: Could you please close the door?
The door is closed.
Is the door closed?
Close the door now!

quiz.q: Which is the correct interrogative form of 'You are finished with your homework'?
options: Finish your homework.
Are you finished with your homework?
You are finished with your homework!
You are finished with your homework.

quiz.q: Which sentence type typically ends with an exclamation mark AND expresses strong emotion?
options: Imperative
Interrogative
Exclamatory
Declarative

quiz.q: 'Watch out for that car!' combines features of which two sentence types?
options: Imperative and exclamatory
Declarative and interrogative
Interrogative and exclamatory
Declarative and imperative

quiz.q: Which sentence is punctuated INCORRECTLY for its type?
options: Did you finish your project?
What an amazing goal!
Please pass the salt.
Stop right there?

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Long Division
subjects.summary: Divide larger numbers using long division, checking your answer with multiplication.

quiz.q: A school places 138 chairs into rows of 12. How many full rows, and how many chairs are left over?
options: 12 rows, 5 left over
11 rows, 5 left over
12 rows, 6 left over
11 rows, 6 left over

quiz.q: Which division problem gives a remainder of exactly 3?
A recipe splits 245 mL of juice equally among 7 cups. How many mL go in each cup?
options: 34 mL
35 mL
33 mL
36 mL

quiz.q: Which calculation correctly checks that 84 ÷ 7 = 12?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Beams, Columns, and Stability
subjects.summary: Beams support loads horizontally, columns support loads vertically, and a structure's base affects how stable it is.

quiz.q: A table has four thin legs vs. one thick central pedestal leg. Which is generally MORE stable, and why?
options: Both are equally stable regardless of base width
The four-legged table, because a wider base spreads the load
The pedestal table, because one leg is simpler
Neither — stability depends only on colour

quiz.q: An architect needs to span a wide gap between two walls with nothing underneath. Which element should they use?
options: A foundation only
A pillar only
A beam
A column

quiz.q: Why might engineers add triangular braces between a tower's vertical columns?
options: Triangular braces serve no structural purpose
To reduce the tower's height
To resist sideways forces on the frame
To make the tower more colourful

quiz.q: A stool with legs splayed outward is generally more stable than one with straight vertical legs because it has...
options: A wider base of support
Less material
Less weight
Fewer legs

quiz.q: If a beam bends too much under a load, what is the MOST likely engineering fix?
options: Paint the beam a different colour
Make the beam thinner
Remove the beam entirely
Use a thicker or stronger beam

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: First Nations, Métis, and Inuit Peoples
subjects.summary: Canada is home to diverse Indigenous peoples — First Nations, Métis, and Inuit — each with distinct histories, cultures, and territories.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: First Nations, Métis, and Inuit Peoples

quiz.q: Which statement BEST explains why 'Indigenous peoples of Canada' is not one uniform group?
options: There are no real differences between these groups
Indigenous peoples only include the Inuit
All Indigenous peoples share one identical culture
First Nations, Métis, and Inuit each have distinct cultures

quiz.q: The traditional Inuit way of life was most closely shaped by...
options: Desert conditions
The Arctic environment and its resources
A tropical, humid climate
Dense rainforest ecosystems

quiz.q: The Métis people emerged historically from...
options: Only Inuit ancestry
Only recent immigration
No historical connection to Canada
First Nations and European ancestry

quiz.q: Why do historians emphasize learning about MULTIPLE Indigenous perspectives rather than one 'Indigenous history'?
options: Because each group has distinct experiences
To make history more confusing
There's no reason to do this
Only one perspective actually matters

quiz.q: How does 'First Nations' relate to specific nations like the Anishinaabe or Haudenosaunee?
options: It's an umbrella term for many distinct nations
These are unrelated terms
First Nations is one specific nation with no smaller groups
First Nations only refers to modern government bands

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Using Context Clues
subjects.summary: Context clues are hints in the surrounding text that help you figure out the meaning of an unfamiliar word.

quiz.q: In 'The famished travelers devoured their meal within minutes,' which clue helps you know 'famished' means very hungry?
options: There is no clue available
A contrast/antonym clue
Their devouring the meal quickly
A direct definition given in the sentence

quiz.q: 'Unlike her gregarious brother, Mia preferred quiet evenings alone.' Based on the contrast, 'gregarious' most likely means...
options: Angry and loud
Tired and sleepy
Outgoing and sociable
Shy and quiet

quiz.q: Which type of context clue gives a word's meaning directly in the same sentence, often set off by commas?
options: Contrast clue
Inference clue
Example clue
Definition clue

quiz.q: 'The recipe called for a pinch of saffron, cumin, and other exotic spices from distant lands.' Which clue helps define 'exotic'?
options: No clue is present
A definition stated directly
Naming spices from far away
A contrast with a familiar word

quiz.q: 'Despite the drought, the resilient cactus thrived.' What does 'resilient' most likely mean here?
options: Colourful
Extremely large
Able to survive and recover
Weak and easily damaged

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Equivalent Fractions
subjects.summary: Equivalent fractions represent the same value, like 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6.

quiz.q: Which fraction is NOT equivalent to 3/4?
Order these fractions from least to greatest: 2/3, 5/6, 1/2
A pizza is cut into 8 equal slices. If you eat 2 slices, what fraction (simplest form) did you eat?
Which pair of fractions can be compared most easily using a common denominator of 12?
If 4/6 = x/9, what is x?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Properties of Matter
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Science strand: matter has measurable properties like mass, volume, density, and the ability to dissolve.

quiz.q: Two objects have the same volume, but Object A is heavier than Object B. What can you conclude?
options: Object A has greater density than Object B
Density cannot be determined
They have the same density
Object A has less density than Object B

quiz.q: A sugar cube stirred into water disappears from sight. Has its mass been destroyed?
options: No, it's just dissolved, still present
Yes, it turned into a gas
Yes, the mass is gone forever
No, it turned into a new element

quiz.q: Why does a solid block of a material usually have a HIGHER density than the same material as a loose powder?
options: Density doesn't depend on particle arrangement
It packs more mass into the same volume
The powder is always heavier
Solids always weigh less than powders

quiz.q: An object floats in water. What does this suggest about its density compared to water?
options: The object is denser than water
The object is less dense than water
There is no relationship between floating and density
The object has no mass

quiz.q: Which pair of properties would you need to CALCULATE an object's density?
options: Mass and volume
Colour and smell
Temperature and taste
Sound and texture

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Government in Canada
subjects.summary: Canada has different levels of government — federal, provincial, and municipal — each responsible for different things.

quiz.q: A city council decides to build a new public library. Which level of government is responsible?
options: Municipal
Federal
None — libraries are privately funded only
Provincial

quiz.q: Which issue would MOST LIKELY be handled by the federal government rather than the province?
options: Local snow removal
Immigration policy
School curriculum for Ontario
Provincial highway speed limits

quiz.q: How do Ontario's Premier and Canada's Prime Minister differ?
options: Only one of them is elected
They have identical responsibilities
The Premier leads the province, the PM the country
The Premier leads the federal government and the PM leads Ontario

quiz.q: Why does Canada divide responsibilities among federal, provincial, and municipal governments?
options: So each level handles issues at its own scale
Because the federal government has no real power
There is no particular reason
To create unnecessary confusion

quiz.q: Healthcare funding and delivery in Ontario is mainly overseen by...
options: The provincial government
Municipal governments only
The federal government only
No government involvement

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Cause and Effect
subjects.summary: A cause is why something happens, and an effect is what happens as a result.

quiz.q: 'Because the road was icy, the bus arrived late, which caused Maria to miss the morning announcements.' What is the ORIGINAL cause?
options: The road was icy
Maria missed the announcements
The bus arrived late
The class started

quiz.q: In that same sentence, 'the bus arrived late' functions as...
options: Only an effect
Both an effect and a cause
Neither a cause nor an effect
Only a cause

quiz.q: Which sentence shows cause and effect WITHOUT an obvious signal word like 'because' or 'so'?
options: It snowed. School was cancelled.
Since it snowed, school was cancelled.
Because it snowed, school was cancelled.
School was cancelled because it snowed.

quiz.q: 'The factory increased production, so more workers were hired, and traffic grew heavier.' How many cause-and-effect links are shown?
options: Three
Two
One
None

quiz.q: Which question would help you identify the EFFECT in a passage?
options: Why did the author write this?
What happened as a result?
Where does the story take place?
What is the main character's name?

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Adding and Subtracting Fractions
subjects.summary: Add and subtract fractions with the same denominator, and simplify your answer.

quiz.q: A recipe uses 1/4 cup of sugar and 1/4 cup of brown sugar. How much sugar in total, simplest form?
options: 2/8 cup
3/4 cup
1/8 cup
1/2 cup

quiz.q: You have 5/6 of a pizza and eat 2/6. How much is left, in simplest form?
Which pair of fractions can be added directly WITHOUT changing either denominator?
A ribbon is 7/8 m long. If 3/8 m is cut off, how much remains, in simplest form?
Which expression equals exactly 1 whole?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Conservation of Energy and Resources
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Science strand: energy can be transformed but not created or destroyed; renewable resources can be replenished while non-renewable resources cannot.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Conservation of Energy and Resources

quiz.q: A dam converts flowing water's energy into electricity. What does conservation of energy say happens to the total energy?
options: Energy is created from nothing
Energy is destroyed as it becomes electricity
Energy changes form, but the total amount stays the same
Energy simply disappears

quiz.q: Which choice is an example of conserving a non-renewable resource?
options: Carpooling to reduce gasoline use
Using more coal for heating
Wasting paper unnecessarily
Leaving lights on all day

quiz.q: Why can solar and wind energy be used repeatedly without running out, unlike coal or oil?
options: They are man-made and unlimited by design
Coal and oil are actually unlimited too
They are naturally replenished, unlike fossil fuels
There is no real difference between them

quiz.q: A factory switches from burning coal to wind turbines for electricity. What's the MOST LIKELY benefit?
options: No change in resource use
Increased use of non-renewable resources
No benefit at all
Reduced use of non-renewable resources

quiz.q: If energy can't be created or destroyed, why do we say energy is 'lost' as heat in machines?
options: Machines create new energy from nothing
This is a myth — no energy ever changes form
Energy is literally destroyed in machines
It isn't destroyed, but changes into a less useful form, like waste heat

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada's Physical Regions
subjects.summary: Canada is divided into physical regions (like the Canadian Shield, the Prairies, and the Cordillera), each with distinct landforms and climate.

quiz.q: A farmer looking for large areas of flat, fertile land for growing wheat would most likely settle in...
options: The Cordillera
The Canadian Shield
The Arctic
The Prairies

quiz.q: Why does the Canadian Shield have relatively poor farmland despite covering a huge area?
options: It never receives any rain
It is entirely underwater
It has no land at all
It is mostly rocky, ancient terrain

quiz.q: A ski resort company looking to build in Canada would most likely choose which region, and why?
options: The Cordillera, for its mountainous terrain
The Shield, for its farmland
The Prairies, for their flat land
The Arctic, for its warm climate

quiz.q: Which factor is the MOST DIRECT reason different Canadian regions have different climates?
options: Provincial borders determine climate
Differences in latitude, elevation, and distance from oceans
All regions actually share the same climate
Random chance with no pattern

quiz.q: A region with 'ancient rock, thousands of lakes, and boreal forest' describes which physical region?
options: The Canadian Shield
The Arctic
The Prairies
The Cordillera

label: Day 5 — Fri (Review)
reviewNote: Review day: mixed questions from Days 1–4.

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Review: Grammar and Comprehension
subjects.summary: Mixed review of parts of speech, sentence types, context clues, and cause/effect.

quiz.q: In 'The exhausted but determined runner pushed through the final kilometre,' which word is an adjective describing 'runner'?
options: Kilometre
Through
Pushed
Exhausted

quiz.q: 'Could you please hand me that book?' combines the tone of a question with the function of a...
options: Exclamation
Simple statement
None of these
Polite command (imperative)

quiz.q: Using context clues, what does 'meticulous' most likely mean in: 'She was meticulous, checking every detail twice before submitting her work'?
options: Extremely careful and detail-oriented
Fast and rushed
Confused
Careless

quiz.q: 'The heavy rain caused the river to flood, which forced the family to evacuate.' What is the FINAL effect in this chain?
options: The family evacuating
None of these
Heavy rain
The river flooding

quiz.q: Which sentence uses figurative language?
options: The lake was a sparkling mirror under the sun.
The lake is located in Ontario.
The lake was 3 kilometres wide.
The lake froze in winter.

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Review: Multiplication, Division, and Fractions
subjects.summary: Mixed review of multiplication, division, and fraction operations.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Multiplication, Division, and Fractions

quiz.q: A baker makes 6 batches of 9 muffins each, then sells 14. How many muffins remain?
Which fraction is equivalent to 3/4?
5/8 − 2/8 = ? (simplest form)
A rectangular garden is 6 m by 4 m. How does its area compare to a square garden with side 5 m?
options: 24 m², smaller than the 25 m² square
20 m², smaller than the square
24 m², equal to the square
24 m², larger than the 25 m² square

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Structures, Matter, and Energy
subjects.summary: Mixed review of forces on structures, properties of matter, and conservation of energy.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Structures, Matter, and Energy

quiz.q: Why might engineers choose triangular supports over square ones in a bridge design?
options: Squares are always stronger
There is no structural reason
Triangles resist changing shape under force
Triangles are only used for decoration

quiz.q: Two blocks have equal mass, but Block X has a larger volume than Block Y. Which has greater density?
options: Block X
Block Y
Cannot be determined
They are equal

quiz.q: A wind turbine generates electricity. According to conservation of energy, where does this energy ultimately come from?
options: It has no origin
It is destroyed and recreated
It is created from nothing by the turbine
Kinetic energy of moving air, from the sun

quiz.q: Which pair correctly matches a body system with its main function?
options: Respiratory system — exchanges oxygen and carbon dioxide
Circulatory system — supports and protects the body
Skeletal system — pumps blood
Digestive system — filters air

quiz.q: A sponge is squeezed from both sides. Which force is applied, and what happens to its shape?
options: Torsion; it twists
No force; nothing changes
Compression; it becomes smaller/compacted
Tension; it stretches longer

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Review: Early Canada and Government
subjects.summary: Mixed review of early encounters, Indigenous peoples, government levels, and physical regions.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Early Canada and Government

quiz.q: Why is it inaccurate to describe all Indigenous peoples in Canada as a single group?
options: Only the Inuit are considered Indigenous
There is no meaningful difference between them
First Nations, Métis, and Inuit each differ
This description is actually accurate

quiz.q: Which decision would most likely be made by a MUNICIPAL government rather than the federal government?
options: Deciding on a new city bus route
Setting immigration policy
Managing the national military
Negotiating international trade agreements

quiz.q: A region with flat, fertile land ideal for growing wheat is most likely which Canadian physical region?
options: The Cordillera
The Arctic
The Canadian Shield
The Prairies

quiz.q: Why was the fur trade significant to the economic development of New France?
options: It was banned by the French government
It only affected First Nations, not settlers
It generated wealth and shaped settlement patterns
It had no economic effect

quiz.q: Why does Canada have three separate branches of government (legislative, executive, judicial)?
options: To balance power so no single branch has total control
There is no real reason
To make government slower on purpose
Because one branch alone could not exist

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Compare and Contrast
subjects.summary: Comparing shows how two things are alike; contrasting shows how they are different.

quiz.q: In 'Unlike the quiet library, the busy market buzzed with noise,' which relationship is shown?
options: Comparison only
Contrast
Cause and effect
Chronological order

quiz.q: Which pair of sentences primarily shows a COMPARISON (similarity)?
options: Winter is cold; summer is hot.
Cats are quiet, while dogs are loud.
Both the wolf and the dog are canines that live in packs.
The desert is dry, but the rainforest is wet.

quiz.q: A Venn diagram with two overlapping circles is useful for showing...
options: Only differences between two things
Only similarities between two things
Both similarities (overlap) and differences (non-overlap)
Neither similarities nor differences

quiz.q: 'Both cheetahs and gazelles are fast, but cheetahs are predators while gazelles are prey.' This sentence uses...
options: Only comparison
Both comparison and contrast
Only contrast
Neither

quiz.q: Which transition word would BEST introduce a contrasting idea comparing two characters?
options: Also
Similarly
Likewise
In contrast

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Decimal Place Value
subjects.summary: Decimals represent parts of a whole using place value — tenths, hundredths, and thousandths.

quiz.q: Which decimal is equivalent to 45/100?
Order from least to greatest: 0.5, 0.45, 0.54
In the number 6.784, what is the value of the digit 8?
options: 8 thousandths
8 ones
8 tenths
8 hundredths

quiz.q: Which decimal rounds to 3.8 when rounded to the nearest tenth?
Which digit is in the hundredths place of 12.639?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Changes in Matter
subjects.summary: Matter can change physically (like cutting paper) or chemically (like burning wood, which forms new substances).

quiz.q: Which of these changes CANNOT be easily reversed?
options: Melting butter
Burning a piece of wood
Cutting a sheet of paper
Freezing water into ice

quiz.q: A scientist mixes vinegar and baking soda and sees bubbles forming. This is evidence of a...
options: No change at all
Change in colour only
Physical change only
Chemical change — a new gas formed

quiz.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes physical and chemical changes?
options: Physical changes alter form but not identity; chemical changes create new substances
There is no real difference
Physical changes always involve heat; chemical changes never do
Chemical changes are always reversible; physical changes are not

quiz.q: Dissolving salt in water is a physical change because...
options: The salt disappears forever
It can be separated again (evaporation)
It cannot be reversed
A new substance is permanently created

quiz.q: Which is the BEST evidence that a chemical change has occurred?
options: A substance with new properties formed
The object changed shape
The object got colder
The object got smaller

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canadian Citizenship and Identity
subjects.summary: Canadian identity is shaped by diverse cultures, languages, and experiences, and citizens have both rights and responsibilities.

quiz.q: Which best describes rights and responsibilities for Canadian citizens?
options: Citizens only have rights, no responsibilities
Both rights and responsibilities apply
Citizens only have responsibilities, no rights
Neither applies to citizens

quiz.q: Why is Canada often described as a 'mosaic' rather than a 'melting pot'?
options: Cultures keep their distinct identities
There is no difference between the two models
Canada encourages cultures to blend into one single identity
Canada has no cultural diversity

quiz.q: A new immigrant learning both English and French reflects which aspect of Canadian identity?
options: A requirement in every country
Something unrelated to citizenship
An uncommon, unofficial practice
Canada's two official languages

quiz.q: Which action demonstrates a citizen exercising a RIGHT rather than fulfilling a RESPONSIBILITY?
options: Serving on a jury when called
Obeying traffic laws
Voting in an election
Paying taxes

quiz.q: Why might cultural identity be described as something that can change and grow over a person's life?
options: Identity never changes once you're born
Shaped by experience and community
Culture has no effect on identity
Only your birthplace determines identity forever

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Fact vs. Opinion
subjects.summary: A fact can be proven true or false; an opinion is a personal belief or feeling.

quiz.q: Which sentence blends a fact with an opinion?
options: Toronto is a city in Ontario.
Toronto has cold winters.
Toronto is located on Lake Ontario.
Toronto, the best city, is huge.

quiz.q: What is the best test for whether a statement is a fact?
options: Whether most people agree with it
Whether it sounds convincing
Whether it can be proven true or false with evidence
Whether it uses strong language

quiz.q: 'Hockey is Canada's most exciting sport' is best classified as...
options: A fact, because hockey is popular in Canada
An opinion — that's a personal judgment
Both equally
Neither fact nor opinion

quiz.q: Which phrase is the strongest signal that an opinion follows?
options: I believe that...
The tallest mountain is...
Water freezes at...

quiz.q: A weather report saying 'Today will feel unpleasantly cold' mixes fact and opinion because...
options: Temperature's fact; feeling's opinion
Weather reports never contain opinions
Both parts are pure fact
Both parts are pure opinion

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Multiplying and Dividing Decimals
subjects.summary: Multiply and divide decimal numbers, paying attention to place value and the decimal point.

quiz.q: A ribbon costs $0.75 per metre. How much would 6 metres cost?
Which expression gives the SAME result as 0.4 × 0.5?
options: 4 × 5, then divide by 10
4 + 5, divided by 100
4 × 5, then divide by 100

quiz.q: A runner completes a lap in 2.5 minutes. At that pace, how long would 3 laps take?
options: 7.0 minutes
8.0 minutes
7.5 minutes
6.5 minutes

quiz.q: Which calculation correctly finds how many 0.25 kg bags can be made from 3 kg of rice?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Human Body Systems Overview
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Life Systems strand: the digestive, respiratory, circulatory, and skeletal-muscular systems work together to keep us alive and moving.

quiz.q: After you eat, which TWO systems work together most directly to get nutrients to your muscles?
options: Skeletal and respiratory systems
Only the digestive system alone
Digestive and circulatory systems
Only the muscular system alone

quiz.q: If the respiratory system fails to bring in enough oxygen, which OTHER system is most directly affected?
options: The skeletal system, since bones need oxygen to grow
The circulatory system, since blood carries oxygen through the body
No other system would be affected
The digestive system, since it doesn't rely on oxygen

quiz.q: Which body system provides the structural framework that protects organs like the heart and lungs?
options: Circulatory system
Skeletal system
Digestive system
Respiratory system

quiz.q: Why are body systems often described as working 'together' rather than independently?
options: Each system works completely alone
Systems compete against each other
Each system depends on the others
Only one system matters

quiz.q: A doctor listens to a patient's heartbeat and breathing with a stethoscope. Which two systems are being checked?
options: Circulatory and respiratory
Skeletal and circulatory
Digestive and respiratory
Digestive and skeletal

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Natural Resources Across Canada's Regions
subjects.summary: Different regions of Canada have different natural resources (like forests, minerals, fish, and farmland) that shape how people live and work there.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Natural Resources Across Canada's Regions

quiz.q: A community built around a large fishing industry is most likely located in which type of region?
options: A landlocked prairie region
A coastal region
A mountain region
A desert region

quiz.q: Why do the Prairie provinces have a strong agricultural economy based on grain?
options: They have no natural resources at all
They are located in the Arctic
They are surrounded by mountains
Their flat land and fertile soil suit large-scale farming

quiz.q: How does a region's natural resources typically shape the JOBS available there?
options: Jobs are randomly distributed regardless of resources
Industries develop around using and processing local resources, creating related jobs
Natural resources have no connection to employment
Only government jobs exist in resource regions

quiz.q: Why does using resources responsibly matter for FUTURE generations?
options: Only the current generation is affected
There is no connection between use and the future
Resources always regenerate instantly, so use doesn't matter
Overusing resources today can leave less available or damage the environment long-term

quiz.q: A logging company plants new trees after harvesting old ones. This practice is an example of...
options: Wasting resources
A tradition unrelated to conservation
Sustainable resource management
Ignoring the economy

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Figurative Language
subjects.summary: Figurative language includes similes (using 'like' or 'as'), metaphors, and personification, used to make writing more vivid.

quiz.q: 'The classroom was a zoo during the fire drill' is an example of...
options: Onomatopoeia
Metaphor
Simile
A literal statement

quiz.q: Which sentence uses personification?
options: The wind howled through the empty streets.
The wind blew at 40 km/h.
The wind is a type of weather.
The wind was as strong as a hurricane.

quiz.q: Why might a writer choose 'Her eyes were pools of sorrow' instead of 'She looked very sad'?
options: It creates a more vivid image
It's shorter to write
It removes all emotion
It's grammatically required

quiz.q: Which phrase is LITERAL, not figurative?
options: The stadium roared with excitement.
Time flew by during the movie.
The runner finished the race in 12 minutes.
Her smile lit up the room.

quiz.q: 'The old car coughed and sputtered before finally starting' gives the car human-like qualities. This is...
options: Personification
Alliteration
A simile
A fact

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Area and Perimeter
subjects.summary: Perimeter is the distance around a shape; area is the space inside a shape, measured in square units.

quiz.q: A rectangular garden has a perimeter of 24 m. If its width is 4 m, what is its length?
options: 6 m
8 m

quiz.q: Two rectangles have the same area but different shapes. What must be true?
options: Same length×width, different perimeter
They are identical shapes
This situation is impossible
They must also have the same perimeter

quiz.q: A square has an area of 49 cm². What is its perimeter?
options: 7 cm
14 cm
49 cm
28 cm

quiz.q: A rectangular room is 8 m long and 5 m wide. To put a border strip around the entire edge, which measurement do you need?
options: Area (40 m²)
Only the length
Volume
Perimeter (26 m)

quiz.q: A rectangle's length is doubled while its width stays the same. What happens to its area?
options: It doubles
It stays the same
It is cut in half
It quadruples

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Energy Sources and Transformations
subjects.summary: Energy can come from many sources and can be transformed from one form to another, like electrical energy becoming light or heat.

quiz.q: A student rubs their hands together quickly and feels warmth. This shows energy transforming from...
options: Chemical energy into electrical energy
Light energy into sound energy
Nothing — no transformation occurs
Mechanical (motion) energy into heat energy

quiz.q: Which sequence correctly traces the energy in a wind turbine powering a lamp?
options: Light energy -> wind energy -> electrical energy
There is no transformation sequence
Wind, mechanical, electrical, then light
Electrical energy -> wind energy -> light energy

quiz.q: Why is a car engine considered inefficient, even though it moves the car?
options: All the energy converts perfectly with no loss
Most of the energy is lost as heat
Cars do not use energy transformations
Efficiency is unrelated to energy

quiz.q: Which energy source requires burning fuel to release its stored chemical energy?
options: A hydroelectric dam
Wind turbines
Solar panels
A coal power plant

quiz.q: A flashlight uses a battery to produce light. What is the correct order of energy transformation?
options: Electrical energy -> chemical energy -> light energy
Chemical energy (battery) -> electrical energy -> light energy
Mechanical energy -> light energy -> chemical energy
Light energy -> electrical energy -> chemical energy

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Map Skills: Latitude and Longitude
subjects.summary: Lines of latitude and longitude form a grid that helps us pinpoint exact locations on Earth.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Map Skills: Latitude and Longitude

quiz.q: A location is described as 45°N, 75°W. What does the 'N' tell you?
options: It is north of the prime meridian
It is north of the equator
It is in the Northern Hemisphere by longitude
It has no meaning

quiz.q: Which pair of coordinates would be needed to locate a city PRECISELY on a map?
options: Only latitude
Neither is needed
Only longitude
Both latitude and longitude together

quiz.q: If you travel due north from the equator, your latitude value will...
options: Stay exactly the same
Become negative
Increase toward 90°N
Decrease toward 0°

quiz.q: The prime meridian is a line of...
options: Longitude, dividing Earth into eastern and western hemispheres
Neither latitude nor longitude
Latitude, dividing Earth into eastern and western hemispheres
Latitude, dividing Earth into northern and southern hemispheres

quiz.q: Why do sailors and pilots rely on latitude/longitude coordinates rather than just place names?
options: Coordinates are exact and universal
Place names are always more precise
Coordinates are only decorative
There is no practical reason

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Paragraph Structure
subjects.summary: A strong paragraph has a topic sentence, supporting details, and a concluding sentence.

quiz.q: Which sentence would work BEST as a topic sentence for a paragraph about the benefits of exercise?
options: My friend plays soccer on weekends.
Exercise benefits both body and mind.
Running shoes come in many colours.
I went for a run yesterday.

quiz.q: A paragraph's supporting details should...
options: Have no connection to the main idea
Repeat the topic sentence word for word
Introduce a completely new topic
Directly relate to and support the topic sentence

quiz.q: Which sentence would be an EFFECTIVE concluding sentence for a paragraph about recycling's benefits?
options: Recycling helps the environment.
Recycling bins are usually blue.
I recycle bottles every Tuesday.
Some people don't recycle.

quiz.q: A paragraph has a topic sentence and a concluding sentence but only ONE supporting detail. What's likely missing?
options: The topic sentence itself
More evidence to support the idea
Nothing; one detail is always enough
Correct spelling

quiz.q: Why might a writer place the topic sentence at the END of a paragraph instead of the beginning?
options: Topic sentences can never appear at the end
To build up to the idea for effect
It is always a mistake
It makes the paragraph shorter

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Introduction to Volume
subjects.summary: Volume measures how much space a 3D object takes up, measured in cubic units.

quiz.q: A fish tank measures 30 cm × 20 cm × 25 cm. What is its volume?
options: 7500 cm³
1500 cm³
5000 cm³

quiz.q: Box A is 2×3×4 and Box B is 6×2×2. Are these volumes equal?
options: Yes, both equal 24 cubic units
Cannot be determined
No, Box B is larger
No, Box A is larger

quiz.q: If a cube has a volume of 64 cm³, what is the length of one side?
options: 8 cm
4 cm
16 cm
6 cm

quiz.q: A rectangular prism has a base area of 12 cm² and a height of 5 cm. What is its volume?
options: 72 cm³
17 cm³
50 cm³
60 cm³

quiz.q: Which change would DOUBLE the volume of a rectangular prism?
options: Doubling only the height
Doubling only the surface colour
Doubling all three dimensions
Halving the height

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Human Body and Energy
subjects.summary: Recap of body systems and how they work together, plus energy transformations.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Human Body and Energy

quiz.q: Which correctly pairs a body system with a related everyday activity?
options: Respiratory system — breathing deeply before a race
Digestive system — filtering air
Circulatory system — chewing food
Skeletal system — pumping blood

quiz.q: A toaster converts electrical energy mostly into which form, and is this ever 100% efficient?
options: Sound energy; yes, always 100% efficient
Mechanical energy; no energy is ever lost
Light energy; yes, always 100% efficient
Heat energy; no, some energy is always lost as unusable forms

quiz.q: Which resource would run out fastest if used at a constant rate without replenishment: coal or wind?
options: Neither can ever run out
Coal, since it's non-renewable and finite
Wind, since it's non-renewable
Both would run out equally fast

quiz.q: If your circulatory system stopped working properly, which critical job would be disrupted?
options: Delivering oxygen and nutrients throughout the body
Filtering air before it reaches the lungs
Breaking down food chemically
Supporting the body's frame

quiz.q: Why are body systems studied together rather than in complete isolation?
options: There is no reason to study them together
They constantly interact and depend on each other
Only one system is actually necessary
They never interact with one another

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada's Government Branches
subjects.summary: Canada's government has three branches: legislative (makes laws), executive (carries out laws), and judicial (interprets laws).

quiz.q: A new law is proposed, debated, and voted on in Parliament. Which branch is primarily responsible?
options: None of these
Judicial
Legislative
Executive

quiz.q: A court rules that a law violates the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. Which branch made this decision?
options: Municipal
Judicial
Legislative
Executive

quiz.q: Which scenario best shows the EXECUTIVE branch at work?
options: A court hearing a case
Citizens voting in an election
Departments implementing a law
Parliament debating a new bill

quiz.q: Why is it important that the judicial branch operates independently from the other branches?
options: To eliminate the need for courts
So judges can make laws instead of Parliament
Independence has no real purpose
So courts can apply laws fairly

quiz.q: Parliament passes a law, and courts later strike it down as unconstitutional. This demonstrates...
options: That courts and Parliament are the same branch
The system of checks and balances between branches of government
A malfunction in the government system
That Parliament has no real power

label: Day 10 — Fri (Review)
reviewNote: Final review day: a mix of everything from the two weeks, getting ready for September.

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Final Review: Language Skills
subjects.summary: Mixed review of parts of speech, fact/opinion, figurative language, and paragraph structure.

quiz.q: 'The stars winked at us from the velvet sky' uses which figurative technique?
options: A fact about astronomy
Personification of the stars
A simile using 'like' or 'as'
Onomatopoeia

quiz.q: Which sentence blends a fact with an opinion?
options: Maple trees grow in Canada.
Maple syrup is a liquid.
The tastiest topping comes from trees.
Maple syrup comes from maple trees.

quiz.q: Which sentence works best as a topic sentence introducing a paragraph about libraries?
options: Libraries offer more than just books.
I visited the library on Tuesday.
The library closes at 6pm.
Books have pages.

quiz.q: Which word functions as an adverb in 'She quietly closed the heavy door'?
options: Closed
Quietly
Door
Heavy

quiz.q: A simile comparing courage to a lion would most likely use which structure?
options: 'His courage, lion, roared.'
'His courage was a lion.'
'His courage was as fierce as a lion.'
'His courage lion-ed through the room.'

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Final Review: Math Skills
subjects.summary: Mixed review of fractions, decimals, area/perimeter, and volume.

quiz.q: A rectangle has an area of 30 cm² and a width of 5 cm. What is its length?
options: 5 cm
6 cm
35 cm
25 cm

quiz.q: What is 0.25 written as a fraction in simplest form?
A box measuring 3×3×3 has what volume?
options: 9 cm³
18 cm³
27 cm³
33 cm³

quiz.q: 6 × 7 = ?, then subtract 9. What is the final result?
Which fraction is closest in value to 1 (a whole)?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Final Review: Science Skills
subjects.summary: Mixed review of structures, matter, energy, and the human body.

quiz.q: Which scenario involves compression?
options: Stretching a rubber band
Sitting down on a cushion
A kite string pulled taut
A rope holding up a swing

quiz.q: Melting ice into water is what type of change, and is it reversible?
options: Physical change; no, it can never be reversed
Physical change; yes, it can be reversed by freezing again
Chemical change; no, it cannot be reversed
Chemical change; yes, always reversible

quiz.q: Which system would be MOST directly affected if someone couldn't properly absorb nutrients from food?
options: Digestive system
Skeletal system
None would be affected
Circulatory system

quiz.q: Which is a renewable resource, and why?
options: Oil, because it's naturally and continuously replenished
Natural gas, because it regenerates instantly
Coal, because it never runs out
Wind, because it's naturally and continuously replenished

quiz.q: Two objects have equal volume but different mass. What can you conclude about density?
options: Mass and density are unrelated
The one with greater mass has greater density
They must have equal density
The one with greater mass has lesser density

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Final Review: Social Studies Skills
subjects.summary: Mixed review of early Canada, Indigenous peoples, government, and physical regions.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Final Review: Social Studies Skills

quiz.q: Which three groups make up Indigenous peoples in Canada, and how do they differ?
options: Settlers, Explorers, and Pioneers — recent immigrant groups
Only Inuit — the only Indigenous group in Canada
First Nations, Métis, and Inuit — each with distinct histories, languages, and cultures
Only First Nations — a single unified group

quiz.q: Which branch of government would be responsible if a new law were being debated and voted on?
options: Legislative
Judicial
None
Executive

quiz.q: A region with 'ancient rock, thousands of lakes, and boreal forest' matches which physical region?
options: The Prairies
The Canadian Shield
The Arctic
The Cordillera

quiz.q: Who leads Canada's federal government, and how does this differ from a provincial Premier?
options: The Prime Minister leads provincially; Premiers lead federally
The Prime Minister leads federally; Premiers lead provincially
Neither is elected
They have identical roles

quiz.q: New France was established by which European country, and for what MAIN purpose?
options: England, to establish a rival colony
Portugal, for religious reasons only
Spain, to search for gold only
France, to expand trade, territory, and influence

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Idioms and Expressions
subjects.summary: An idiom is a phrase whose meaning is different from the literal meaning of the words, like 'break the ice.'

quiz.q: A character says 'I'm feeling under the weather today.' What does this idiom most likely mean?
options: They are not feeling well
They are outside in bad weather
They are talking about a storm
They are very happy

quiz.q: Which sentence uses an idiom correctly to mean someone is very nervous?
options: She had butterflies in her stomach.
She ate butterflies for lunch.
She had a stomach ache from lunch.
She felt calm before the show.

quiz.q: Why can idioms be confusing for someone learning English as a new language?
options: They only appear in formal writing
They have no meaning at all
Meaning can't be guessed word-by-word
They are always spelled incorrectly

quiz.q: 'Once in a blue moon' is an idiom meaning...
options: During a full moon only
Very rarely
Every single day
Never, under any circumstances

quiz.q: A writer uses 'spill the beans' instead of 'reveal a secret.' What effect does this likely have?
options: Is factually about beans
Removes all meaning from the sentence
Makes the writing more casual and vivid
Makes the meaning less clear to all readers

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Prime and Composite Numbers
subjects.summary: A prime number has exactly two factors (1 and itself); a composite number has more than two factors.

quiz.q: A number's only factors are 1, 2, 4, and itself (8). What kind of number is it?
options: Both prime and composite
Neither prime nor composite
Composite
Prime

quiz.q: Which number between 20 and 30 is prime?
If a number greater than 2 is even, can it be prime?
options: No number can ever be determined this way
Yes, but only if it's a multiple of 4
No — it always has at least 3 factors
Yes, all even numbers are prime

quiz.q: Which statement about the number 1 is TRUE?
options: 1 is prime because it only has one factor
1 is composite because it has many factors
1 has only one factor, so it's neither
1 is both prime and composite

quiz.q: A student says '51 is prime because it's odd.' Is this correct?
options: No number ending in 1 can be prime
Yes, but only sometimes
Yes, all odd numbers are prime
No, 51 = 3 × 17, so it has more than two factors

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Simple and Complex Machines
subjects.summary: Simple machines (like levers, pulleys, and ramps) make work easier; complex machines combine two or more simple machines.

quiz.q: Which combination of simple machines works together in a pair of scissors?
options: An inclined plane only
Two levers combined with a wedge
A wheel and axle only
A single pulley only

quiz.q: Why is a wheelbarrow considered an example of a lever?
options: It only rolls and does not lift anything
It uses a pivot point to ease lifting
It has no pivot point at all
It is powered by electricity

quiz.q: A ramp used to load furniture into a truck reduces the force needed by...
options: Eliminating gravity
Increasing the distance force acts over
Making the object lighter
Increasing friction only

quiz.q: Why might a bicycle be classified as a complex machine rather than a simple one?
options: It has no moving parts
It combines several simple machines
It is powered by electricity, not force
It is actually a simple machine

quiz.q: Which simple machine would be MOST useful for splitting a log?
options: A wedge
A wheel and axle
A lever only
A pulley

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Fur Trade and the Economy of New France
subjects.summary: The fur trade was central to the economy of New France, connecting French settlers and First Nations through trade networks and alliances.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: The Fur Trade and the Economy of New France

quiz.q: Why were beaver pelts especially valuable to European traders?
options: They had religious significance only
They were used to make fashionable felt hats in Europe
They could not be found anywhere else in the world
They were used as currency directly

quiz.q: How did the fur trade shape relationships between French settlers and First Nations peoples?
options: It had no effect on their relationship
It created economic partnerships and alliances, alongside occasional conflict
It caused complete isolation between the two groups
It only caused permanent conflict, with no cooperation

quiz.q: Which skill would have been MOST valuable to a fur trader working with First Nations partners?
options: Knowledge of ocean navigation only
No particular skill was needed
Skill in farming only
Trade routes, negotiation, geography

quiz.q: Why might historians describe the fur trade as having both positive and negative long-term effects?
options: It had no long-term effects at all
It was entirely negative with no benefits
It was entirely positive with no downsides
It built ties but disrupted traditions

quiz.q: How did the fur trade influence the geography of settlement in New France?
options: Settlement patterns had no connection to trade
All settlements were built far from First Nations communities
Settlements often formed along trade routes and waterways
Settlements avoided all rivers and trade routes

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Multiple-Meaning Words
subjects.summary: Some words have more than one meaning, and you must use context to figure out which meaning is intended.

quiz.q: In 'The pitcher threw a curveball' and 'I filled the pitcher with water,' how does 'pitcher' change meaning?
options: It doesn't change; both mean the same thing
Both refer to a baseball player
Both refer to a container
One refers to a baseball player, the other to a container

quiz.q: Which sentence uses 'fair' to mean an outdoor event, not 'just/equitable'?
options: We rode the Ferris wheel at the county fair.
It's not fair that she won twice.
The judge made a fair decision.
Please be fair to your teammates.

quiz.q: 'Bark' can mean a tree's covering OR a dog's sound. Which sentence uses the TREE meaning?
options: She could hear a bark in the distance.
The dog began to bark loudly.
The puppy's bark startled everyone.
The bark of the oak tree was rough and grey.

quiz.q: Why is context especially important for words like 'bat,' 'bank,' and 'spring'?
options: Context has no effect on meaning
These words always mean the same thing
Without context, the intended meaning would be ambiguous or unclear
These words are never used in real writing

quiz.q: In 'The stadium had a huge crowd' vs. 'Please don't crowd the doorway,' how does 'crowd' function differently?
options: First a noun, then a verb
Both are used as verbs
Both are used as nouns
Both mean exactly the same thing

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Factors and Multiples
subjects.summary: A factor divides evenly into a number; a multiple is the product of a number and any whole number.

quiz.q: What are ALL the factors of 18?
Which number is a common multiple of BOTH 4 and 6?
A student says 'Every multiple of 6 is also a multiple of 3.' Is this true?
options: Yes — 6 is itself a multiple of 3
No, this is never true
Only sometimes, depending on the number
No, multiples of 6 and 3 never overlap

quiz.q: What is the greatest common factor (GCF) of 12 and 18?
Which number has the MOST factors: 16, 17, or 18?
options: 17 (1,17 — 2 factors, since it's prime)
They all have the same number

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Water Cycle
subjects.summary: The water cycle describes how water moves through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and collection.

quiz.q: A lake's water evaporates, rises, condenses into clouds, and falls as rain over the same region. This shows the water cycle is...
options: A chemical transformation of water
One-way and non-repeating
A complete disappearance of water
Continuous and cyclical

quiz.q: Why does water vapour condense into visible clouds at higher altitudes?
options: Water vapour disappears at high altitude
Clouds are unrelated to temperature
Higher altitudes have more heat, causing condensation
Cooler air at higher altitudes turns water vapour back into liquid droplets

quiz.q: Which correctly orders the water cycle starting from a puddle drying up?
options: Precipitation -> Evaporation -> Collection -> Condensation
Condensation -> Collection -> Evaporation -> Precipitation
Collection -> Precipitation -> Evaporation -> Condensation
Evaporation -> Condensation -> Precipitation -> Collection

quiz.q: How can the same water molecules end up in a completely different location weeks later?
options: The water cycle only affects one specific lake
Water molecules never move locations
Only saltwater moves through the cycle
It moves via evaporation and rainfall

quiz.q: What role does the sun play in driving the water cycle?
options: It causes water to freeze permanently
It provides the energy needed for evaporation to occur
It has no role in the water cycle
It only affects precipitation, not evaporation

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Underground Railroad and Canada
subjects.summary: The Underground Railroad was a secret network that helped enslaved people from the United States reach freedom in Canada in the 1800s.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: The Underground Railroad and Canada

quiz.q: Why did the Underground Railroad end in Canada rather than a northern U.S. state for many freedom seekers?
options: There was no reason; Canada was chosen randomly
Canada was closer to the starting point for everyone
Slavery was already banned in Canada
The U.S. had no states at the time

quiz.q: What was the role of a 'conductor' on the Underground Railroad?
options: A government official who tracked routes
A person who drove an actual train
Someone who guided freedom seekers safely along the route
A type of safe house only

quiz.q: Why might learning about the Underground Railroad matter for understanding Canadian identity?
options: It only relates to American history
It shows Canada as a haven for freedom
It has no connection to Canadian history
It is a fictional legend with no historical basis

quiz.q: The term 'Underground Railroad' is symbolic rather than literal because...
options: It only operated railways, not routes
It wasn't an actual railway or literally underground
It has no symbolic meaning at all
It was an actual underground train system

quiz.q: How did communities along the Underground Railroad in Canada support arriving freedom seekers?
options: By ignoring their arrival completely
By turning them away entirely
By offering settlement and support
By requiring payment for safety

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Author's Purpose
subjects.summary: Authors write to persuade, inform, or entertain — and sometimes a combination of these purposes.

quiz.q: A brochure filled with glowing descriptions encouraging tourists to visit Niagara Falls is written mainly to...
options: Entertain with fiction
Inform with pure facts only
Confuse readers
Persuade

quiz.q: Which text would most likely combine BOTH informing and entertaining as its purpose?
options: A tax form
A textbook chapter with no illustrations
A dictionary entry
An engaging story-driven documentary

quiz.q: An author writes an editorial arguing school days should start later. What is the primary purpose?
options: To inform with no opinion
To describe a setting
To persuade
To entertain

quiz.q: Why might a cereal box include both persuasive slogans AND informative nutrition facts?
options: Only persuasion matters on packaging
Nutrition facts are never included on purpose
To mix persuasion with required info
This combination never happens

quiz.q: A satirical comic strip that entertains while subtly criticizing a political decision is an example of...
options: Multiple purposes working together
No clear purpose at all
A purely informative purpose
A purely factual report

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Order of Operations (BEDMAS)
subjects.summary: BEDMAS tells you the order to solve expressions: Brackets, Exponents, Division/Multiplication, Addition/Subtraction.

quiz.q: What does the 'B' in BEDMAS stand for?
options: Both
Brackets
Base
Big numbers

quiz.q: In BEDMAS, which comes first if there are no brackets or exponents?
options: Subtraction
Multiplication/Division
Addition
Nothing, it's random

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Weather Patterns and Instruments
subjects.summary: Weather instruments like thermometers, barometers, and anemometers help us measure and predict weather patterns.

quiz.q: A thermometer measures...
options: Temperature
Air pressure
Rainfall
Wind speed

quiz.q: An anemometer measures...
options: Light
Temperature
Wind speed
Humidity

quiz.q: A barometer measures...
options: Temperature only
Wind direction
Sound
Air pressure

quiz.q: A rain gauge is used to measure...
options: The amount of rainfall
Wind speed
Air pressure
Temperature

quiz.q: Why do meteorologists track weather patterns?
options: To predict future weather
For no reason
To change the weather
To slow down storms

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Immigration Patterns to Canada
subjects.summary: People have immigrated to Canada from around the world for different reasons, shaping Canada's population and culture over time.

quiz.q: What is immigration?
options: Staying in one place forever
Studying geography
Visiting a country briefly
Moving to a new country to live

quiz.q: What is one common reason people immigrate to Canada?
options: Random chance only
There is never a reason
To avoid all change
To find new opportunities or safety

quiz.q: Canada's population has been shaped by...
options: Only internal movement
Immigration from many countries
No immigration at all
Immigration from only one country

quiz.q: A 'wave' of immigration refers to...
options: An ocean event
A natural disaster
A group arriving during one time period
A type of visa

quiz.q: Immigrants often contribute to Canada through...
options: Skills, culture, and involvement
Only negative effects
Isolation from others
Nothing at all

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Summarizing a Text
subjects.summary: A summary briefly retells the most important ideas of a text in your own words, without unnecessary details.

quiz.q: Which summary of a story about a boy who overcomes his fear of swimming BEST follows summarizing rules?
options: Sam went to the pool on Tuesday wearing blue goggles and ate a granola bar afterward.
Sam feared water but learned to swim.
Sam is scared.
The story has many characters and events that happen throughout.

quiz.q: A student's summary of an article is nearly as long as the article and includes minor examples. What's the problem?
options: It uses too many of the author's exact words
It's too short
It fails to condense to main ideas
It successfully captures every detail

quiz.q: Which is the best test for whether a detail belongs in a summary?
options: Does this use difficult vocabulary?
Is this the first sentence of the paragraph?
Is this necessary to understand the main idea?
Did I find this detail interesting?

quiz.q: A summary written entirely in sentences copied from the original text is a problem because...
options: It shows understanding by using the author's exact words
Summaries must always match the original wording
There's no issue at all
It's copying, not real understanding

quiz.q: When summarizing a chapter with several subplots, which approach works best?
options: Randomly pick a few sentences from anywhere
Only describe the setting, not the events
Focus on the main plot's key points
Include every subplot in full detail

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Mixed Numbers and Improper Fractions
subjects.summary: A mixed number combines a whole number and a fraction; an improper fraction has a numerator larger than its denominator.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Mixed Numbers and Improper Fractions

quiz.q: A recipe calls for 7/2 cups of flour. Expressed as a mixed number, how much flour is that?
Which improper fraction is equivalent to 4 2/5?
Comparing 2 1/4 (9/4) and 2 3/4 (11/4) as improper fractions, which is larger and why?
options: 2 1/4 is larger because 9 is an odd number
Cannot be determined
2 3/4 is larger because 11/4 > 9/4
They are equal

quiz.q: A board is cut into ten pieces, each 1/3 metre long. As an improper fraction, what length of board was used?
options: 30/3 metres
10/3 metres
3/10 metres
13/3 metres

quiz.q: Which improper fraction is NOT equivalent to 3 1/2?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Classification of Living Things
subjects.summary: Scientists classify living things into groups based on shared characteristics, such as plants, animals, and other kingdoms.

quiz.q: A newly discovered animal has a backbone, fur, and nurses its young. Which group does it belong to?
options: Reptile
Insect
Mammal
Invertebrate

quiz.q: Why is classifying organisms into groups like vertebrates and invertebrates useful to scientists?
options: It prevents new species from being discovered
It has no scientific value
It helps them see patterns in life
It only matters for pets

quiz.q: An earthworm and a jellyfish are both classified as invertebrates. What do they have in common?
options: Both are mammals
Both have a backbone
Neither has a backbone
Both are plants

quiz.q: A student claims all animals with wings must be birds. Which organism proves this classification wrong?
options: A robin
An eagle
A chicken
A bat, a mammal with wings

quiz.q: Which characteristic would classify an organism as a fish rather than an amphibian?
options: Lays eggs on land only
Has fur and nurses its young
Lives only in water its whole life and breathes through gills
Can live on land and in water, breathing with lungs as an adult

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Multiculturalism in Canada
subjects.summary: Canada's Multiculturalism Policy recognizes and celebrates the diversity of cultures, languages, and traditions across the country.

quiz.q: A city hosts an annual festival celebrating food, music, and traditions from dozens of cultures in its population. This is an example of...
options: Multiculturalism in action
An isolated, meaningless event
A violation of Canadian law
Assimilation into one culture

quiz.q: How does Canada's Multiculturalism Policy differ from a policy requiring immigrants to adopt only one national culture?
options: It only applies to Indigenous peoples
It requires the same thing — full assimilation
It preserves diversity, not sameness
It bans all cultural practices

quiz.q: Why might multiculturalism be considered both a cultural AND economic strength for Canada?
options: Diverse skills can boost trade too
It only affects government spending
Diversity only causes economic problems
It has no economic connection at all

quiz.q: A new student from another country brings different foods and customs to class. What reflects a multicultural approach?
options: Ignoring their background
Being curious and respectful
Asking them to abandon their traditions
Excluding them until they change

quiz.q: Which statement BEST reflects how multiculturalism shapes Canadian identity?
options: Multiculturalism has no connection to identity
It includes many cultural backgrounds
Canadian identity is defined by a single culture
Canadian identity rejects all cultural influences

label: Day 15 — Fri (Review)
reviewNote: Review day: mixed questions from Days 11–14.

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Review: Idioms, Word Meanings, and Purpose
subjects.summary: Mixed review of idioms, multiple-meaning words, author's purpose, and summarizing.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Idioms, Word Meanings, and Purpose

quiz.q: A story describes a character 'burning the midnight oil' before a big test. What does this reveal about the character?
options: They are literally starting a fire, showing carelessness
They feel sleepy, showing laziness
They are cooking dinner, showing hunger
They are working late into the night, showing dedication

quiz.q: In 'The plane will bank sharply to the left,' the word 'bank' means...
options: A pile of snow
The land beside a river
To tilt or turn at an angle
A financial institution

quiz.q: A user manual explaining how to assemble furniture is written mainly to...
options: Confuse
Persuade
Entertain
Inform

quiz.q: Which summary correctly captures only the essential parts of a story about a lost dog finding its way home?
options: Every scene of the dog's journey is described in full detail.
A dog exists.
The dog was brown with white spots and it rained on Tuesday.
A dog got lost and found its way home.

quiz.q: 'She let the cat out of the bag' means she...
options: Cleaned her room
Went shopping
Freed an animal
Accidentally revealed a secret

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Review: Numbers, Operations, and Fractions
subjects.summary: Mixed review of prime/composite numbers, factors/multiples, BEDMAS, and mixed numbers.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Numbers, Operations, and Fractions

quiz.q: Which number is both prime AND a multiple of 3?
A classroom of 30 students is divided into equal groups. Which group size would NOT divide evenly?
Solve using BEDMAS: 4 + 2 × (5 - 2) = ?
Convert 11/4 to a mixed number.
options: 3 1/4, since 4 goes into 11 three times
2 3/4, since 4 goes into 11 twice with remainder 3
2 1/4, since 4 goes into 11 twice with remainder 1
1 3/4, since 4 goes into 11 once

quiz.q: Which pair of numbers are BOTH composite?
options: 7 and 11
5 and 17
9 and 15
2 and 13

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Machines, Water Cycle, and Classification
subjects.summary: Mixed review of simple machines, the water cycle, and classifying living things.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Machines, Water Cycle, and Classification

quiz.q: A see-saw at a playground is an example of which simple machine, and what does it do?
options: An inclined plane; spreads a force over distance
A pulley; changes the direction of a force
A wheel and axle; reduces friction
A lever; helps lift a load with less effort

quiz.q: During which stage of the water cycle does liquid water turn into vapour and rise into the air?
options: Collection
Condensation
Precipitation
Evaporation

quiz.q: A newly found animal has no backbone and lives in the ocean. Which classification best fits, and why?
options: Mammal, because all sea creatures are mammals
Invertebrate, because it lacks a backbone
Vertebrate, because it lives in water
Reptile, because it has scales

quiz.q: What provides the energy that drives the entire water cycle?
options: Wind currents alone
Energy from the sun
Electricity from storms
Ocean currents

quiz.q: A pulley system lets one person lift a heavy flag up a pole. What is the main benefit of the pulley here?
options: It increases the weight of the flag
It changes the direction of the force
It only works underwater
It eliminates the need for any force

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Review: Fur Trade, Underground Railroad, and Immigration
subjects.summary: Mixed review of the fur trade economy, the Underground Railroad, immigration, and multiculturalism.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Fur Trade, Underground Railroad, and Immigration

quiz.q: Why were beaver pelts the most valuable resource in the fur trade, rather than something like corn?
options: Beaver fur was fashionable in Europe
There was no demand for any furs
Beaver pelts were used directly as currency
Corn could not be grown in Canada

quiz.q: How did the Underground Railroad help people seeking freedom, despite not being an actual railway?
options: It was only a symbolic name with no real function
It transported goods, not people
It provided a secret network of routes and safe houses to reach freedom
It was a literal underground train system

quiz.q: Which is the BEST example of a factor that would encourage someone to immigrate to Canada?
options: Random chance
Bad weather elsewhere
Job opportunities and safety
Conflict at home

quiz.q: How does Canada's multicultural policy differ from requiring full assimilation to one culture?
options: It only applies to select groups
It preserves diversity, not sameness
It bans cultural practices
It requires the same thing as assimilation

quiz.q: Why were First Nations peoples essential partners with the French in the fur trade, rather than just suppliers?
options: They opposed all trade with Europeans
They only observed from a distance
They had no role in the fur trade
They provided knowledge and alliances

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Making Inferences
subjects.summary: An inference is a conclusion you draw by combining clues from the text with what you already know.

quiz.q: A character enters a room, immediately opens all the windows, and fans their face with a magazine. What can you infer?
options: The room is cold
The character is bored
The character is sad
The room is hot or stuffy

quiz.q: Which clue combination would BEST support the inference that a character is nervous before a performance?
options: Smiling calmly and humming a tune
Pacing, sweaty palms, and repeatedly checking the time
Sitting still and reading a book
Eating a leisurely breakfast

quiz.q: A text never directly states a character is poor, but describes their torn shoes, empty fridge, and unpaid bills. This is an example of...
options: A directly stated fact
An opinion with no evidence
An inference from text clues
A summary

quiz.q: Why is combining textual clues with prior knowledge important when inferring?
options: It guarantees your inference is always correct
It only works for nonfiction
It helps you draw unstated conclusions
It replaces the need to read the text

quiz.q: A student infers a character is lying because they avoid eye contact and give inconsistent details. Which clue would STRENGTHEN this inference?
options: The character smiles throughout
The character speaks in a calm, steady voice
The character has good grades
The character's story keeps changing

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Multiplying Fractions by Whole Numbers
subjects.summary: To multiply a fraction by a whole number, multiply the numerator by the whole number and keep the denominator the same.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Multiplying Fractions by Whole Numbers

quiz.q: A recipe needs 2/3 cup of sugar per batch. How much sugar is needed for 5 batches?
options: 10/15 cups
7/3 cups
10/3 cups
2/15 cups

quiz.q: Which expression is equivalent to 4 × 3/5?
A runner covers 3/4 km each lap. After 6 laps, how far have they run?
options: 4/6 km
18/4 km, which simplifies to 4 1/2 km

quiz.q: Which multiplication does NOT result in 8/3?
A jug holds 5 × 3/8 litres of juice. How many litres is that?
options: 15/40 litres
15/8 litres
8/15 litres
3/40 litres

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Light and Reflection
subjects.summary: Light travels in straight lines and reflects off surfaces, which is how we see objects and images in mirrors.

quiz.q: You see a clear reflection in a still lake but a blurry one in a rippling lake. What does this reveal about reflection?
options: Reflection needs a smooth surface
Reflection only happens on land
Water never reflects light
Ripples improve reflection quality

quiz.q: A straw appears bent when placed in a glass of water. What causes this optical effect?
options: Light stops moving in water
The straw physically bends
Light refracts as it passes from air into water
The glass distorts all objects

quiz.q: Why can you see a non-glowing object, like a book, in a lit room?
options: Light reflects off it to your eyes
Light passes through the book
Books are naturally visible without light
The book produces its own light

quiz.q: A magician uses angled mirrors to make an object appear to disappear. This trick relies on...
options: Gravity manipulation
Sound waves
Magnetism
Light reflecting off angled mirrors

quiz.q: Which scenario demonstrates refraction rather than reflection?
options: Seeing your face in a bathroom mirror
A fish appearing to be where it isn't
Light reflecting off a lake's surface
Sunlight bouncing off a shiny car

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada's Territories
subjects.summary: Canada has three territories — Yukon, Northwest Territories, and Nunavut — located in the north, each with unique geography and peoples.

quiz.q: Why do Canada's territories receive certain powers directly from the federal government rather than functioning exactly like provinces?
options: Territories relate differently to Ottawa
There is no difference between them
Territories have more power than provinces
This is not actually true

quiz.q: Nunavut has a large Inuit population and was created as its own territory in 1999. Why might this matter for the Inuit?
options: It merges Inuit lands with a province
It eliminates all Inuit traditions
It gives Inuit more self-governance
It has no significance for Inuit people

quiz.q: Which factor would make large-scale crop farming especially difficult in Canada's northern territories?
options: Cold climate and short growing season
The land is too flat
There is too much rainfall
There are too many farmers already

quiz.q: A mining company wants to operate in the Northwest Territories. Which geographic feature would most affect its planning?
options: Coral reefs
Tropical rainforest terrain
Dense urban population
Remote, permafrost, extreme cold

quiz.q: How does the population distribution of Canada's territories compare to its provinces?
options: Territories have larger populations than most provinces
Provinces have no population at all
Population is identical everywhere in Canada
Territories have much smaller populations spread across vast, remote areas

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Text Features in Nonfiction
subjects.summary: Nonfiction texts use features like headings, captions, bold text, and diagrams to help organize information and aid understanding.

quiz.q: A chapter includes a bolded term, a labeled diagram, and a caption. Which reading strategy uses these features BEST?
options: Ignore them and only read the paragraphs
Read them after finishing without connecting to the text
Preview them first to build a framework for understanding the text
Skip the chapter entirely

quiz.q: Why might an author use a heading like 'Causes of the Water Cycle' instead of just starting the paragraph directly?
options: It is required by law
It replaces the need for a topic sentence
It has no real purpose
It signals the topic to guide readers

quiz.q: A diagram of the digestive system includes labeled arrows showing the path of food. What is the PRIMARY benefit of this feature?
options: It helps readers visualize the process
It replaces all the text
It decorates the page
It has no connection to the content

quiz.q: Which nonfiction text feature would be MOST useful for quickly finding which page discusses 'Volcanoes'?
options: A photograph
A bolded word
A caption
An index or table of contents

quiz.q: A student skips all text features and only reads paragraphs. What is a likely consequence?
options: They will understand the text just as well
They will read faster with no downside
They may miss key info and visuals
Nothing negative happens

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Ratios
subjects.summary: A ratio compares two quantities, showing how much of one thing there is compared to another.

quiz.q: A fruit basket has a ratio of apples to oranges of 3:2. If there are 15 apples, how many oranges are there?
A paint mix uses a ratio of blue to yellow of 2:5. To make a batch using 20 units of yellow, how much blue is needed?
Which ratio is NOT equivalent to 3:4?
A classroom has a ratio of boys to girls of 5:6, totaling 22 students. How many boys are there?
A recipe ratio of water to rice is 2:1. If you have 3 cups of rice, how much water do you need?
options: 6 cups
4 cups
1.5 cups
3 cups

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Sound and Vibration
subjects.summary: Sound is produced by vibrations that travel through a medium like air, water, or solids, reaching our ears as sound waves.

quiz.q: Why can astronauts not hear sound in the vacuum of space, even during an explosion?
options: Sound only travels through solids
Explosions don't make sound
Sound needs a medium; space has none
Astronauts' ears don't work in space

quiz.q: A guitarist presses a string shorter to raise its pitch. Why does this happen?
options: Shorter strings are always louder
A shorter string vibrates faster, producing a higher pitch
A shorter string vibrates slower
String length has no effect on pitch

quiz.q: Which comparison correctly relates pitch and vibration frequency?
