options: Faster vibrations mean higher pitch
Pitch depends only on volume
Vibration speed has no effect on pitch
Faster vibrations produce lower pitch

quiz.q: Why does sound travel faster through water than through air?
options: Water molecules are packed tighter
Sound cannot travel through water at all
Water blocks all sound
Air is denser than water

quiz.q: A drummer hits a drum harder. What property of the sound changes MOST directly, and why?
options: Pitch increases, because harder hits always raise pitch
Volume rises with bigger vibrations
The sound stops
Nothing changes

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Arctic: Geography and Peoples
subjects.summary: The Arctic region of Canada has a unique cold climate, tundra landscape, and is home to Inuit communities with rich traditions.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: The Arctic: Geography and Peoples

quiz.q: Why has traditional Inuit knowledge been essential for survival in the Arctic environment?
options: Arctic survival requires no special knowledge
It has no practical use
It only applies to farming
It offers skills for extreme cold

quiz.q: How does the Arctic's tundra landscape affect what kinds of building and infrastructure are possible there?
options: Tundra has no effect on construction
There are no environmental challenges to building
The tundra allows unlimited large-scale farming
Permafrost and extreme cold require specialized construction methods

quiz.q: Why might climate change be a particularly significant concern for Arctic communities?
options: The Arctic is unaffected by climate change
Arctic ecosystems cannot be disrupted
Climate change only affects tropical regions
Melting ice disrupts traditional life

quiz.q: Which comparison best explains why the Arctic has a very different climate than southern Canada?
options: The Arctic is actually warmer than the south
The Arctic and southern Canada have identical climates
High latitude means less sunlight
Latitude has no effect on climate

quiz.q: A researcher studying the Arctic would find Inuit oral traditions valuable because they...
options: They hold generations of knowledge
Have no connection to the environment
Are purely fictional with no factual basis
Only describe events outside the Arctic

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing a Persuasive Paragraph
subjects.summary: A persuasive paragraph states an opinion and supports it with convincing reasons and evidence.

quiz.q: Which sentence uses the STRONGEST persuasive evidence to support its opinion?
options: Recess is fun.
Some students like recess.
Studies show recess boosts focus.
Recess happens at noon.

quiz.q: A paragraph claims 'Homework should be banned' but gives no supporting reasons. What is the main weakness?
options: It is too short
It uses too many facts
It has no weakness
It states an opinion without evidence

quiz.q: Which technique would make a persuasive paragraph about recycling MORE convincing?
options: Only stating a personal preference with no reasons
Ignoring the topic entirely
Repeating the same sentence multiple times
Including a statistic about how much waste recycling reduces

quiz.q: Why might a persuasive writer address a counterargument, an opposing view, in their paragraph?
options: It shows they considered other views
It has no effect on persuasiveness
It is required by law
It weakens their own argument

quiz.q: Which of these sentences signals a persuasive opinion rather than a fact?
options: The school day is six hours long.
PE should be required for health.
There are 25 students in the class.
The bell rings at 3:00 pm.

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Coordinate Grids
subjects.summary: A coordinate grid uses an x-axis and y-axis to locate points using ordered pairs (x, y) in the first quadrant.

quiz.q: A point starts at the origin (0,0) and moves 4 units right and 6 units up. What are its new coordinates?
Which two points are the SAME distance from the y-axis?
A rectangle has corners at (1,1), (1,4), (5,4), and (5,1). What is its width, the horizontal side length?
Which ordered pair is located closest to the origin?
A treasure map marks a location at (6, 2), but the map's creator accidentally swapped the coordinates. Where would someone mistakenly search?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Rock Cycle
subjects.summary: Rocks change form over time through the rock cycle, moving between igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic types.

quiz.q: A geologist finds rock with visible layers of compressed sediment containing fossils. Which rock type is this most likely to be?
options: Metamorphic
Igneous
None of these
Sedimentary

quiz.q: Why can a single rock theoretically transform into all three rock types over millions of years?
options: Only igneous rocks can change
Heat and pressure reshape rock
Rocks never change once formed
Rock type is permanent and fixed

quiz.q: Granite (igneous) is exposed to intense heat and pressure deep underground without melting. What does it become?
options: Liquid magma
It stays igneous with no change
Sedimentary rock
A metamorphic rock

quiz.q: Which process would most directly turn magma into igneous rock?
options: Extreme heat and pressure without melting
Compression of sediment layers
Cooling and hardening
Weathering by wind and rain

quiz.q: A riverbed contains smooth, rounded pebbles. What does this suggest about the rock's history?
options: It formed instantly from cooling lava
It was never exposed to any natural forces
It has been worn down and shaped by water erosion over time
It is definitely metamorphic

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Comparing Canada's Provinces
subjects.summary: Canada's provinces each have their own capital cities, geography, and key features that make them unique.

quiz.q: Why might Alberta's economy differ significantly from Prince Edward Island's, based on geography?
options: Geography has no effect on provincial economies
PEI has more oil resources than Alberta
Both provinces have identical economies
Alberta has oil; PEI has farming

quiz.q: Why does Quebec's French-speaking majority make it culturally distinct among Canadian provinces?
options: This has no historical basis
Quebec has no distinct culture
All provinces are entirely French-speaking
It ties to New France heritage

quiz.q: Which statement about provincial capitals is TRUE?
options: British Columbia's capital, Victoria, is larger than Vancouver
Ontario's capital, Toronto, is also its largest city
Every province's capital is automatically its largest city
Canada's capital, Ottawa, is located in Quebec

quiz.q: Why is Prince Edward Island's small size an important geographic fact for understanding its economy and lifestyle?
options: Small provinces cannot have any industries
Small size concentrates its industries
PEI's size has no economic effect
Small size means it has no coastline

quiz.q: How would comparing the Rocky Mountains (Alberta/BC) to PEI's flat coastal terrain help explain tourism differences between the two?
options: Terrain has no connection to tourism
Only mountainous provinces can have tourism
Terrain shapes the type of tourism
Both provinces attract identical types of tourists

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Dialogue and Quotation Marks
subjects.summary: Quotation marks show exactly what a character says, and dialogue helps bring characters and stories to life.

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly punctuates dialogue with a dialogue tag after the quote?
options: "I can't believe it," she whispered.
She whispered "I can't believe it."
I can't believe it, she whispered.
"I can't believe it" she whispered.

quiz.q: In a story with two characters talking back and forth, why does the writer start a new paragraph each time the speaker changes?
options: It saves paper
It is optional and rarely used
It helps readers track who's speaking
It has no real purpose

quiz.q: Which punctuation choice is correct?
options: Neither is correct
"Watch out"! she shouted.
"Watch out!" she shouted.
Both are equally correct

quiz.q: A writer wants to show a character's exact words while also revealing HOW they said it. Which sentence does this best?
options: Maria said, "I'm going to be late."
Maria was late.
Maria said something about being late.
Maria groaned, "I'm going to be late."

quiz.q: Why might dialogue reveal more about a character than a narrator's description alone?
options: Dialogue and narration are identical
Dialogue never reveals character traits
Only narration can show character
Dialogue reveals personality and tone

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Angles
subjects.summary: Angles are measured in degrees and classified as acute (less than 90°), right (exactly 90°), or obtuse (more than 90° but less than 180°).

quiz.q: Two angles together form a straight line (180°). If one angle measures 65°, what is the other?
A triangle has angles measuring 90°, 40°, and an unknown third angle. What is the third angle?
Which angle classification would a 95° angle fall into?
options: Acute
Right
Obtuse
Straight

quiz.q: A carpenter needs to cut a piece of wood at exactly a right angle. What angle measurement should they use?
Two complementary angles add up to 90°. If one angle is 35°, what is the other?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: States of Matter and the Particle Model
subjects.summary: Matter exists as solid, liquid, or gas, and the particle model explains how tightly particles are packed and how they move in each state.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: States of Matter and the Particle Model

quiz.q: Ice is heated until it becomes water, then continues to be heated until it becomes steam. What happens to the particles throughout this process?
options: They disappear entirely
They become heavier
They stop moving completely
They move faster and spread apart

quiz.q: Why can a gas be compressed into a smaller container more easily than a liquid or solid?
options: Liquids have more space between particles than gases
Gases have no particles at all
Gas particles are tightly packed like a solid
Gas particles are far apart with lots of empty space between them

quiz.q: A student places a balloon in a freezer and it shrinks. Using the particle model, what explains this?
options: Cold air adds more particles to the balloon
Particles disappear when cooled
Cooling slows particles, so gas shrinks
The balloon material shrinks on its own

quiz.q: Which statement correctly compares particle movement across the three states of matter?
options: Gas particles don't move at all
Movement increases: solid, liquid, gas
All three states have identical particle movement
Solid particles move the fastest

quiz.q: Why does a liquid take the shape of its container while a solid does not?
options: Liquid particles are frozen in place
Solids are always larger than liquids
Liquid particles flow past each other
Liquids have no particles

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Rights and Freedoms in Canada
subjects.summary: The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms protects basic rights like freedom of expression, equality, and democratic rights for all Canadians.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Rights and Freedoms in Canada

quiz.q: Why does the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms apply to laws made by governments, rather than just individual actions?
options: It cannot be used to challenge any law
It has no connection to government laws
It only applies to private businesses
It makes laws respect basic rights

quiz.q: A law is proposed that would prevent a specific group from voting. Which Charter right would this violate?
options: Freedom of religion only
Property rights
Democratic rights
Mobility rights only

quiz.q: Why might equality rights be considered essential in a diverse country like Canada?
options: They ensure fair treatment for everyone
They only protect one group of people
They have no connection to diversity
They eliminate the need for laws

quiz.q: Which scenario BEST illustrates freedom of expression being exercised?
options: A person being arrested for their opinion
Citizens being told what to think by the government
A citizen peacefully protesting a government policy
A newspaper being shut down for printing an opinion

quiz.q: Why do rights and freedoms often come with responsibilities, such as respecting others' rights?
options: Because only adults have responsibilities
Because rights have no limits
Because responsibilities cancel out rights
Rights shouldn't unfairly limit others'

label: Day 20 — Fri (Review)
reviewNote: Review day: mixed questions from Days 16–19.

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Review: Inferences, Text Features, and Persuasion
subjects.summary: Mixed review of making inferences, nonfiction text features, persuasive writing, and dialogue.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Inferences, Text Features, and Persuasion

quiz.q: A character repeatedly glances at the clock and taps their foot while waiting. What can you infer about them?
options: They are relaxed
They are asleep
They are impatient or anxious
They are bored of reading

quiz.q: Why would an author include a labeled diagram alongside a nonfiction paragraph about the water cycle?
options: It serves no purpose
To replace the paragraph entirely
To decorate the page only
To help readers visualize the process

quiz.q: Which sentence provides the STRONGEST persuasive evidence?
options: Recycling happens weekly.
Some people recycle.
Recycling is good.
Studies show recycling cuts waste 30%.

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly punctuates a line of dialogue with an action beat?
options: I found it, she said, holding up the key.
She said "I found it," holding up the key
"I found it" she said, holding up the key.
"I found it," she said, holding up the key.

quiz.q: A student infers a character is proud of their work because they smile and show it to everyone. Which detail would STRENGTHEN this inference?
options: The character hides the work in a drawer
The character talks about it excitedly to friends
The character ignores the work completely
The character throws the work away

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Review: Fractions, Ratios, Grids, and Angles
subjects.summary: Mixed review of multiplying fractions, ratios, coordinate grids, and angles.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Fractions, Ratios, Grids, and Angles

quiz.q: A recipe needs 3/4 cup of oil per batch. How much oil is needed for 4 batches?
options: 3/16 cups
12/16 cups
4/3 cups
3 cups

quiz.q: A ratio of cats to dogs at a shelter is 3:5. If there are 15 cats, how many dogs are there?
A point moves from the origin 5 units right and 3 units down. If down is negative, what are its coordinates?
Two angles are complementary and add to 90°. If one is 62°, what is the other?
An obtuse angle measures more than 90° but less than ___?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Light, Sound, Rocks, and Matter
subjects.summary: Mixed review of light and reflection, sound and vibration, the rock cycle, and states of matter.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Light, Sound, Rocks, and Matter

quiz.q: A straw looks bent in a glass of water. Which phenomenon explains this?
options: Vibration
Refraction
Reflection
Absorption

quiz.q: Why does sound travel faster through water than through air?
options: Water molecules are packed tightly
Sound cannot travel through liquids
Water blocks all sound
Air is denser than water

quiz.q: A rock shows compressed layers of sediment with visible fossils. Which type of rock is it?
options: Igneous
Sedimentary
Metamorphic
None of these

quiz.q: Why can a gas be compressed into a smaller space more easily than a solid?
options: Gas particles are tightly packed
Gas particles are spread far apart with room to compress
Gases have no particles
Solids have more space between particles

quiz.q: A guitar string is shortened to raise its pitch. Why does this happen?
options: A shorter string vibrates slower
A shorter string vibrates faster, raising the pitch
Shorter strings are always quieter
String length doesn't affect pitch

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Review: Territories, the Arctic, Provinces, and Rights
subjects.summary: Mixed review of Canada's territories, the Arctic, comparing provinces, and rights and freedoms.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Review: Territories, the Arctic, Provinces, and Rights

quiz.q: Why do Canada's territories receive certain powers directly from the federal government, unlike provinces?
options: There is no difference
Territories have more power than provinces
This isn't actually true
Territories relate differently to Ottawa

quiz.q: Why has traditional Inuit knowledge been essential for survival in the Arctic?
options: It has no practical use
It only applies to farming
It offers skills for extreme cold
Arctic survival needs no special knowledge

quiz.q: Why might Alberta's economy differ from Prince Edward Island's, based on geography?
options: PEI has more oil than Alberta
Geography has no effect on economies
Both provinces have identical economies
Alberta has oil; PEI has farming

quiz.q: A law is proposed preventing a specific group from voting. Which Charter right would this violate?
options: Freedom of religion only
Mobility rights only
Democratic rights
Property rights

quiz.q: Why do rights and freedoms often come with responsibilities?
options: Because responsibilities cancel rights
Because one person's rights should not unfairly limit another's
Because rights have no limits
Because only adults have responsibilities

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Descriptive Writing with Sensory Details
subjects.summary: Descriptive writing uses sensory details — sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch — to help readers picture a scene vividly.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Descriptive Writing with Sensory Details

quiz.q: Which revision adds the MOST effective sensory detail to 'The forest was quiet'?
options: The forest was quiet, which was good.
Silent but for leaves and an owl call.
The forest was quiet and nice.
The forest existed and was quiet.

quiz.q: A writer describes a bakery scene using only sight ('the bread was golden'). What would improve the description?
options: Only using longer words
Removing all description entirely
Adding details from smell and warmth
Repeating the same sentence

quiz.q: Why might a writer choose 'the icy wind sliced across my face' instead of 'it was windy and cold'?
options: It removes sensory detail entirely
Shorter sentences are always better
Both sentences are equally effective
It creates a more vivid experience

quiz.q: Which word choice appeals to the sense of smell most directly?
options: Fragrant
Crunchy
Blue
Loud

quiz.q: A student's descriptive paragraph names objects but includes no sensory language ('There was a table, a chair, and a lamp'). What's missing?
options: Punctuation
Correct spelling
Nothing — this is already vivid
Sensory details to picture the scene

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Probability
subjects.summary: Probability describes how likely an event is to happen, using terms like impossible, unlikely, likely, and certain.

quiz.q: A bag has 4 red, 3 blue, and 3 green marbles (10 total). What is the probability of picking a blue marble?
A spinner has 8 equal sections: 5 yellow and 3 purple. Which best describes the probability of landing on purple?
options: Unlikely — under half are purple
Impossible
Certain
Likely, but not certain

quiz.q: A weather forecast says there's a 90% chance of rain. How would you describe this probability?
options: Impossible
Very likely
Unlikely
Certain, no exceptions

quiz.q: A bag contains only red marbles. What is the probability of picking a blue marble?
options: Likely
Unlikely
Certain
Impossible

quiz.q: Which event has a probability closest to 50/50, equally likely?
options: Rolling a 6 on a standard die
Drawing the ace of spades from a full deck
Picking a red marble from a bag of 9 red and 1 blue
Flipping a coin and getting heads

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Simple Electrical Circuits
subjects.summary: A simple electrical circuit needs a power source, a conductor, and a closed path for electricity to flow and power things like a light bulb.

quiz.q: A light bulb in a circuit doesn't light up even though the battery is connected. Which problem would MOST likely explain this?
options: The wire is made of copper
The circuit has too much electricity
The switch is closed
The circuit path is open or broken somewhere

quiz.q: Why is copper commonly used for electrical wiring instead of rubber?
options: Copper is an insulator, better for safety
There's no difference between the two materials
Copper conducts; rubber insulates
Rubber conducts electricity better than copper

quiz.q: A switch is added to a circuit. What happens when the switch is opened?
options: The battery is recharged
The circuit breaks, stopping the flow
The circuit is completed and the bulb lights
The wire changes material

quiz.q: Two identical bulbs are connected in a circuit, but one is much dimmer. What might explain the difference?
options: Both bulbs always receive identical electricity regardless of connections
A weaker connection or partial break is limiting current to that bulb
The dimmer bulb produces more heat
This cannot happen in real circuits

quiz.q: Why does touching a bare, exposed wire pose a safety risk?
options: The human body can act as a conductor for the electrical current
Electricity cannot flow through people
Wires are always insulated so this cannot occur
The human body always acts as an insulator

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Importance of Voting and Elections
subjects.summary: Voting in elections lets citizens choose their representatives and have a say in decisions that affect their communities and country.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: The Importance of Voting and Elections

quiz.q: Why might low voter turnout in an election be a concern for a democracy?
options: Elections require no voters to be valid
Low turnout has no effect on outcomes
Fewer voters always produce fairer results
Decisions may not reflect most views

quiz.q: Which levels of government does a Canadian citizen have the opportunity to vote for representatives in?
options: Only local school boards
Only the Prime Minister directly
Only federal elections exist
Federal, provincial, and municipal

quiz.q: Why is voting considered both a right and a responsibility in a democracy?
options: Only elected officials have this obligation
Voting has no connection to responsibility
A right to a say, a duty to participate
It is neither a right nor a responsibility

quiz.q: A citizen decides not to vote because they feel their single vote 'doesn't matter.' Why might this reasoning be flawed?
options: Voting is completely random regardless of participation
Individual votes are actually deleted before counting
This reasoning is entirely correct
Elections can hinge on small margins

quiz.q: Why do democratic countries typically hold elections at regular intervals rather than allowing leaders to stay in power indefinitely?
options: Regular elections are only symbolic
Indefinite power keeps decisions more stable
Elections have no connection to accountability
It keeps leaders accountable to voters

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Point of View: First vs. Third Person
subjects.summary: First-person narration uses 'I' and 'we'; third-person narration uses 'he,' 'she,' or 'they' to tell the story.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Point of View: First vs. Third Person

quiz.q: A story reads: 'James walked into the room, unaware that everyone was watching him.' Which point of view is this, and how do you know?
options: First person, because it reveals his inner thoughts only
First person, because it uses 'I'
Third person, because it uses a character's name and describes him from outside
Second person, because it addresses the reader directly

quiz.q: Why might an author choose third-person point of view to tell a story with multiple important characters?
options: It removes all characters from the story
It only allows describing one character at a time forever
Third person cannot describe more than one character
It allows the narrator to show the thoughts, feelings, or actions of multiple characters

quiz.q: Which sentence would be rewritten from first person to third person correctly?
options: 'I ran to the bus stop.' → 'I ran to the bus stop.'
'I ran to the bus stop.' → 'You ran to the bus stop.'
'I ran to the bus stop.' → 'She ran to the bus stop.'
'I ran to the bus stop.' → 'We ran to the bus stop.'

quiz.q: A reader only knows what one character thinks and feels throughout a story told using 'I'. What is a LIMITATION of this point of view?
options: This point of view reveals no information
There is no limitation at all
The reader cannot know the private thoughts of other characters
The reader knows everything about every character

quiz.q: Which point of view would be BEST suited for a detective story that keeps the reader guessing along with the detective?
options: First person, limited to the detective
Second person
None of these matter for suspense
An all-knowing third person that reveals every character's secrets immediately

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: 2D Shape Properties and Symmetry
subjects.summary: Polygons can be classified by their sides and angles, and a shape has symmetry if it can be divided into matching mirror-image parts.

quiz.q: A shape has 6 equal sides and 6 equal angles. What is it called, and is it regular?
options: An irregular hexagon
A regular octagon
A regular hexagon
An irregular pentagon

quiz.q: A quadrilateral has exactly one pair of parallel sides. Which shape is this MOST likely to be?
options: A regular pentagon
A square
A trapezoid
A circle

quiz.q: Which shape has NO lines of symmetry at all?
options: A square
A regular hexagon
A perfect circle
A scalene triangle (all sides differ)

quiz.q: A shape has 4 equal sides but its angles are NOT all equal. Is it a square?
options: It cannot be determined
Yes, because equal sides are enough
No, because a square also requires four equal right angles
Yes, all quadrilaterals with equal sides are squares

quiz.q: How many lines of symmetry does a regular octagon (8 equal sides) have?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Digestive System in Depth
subjects.summary: The digestive system breaks down food step by step through the mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, and large intestine.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: The Digestive System in Depth

quiz.q: Food takes longer to digest fully in the stomach than to travel through the esophagus. What explains this?
options: Food doesn't actually change in the stomach
The stomach only stores food without processing it
The esophagus is longer than the stomach
The stomach churns and breaks it down

quiz.q: Why is the small intestine considered the main site of nutrient absorption rather than the stomach?
options: The small intestine does not touch digested food
Nutrient absorption happens only in the mouth
It's built for absorbing nutrients
The stomach absorbs all nutrients before food even arrives

quiz.q: If someone's large intestine were not functioning properly to absorb water, what would likely result?
options: Watery, less formed waste
Firmer, more solid waste
Improved digestion overall
No waste production at all

quiz.q: Which correctly orders the digestive path food travels?
options: Esophagus, mouth, small intestine, stomach, large intestine
Mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine
Stomach, mouth, esophagus, large intestine, small intestine
Small intestine, stomach, mouth, esophagus, large intestine

quiz.q: Why does the stomach's lining need to be protected from its own digestive acid?
options: The stomach has no lining at all
Acid only affects food, never body tissue
The acid could damage its own tissue
Stomach acid is too weak to cause any damage

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Geography of the Great Lakes
subjects.summary: The Great Lakes — Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, and Ontario — form a major freshwater system shared between Canada and the United States.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Geography of the Great Lakes

quiz.q: Why are the Great Lakes considered vital to both Canada's and the United States' economies?
options: They have no economic significance
They are located entirely within one country
They provide shipping and fresh water
They only benefit Canada

quiz.q: Which factor makes the Great Lakes an important source of freshwater for millions of people?
options: They are too small to matter
They are located in a desert region
They are made of saltwater like oceans
They hold a large portion of the world's surface freshwater

quiz.q: Why might pollution in one Great Lake affect both Canadian and American communities?
options: Pollution stays within a single country's water
The lakes have no connection to each other
The lakes and their waters are shared and connected across the border
Border lines physically separate the water

quiz.q: How does the connection between Lake Ontario and the Atlantic Ocean, via the St. Lawrence River, benefit trade?
options: It lets ships reach international ports
It has no effect on trade
It blocks all shipping
It only allows small boats with no cargo

quiz.q: Why would a map-maker likely place Lake Superior at the northwestern end of the Great Lakes chain?
options: Lake Superior's position on maps is random
Its position changes every year
It reflects its actual position
Lake Superior isn't part of the Great Lakes

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Using Transition Words
subjects.summary: Transition words like 'first,' 'next,' 'however,' and 'finally' help connect ideas and guide the reader through a text.

quiz.q: Which transition word would BEST connect: 'It rained all day. ___, the game continued as scheduled.'
options: Also
However
First
Similarly

quiz.q: A student writes a paragraph with steps but no transition words. What problem does this likely cause?
options: The steps become harder to follow in order
Transition words are never necessary
The paragraph becomes automatically organized
No problem occurs

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly uses a transition word to add a supporting idea?
options: The trip was fun. First, we saw dolphins.
The trip was fun. Also, we saw dolphins.
The trip was fun. Unless we saw dolphins.
The trip was fun. However, we saw dolphins.

quiz.q: Which transition word signals that a conclusion or final point is coming?
options: In conclusion
Meanwhile
Next
Also

quiz.q: Why might a writer use 'meanwhile' when describing two events?
options: To show a conclusion
To show a cause and effect relationship
To show the two events happening at the same time
To show a contrast only

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Transformations: Translations and Reflections
subjects.summary: A translation slides a shape without flipping or turning it; a reflection flips a shape over a line, like a mirror image.

quiz.q: A triangle is reflected over a horizontal line. Which property stays the same?
options: Its orientation, left/right
Its size and shape
Its position on the grid
Nothing stays the same

quiz.q: A shape is translated 3 units right and 2 units down. What happens to its size and orientation?
options: Both stay the same; position changes
The shape gets bigger
The shape flips
The shape rotates

quiz.q: Which transformation would turn the letter 'b' into the letter 'd'?
options: A reflection over a vertical line
Shrinking the letter
A translation only
No transformation can do this

quiz.q: A point at (2, 3) is translated 4 units left. What is its new position?
Which statement correctly distinguishes a translation from a reflection?
options: Both do the exact same thing
A translation flips a shape; a reflection slides it
Neither changes a shape's position
A translation slides a shape; a reflection flips it like a mirror

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Food Chains and Energy Pyramids
subjects.summary: A food chain shows how energy moves from the sun to producers, then to consumers, while an energy pyramid shows energy decreasing at each level.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Food Chains and Energy Pyramids

quiz.q: In a food chain of grass → rabbit → fox, which organism has the LEAST energy available to it?
options: Grass
Rabbit
All have equal energy
Fox

quiz.q: Why do energy pyramids typically show fewer organisms at the top than at the bottom?
options: This pattern is not actually true
There are always more predators than prey
Producers need more energy than consumers
Energy decreases at each level as it's used for life processes

quiz.q: If all the decomposers in an ecosystem disappeared, what would MOST likely happen?
options: Producers would multiply infinitely
More energy would be created
Nutrients would stop cycling back
Nothing would change

quiz.q: A hawk eats a snake that ate a mouse that ate seeds. Which organism is the producer in this chain?
options: Mouse
Seeds/plants
Hawk
Snake

quiz.q: Why might removing all foxes from an ecosystem cause a population explosion in rabbits?
options: Foxes have no connection to rabbit populations
Rabbits would have less food available
Without a predator, fewer are eaten
Rabbit populations always stay the same

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Trade Between Canada and Other Countries
subjects.summary: Canada trades goods and resources with countries around the world, which supports jobs and the economy.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Trade Between Canada and Other Countries

quiz.q: Why might Canada import bananas rather than growing them domestically?
options: Canada has too many bananas already
Canada's climate can't grow bananas
Imports are illegal in Canada
Bananas cannot be transported

quiz.q: How does international trade generally benefit both countries involved in an exchange?
options: Only the importing country benefits
Only the exporting country benefits
Trade never benefits either country
Each country gets what it can't make

quiz.q: If Canada exports lumber to another country, how does this affect Canadian jobs?
options: It eliminates all forestry jobs
It supports forestry jobs
It has no effect on jobs
It only creates jobs in the importing country

quiz.q: Why might a disruption in trade with a major partner, like the United States, significantly affect Canada's economy?
options: Trade disruptions never affect economies
The US has no economic relationship with Canada
Much trade depends on that one partner
Canada trades with no other countries

quiz.q: Which best explains why countries trade instead of only producing everything they need themselves?
options: Trade has no economic advantage
Every country can efficiently produce everything itself
Trade lets countries specialize
Trade only benefits large countries

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Recognizing Genre
subjects.summary: Genres like fiction, nonfiction, and poetry each have distinct features and purposes that readers can learn to identify.

quiz.q: A book tells a story about talking animals solving mysteries, clearly invented by the author. Which genre is this, and why?
options: Nonfiction, because it teaches a lesson
Poetry, because it has rhythm
Fiction — an imagined, invented story
Biography, because it's about real animals

quiz.q: A text uses short line breaks, rhyme, and vivid imagery to express emotion. Which genre does this describe?
options: Nonfiction textbook
A biography
A user manual
Poetry

quiz.q: Why might recognizing a text is a biography help a reader set expectations before reading?
options: They'll expect a fictional talking animal story
Biographies have no real purpose
They'll expect rhyming poetry
They'll expect factual information about a real person's life

quiz.q: A text has characters, dialogue, and an invented plot, but is inspired by a real historical event. How would this BEST be classified?
options: A dictionary entry
Purely nonfiction, since it involves history
Historical fiction
Poetry

quiz.q: Which clue would MOST strongly suggest a text is nonfiction rather than fiction?
options: It includes dragons and magic spells
It has rhyming stanzas
It has talking animals
It includes citations, dates, and verified facts

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Data Management: Bar and Line Graphs
subjects.summary: Bar graphs compare quantities across categories, while line graphs show how data changes over time.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Data Management: Bar and Line Graphs

quiz.q: A researcher wants to show how a plant's height changed every week over two months. Which graph type is MOST appropriate?
options: A line graph, since it shows change over time
A bar graph, since it compares categories
Neither works for this data
A graph is never useful for this data

quiz.q: A student wants to compare the favourite fruit choices of four different classes. Which graph type works best?
options: Only a pie chart could show this
A bar graph, to compare quantities across categories
A line graph, to show change over time
Neither graph works for this

quiz.q: A line graph shows temperature rising steadily from 8am to 2pm, then dropping sharply. What does the sharp drop likely indicate?
options: Temperature never actually changes
The data stopped being collected
A rapid decrease in temperature after 2pm
The graph has an error

quiz.q: Why is it important for a graph to have a title and labeled axes?
options: Titles and labels are optional decorations
Labels only matter for line graphs
Without them, readers cannot understand what the data represents
Graphs are always self-explanatory without labels

quiz.q: A bar graph shows that Monday had 40 visitors and Tuesday had 25. What is the difference between the two days?
options: 5 visitors
10 visitors
15 visitors
65 visitors

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Adaptations of Living Things
subjects.summary: Adaptations are special features or behaviours that help living things survive in their environment.

quiz.q: A desert fox has unusually large ears compared to other foxes. What is the LIKELY purpose of this adaptation?
options: To help release excess body heat in a hot climate
To store water
To help it hear better only, unrelated to climate
To make it look different for no reason

quiz.q: Why would a fish's gills NOT be useful for a land mammal?
options: Land mammals also breathe through gills
Adaptations always transfer between environments
Gills work equally well in water and air
Gills work in water, not air

quiz.q: A chameleon changes colour to match its surroundings. How does this adaptation increase its chances of survival?
options: It helps the chameleon fly
It has no survival benefit
It makes the chameleon louder
It helps it avoid predators or prey

quiz.q: Over generations, moths became darker after tree bark darkened from pollution. What does this best illustrate?
options: Adaptations can develop over generations in response to environmental changes
Adaptations have no connection to environment
This has nothing to do with survival
Moths intentionally chose to change colour instantly

quiz.q: Which pairing correctly matches an adaptation with its environmental benefit?
options: Webbed feet on a duck — protection from predators only
Long legs on a desert lizard — increased water storage
Thick blubber on a whale — insulation in cold ocean water
Bright colours on a poisonous frog — camouflage to hide completely

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Local Government and Your Municipality
subjects.summary: Local (municipal) governments make decisions about community services like roads, parks, libraries, and waste collection.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Local Government and Your Municipality

quiz.q: Why might a municipal government be better suited than the federal government to decide on local speed limits?
options: Speed limits are set internationally
Municipal governments understand and respond to specific local community needs
Local governments have no authority over roads
The federal government makes all decisions about every city

quiz.q: A city council votes to build a new public library. Which level of government would MOST likely make this decision?
options: Municipal government
Federal government only
No government decides this
International organizations

quiz.q: Why do citizens often interact with municipal government more frequently in daily life than with federal government?
options: Citizens never interact with any level of government
Federal government manages every daily service
Municipal government has no real responsibilities
It manages everyday local services

quiz.q: If a community wants a new park built, which step would be part of the municipal government process?
options: Only the Prime Minister can approve new parks
Bringing the proposal to city council for consideration and budgeting
Parks require international approval
No government involvement is needed to build a park

quiz.q: Why is understanding local government important for engaged citizenship, even for young people?
options: Local government has no connection to citizens' lives
Only adults over 50 are affected by local government
Local government issues never affect anyone
Local decisions affect daily life

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Comparing Two Texts on the Same Topic
subjects.summary: Comparing two texts on the same topic helps readers notice different perspectives, details, and ways of presenting information.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Comparing Two Texts on the Same Topic

quiz.q: Two articles describe the same wildfire — one focuses on economic losses, the other on environmental impact. What does this reveal?
options: Authors can focus on different aspects
Both articles must be describing different events
The event only happened one way and one article is wrong
Comparing texts is unnecessary here

quiz.q: If one article calls a historical event a 'necessary decision' and another calls it a 'tragic mistake,' what does this show?
options: Both texts describe different events
A factual error in one of the articles
A difference in perspective or interpretation of the same event
Nothing meaningful can be learned

quiz.q: Why might a student use a Venn diagram when comparing two nonfiction texts on the same animal?
options: To organize what's unique and shared
Venn diagrams only work for math
To make the texts longer
To avoid reading either text

quiz.q: Two texts about renewable energy: one emphasizes benefits, the other costs and challenges. Reading both would help a student...
options: Assume one source is always wrong
Understand a more balanced picture
Ignore the topic entirely
Only trust one source blindly

quiz.q: Which is the BEST reason to compare a primary source (eyewitness account) with a secondary source (textbook summary)?
options: There's no benefit to comparing them
Primary sources are always less accurate
To compare firsthand and later views
Secondary sources should never be trusted

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Financial Literacy: Making Change and Budgets
subjects.summary: Financial literacy includes calculating change from a purchase and creating a simple budget that balances income and expenses.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Financial Literacy: Making Change and Budgets

quiz.q: An item costs $14.75. You pay with a $20 bill. How much change should you receive?
A student earns $30 allowance, spends $12 on a toy, and saves the rest. What fraction of their allowance did they save?
A monthly budget has $50 income and $65 in planned expenses. What does this indicate?
options: The budget has a $15 surplus
The budget has a $15 deficit
There is no way to know
The budget is balanced

quiz.q: Which is the BEST strategy for creating a balanced budget?
options: Spending as much as possible immediately
Only tracking expenses, never income
Ignoring income entirely
Making sure planned expenses do not exceed income

quiz.q: A toy costs $22.40. You have two $10 bills and a $5 bill. Do you have enough, and how much change would you get?
options: No, you are short
Yes, with $2.60 in change
Yes, with $5.00 in change
Yes, with exactly $0 in change

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Renewable vs. Non-Renewable Resources Revisited
subjects.summary: A deeper look at how renewable resources like wind and solar are managed sustainably, while non-renewable resources like fossil fuels are limited and impact the environment.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Renewable vs. Non-Renewable Resources Revisited

quiz.q: Why might a country invest heavily in solar and wind energy despite higher upfront installation costs?
options: Non-renewable resources are unlimited
They don't run out and pollute less
Renewable energy has no environmental benefit
Solar and wind are always cheaper immediately

quiz.q: A power plant burns coal to generate electricity. What is a likely long-term consequence of relying on this method?
options: This method has no downsides
Coal will regenerate within days
Depleting a resource and polluting air
No environmental impact at all

quiz.q: Why is hydroelectric power classified as renewable while natural gas is classified as non-renewable?
options: Natural gas is actually renewable too
Hydroelectric power also depletes permanently
Water renews fast; gas takes ages
Both take the same amount of time to form

quiz.q: Why does reducing energy waste matter even in a home powered entirely by renewable solar energy?
options: Producing energy still uses resources
There's no reason to conserve renewable energy
Solar power cannot be wasted
Renewable energy has unlimited capacity so waste never matters

quiz.q: Which scenario best demonstrates a trade-off between economic and environmental factors in energy use?
options: Choosing cheap coal despite pollution
There are never trade-offs in energy choices
Renewable energy always costs nothing
Non-renewable energy has zero cost

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Environmental Issues Affecting Canada
subjects.summary: Canada faces environmental issues such as climate change, melting Arctic ice, and the need for conservation of natural resources.

quiz.q: Why is melting Arctic ice a concern that affects more than just the Arctic region itself?
options: It has no effect beyond the Arctic
It only affects animals living in the Arctic
It raises sea levels worldwide
Melting ice has no connection to climate

quiz.q: How might conservation efforts in one province indirectly benefit a different province's environment?
options: There is no environmental connection between provinces
Ecosystems, water systems, and air quality can be interconnected across regions
Provinces are entirely disconnected environmentally
Conservation only affects the province doing it

quiz.q: Why might reducing plastic waste be considered an important environmental action in Canada?
options: Reducing waste has no benefit
Plastic waste disappears naturally within days
Plastic pollution harms wildlife
Plastic has no environmental impact

quiz.q: A community plants trees along a riverbank. How does this action likely benefit the local environment?
options: This action harms local wildlife
Trees reduce erosion, improve quality
Trees have no environmental benefit
Planting trees increases pollution

quiz.q: Why is addressing environmental issues often described as a shared responsibility rather than one group's job alone?
options: It affects everyone, so all must help
Individuals have no ability to help the environment
Environmental issues only affect one group of people
Only governments can address environmental issues

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Sequence and Chronological Order in Nonfiction
subjects.summary: Nonfiction texts often present events or steps in chronological (time) order, using signal words like 'first,' 'then,' and 'after.'
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Sequence and Chronological Order in Nonfiction

quiz.q: A recipe lists steps out of order: 'Bake for 20 minutes. Mix the batter. Preheat the oven.' Why does this order cause a problem?
options: It doesn't matter what order cooking steps are in
This order is actually correct
Baking should come last, not first
Recipes never need a specific order

quiz.q: Which text would MOST likely use chronological order as its main structure?
options: A comparison of two animals' habitats
A dictionary entry
A biography of someone's whole life
A persuasive essay about school rules

quiz.q: Why might a historian use a timeline instead of a random list of events?
options: Timelines have no advantage over random lists
A timeline shows order and cause-effect
Random lists are always clearer
Timelines remove all dates

quiz.q: In a set of instructions, why would starting with 'Finally' instead of 'First' create confusion?
options: Finally always means the same as First
It puts the last step out of order
Instructions don't need an order
It would have no effect on understanding

quiz.q: An article about the telephone's history jumps between decades randomly instead of in order. What is the LIKELY effect on readers?
options: Order has no impact on understanding development over time
Readers may find it confusing to understand how the technology developed over time
This is the best way to describe history
Readers will understand it more clearly

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Patterning with Tables of Values
subjects.summary: A table of values shows how one quantity changes in relation to another, helping you find and describe a pattern's rule.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Patterning with Tables of Values

quiz.q: A pattern rule is 'multiply by 3, then subtract 1,' starting at 2. What is the second term?
A table shows: position 1→4, position 2→7, position 3→10. What is the pattern rule?
options: Add 4 each time
Multiply by 2
Subtract 3 each time
Add 3 to the previous term

quiz.q: Using the rule 'double the term number and add 1,' what is the value when the term number is 5?
A table of values shows a constant difference of 5 between consecutive terms. What kind of pattern does this suggest?
options: A linear, steady, consistent pattern
A random pattern with no rule
No pattern exists
A pattern that changes rule each time

quiz.q: If a pattern starts at 100 and the rule is 'subtract 7,' what is the 4th term (100, 93, 86, ...)?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Environmental Stewardship Projects
subjects.summary: Environmental stewardship means taking action to protect and care for the environment, such as through recycling, planting trees, or reducing waste.

quiz.q: A school starts a composting program for cafeteria food waste. How does this support environmental stewardship?
options: It has no environmental benefit
It cuts landfill waste and feeds soil
It only affects the school's budget
It increases pollution

quiz.q: Why might reducing consumption be considered even more effective than recycling alone?
options: Reducing consumption has no environmental benefit
Recycling always uses fewer resources than reducing consumption
Preventing waste avoids recycling costs
Recycling and reducing have identical effects

quiz.q: A community plants a pollinator garden to support local bees and butterflies. What environmental benefit does this provide?
options: It reduces the bee population
It boosts biodiversity and pollination
It only benefits humans
It has no effect on the ecosystem

quiz.q: Why is water conservation considered an important stewardship action, even in areas with plentiful rainfall?
options: Water is infinite everywhere, so conservation is unnecessary
Rainfall eliminates the need for any conservation
Treating water still uses energy
Only desert regions need to conserve water

quiz.q: A stewardship project involves removing invasive plant species from a local park. Why would this help the ecosystem?
options: This action has no ecological effect
Removing plants always harms ecosystems
Invasive plants crowd out native ones
Invasive species always help ecosystems thrive

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Historical Figures in Canadian History
subjects.summary: Canadian history includes notable figures, such as Laura Secord and Terry Fox, whose actions had a lasting impact on the country.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Historical Figures in Canadian History

quiz.q: Why is Laura Secord remembered as significant in Canadian history despite not being a soldier?
options: Her warning helped prepare defenders
She led an army into battle personally
She has no historical significance
She was a formal government official

quiz.q: Terry Fox's Marathon of Hope is often cited as an example of what kind of impact?
options: An impact limited only to sports
An event with no lasting significance
A purely local, forgotten event
How determination can inspire change

quiz.q: Why might studying historical figures from diverse backgrounds give students a fuller understanding of Canadian history?
options: It shows that many different people and perspectives have shaped the country's story
History is only about one perspective
Diverse figures have no historical relevance
Only one type of person has ever influenced Canadian history

quiz.q: What does Terry Fox's continued effort despite having lost a leg to cancer suggest about his character?
options: He was unaffected by any hardship
He completed his goal with no difficulty
A lack of planning
Determination despite great hardship

quiz.q: Why do biographies of historical figures like Laura Secord often include the broader historical context of their time?
options: Context has no relevance to a person's actions
Understanding the context helps explain why their actions were significant
Biographies never include historical background
This makes the biography less accurate

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Revising and Editing a Paragraph
subjects.summary: Revising improves ideas and organization, while editing fixes spelling, grammar, and punctuation errors in a paragraph.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Revising and Editing a Paragraph

quiz.q: A paragraph has correct spelling and punctuation, but its ideas are disorganized and hard to follow. What does it need?
options: Revising, to improve organization and clarity
Editing only, since spelling is correct
A completely new topic
Nothing, since spelling and punctuation are fine

quiz.q: Which change is an example of EDITING rather than revising?
options: Correcting a subject-verb agreement error
Reorganizing paragraphs so ideas flow logically
Strengthening a weak thesis statement
Adding a new supporting example

quiz.q: Why might a writer read their paragraph aloud during revision?
options: It only helps with spelling
It replaces the need to revise at all
Reading aloud has no benefit
To catch awkward or unclear phrasing

quiz.q: A sentence reads: 'The dog runned fast and it was scary because it barked.' Which issue should be fixed FIRST through editing?
options: The grammar error 'runned' should become 'ran'
The sentence needs an entirely new topic
The sentence has no errors
The sentence should be deleted entirely

quiz.q: Why is it recommended to revise BEFORE editing, rather than the reverse?
options: Editing should always happen first
Big-picture ideas should be fixed first
Revising is not necessary if editing is done well
Order makes no difference

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Rounding Decimals
subjects.summary: Rounding decimals means simplifying a number to a given place value, like the nearest tenth or whole number.

quiz.q: A store advertises '$4.99, rounded to the nearest dollar is $5.' Which rounding rule explains this?
options: 0.99 is closer to 1.00, so it rounds up
This rounding is incorrect
0.99 always rounds down
Prices are never rounded

quiz.q: Round 8.995 to the nearest hundredth.
A runner's time is 12.348 seconds. Rounded to the nearest tenth, what is their time?
Why might rounding be useful when estimating a total grocery bill quickly?
options: Rounding makes numbers less useful
Estimating is never helpful
Rounding always gives the exact total
It gives a quick, close estimate

quiz.q: Round 0.449 to the nearest tenth. Which is correct and why?
options: 0.4, because the hundredths digit (4) rounds down
0.5, because all decimals round up
0.40, which is different from 0.4
0.45, because it stops at hundredths

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Moon's Phases
subjects.summary: The moon appears to change shape over about a month due to its orbit around Earth and how sunlight reflects off it.

quiz.q: A person sees a moon that is exactly half-lit, appearing as a semicircle. Approximately how far into the lunar cycle might this be?
options: Exactly halfway between two full moons only
About one week (a quarter phase)
The very start of the cycle (new moon)
The very end, right before a new full moon

quiz.q: Why does the moon appear to change shape throughout the month, even though the moon itself doesn't physically change?
options: Varying views of its sunlit half
Clouds cause the moon to look different
The moon actually changes shape each night
The moon creates its own changing light

quiz.q: If tonight is a new moon, approximately how many days until the next full moon?
options: About 1 day
About 25 days
About 14-15 days, half the cycle
The two events never relate to each other

quiz.q: Why can we never see a permanently dark side of the moon that never receives sunlight?
options: The moon has no far side
This is not actually true; the moon doesn't rotate
The far side never gets any sunlight ever
The far side of the moon does receive sunlight

quiz.q: A student claims the moon produces its own light like the sun. Why is this claim incorrect?
options: The moon reflects sunlight rather than producing its own light
This claim is scientifically accurate
The moon glows due to its own chemical reactions
The moon and sun work identically

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Rural and Urban Life in Canada
subjects.summary: Rural areas are less densely populated and often focus on agriculture, while urban areas are densely populated cities with more services and infrastructure.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Rural and Urban Life in Canada

quiz.q: Why might a rural community have fewer public transit options than an urban city?
options: Population has no connection to transit availability
Urban areas never have transit either
Low density makes transit less practical
Rural areas are legally prohibited from having transit

quiz.q: A family moves from a large city to a small farming town. Which lifestyle change would they MOST likely experience?
options: Access to more skyscrapers and subways
Immediate loss of all modern conveniences
More space, slower pace, fewer services
No change in lifestyle at all

quiz.q: Why might urban areas typically have greater access to specialized services like hospitals with advanced technology?
options: Urban areas have no advantage in service access
More people support specialized services
Rural areas always have equal or greater access
Specialized services are randomly distributed

quiz.q: How might farming in a rural area differ in scale and purpose from a small urban community garden?
options: Rural farms are bigger and commercial
Urban gardens always produce more food than rural farms
Both are always identical in scale and purpose
Rural areas cannot support farming

quiz.q: Why might some people prefer rural life while others prefer urban life?
options: Priorities vary by individual
Everyone prefers the exact same lifestyle
Preference has no connection to lifestyle differences
Rural and urban life offer identical experiences

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Dictionary and Thesaurus Skills
subjects.summary: A dictionary gives word meanings, pronunciations, and parts of speech, while a thesaurus provides synonyms and antonyms.

quiz.q: A writer wants to avoid repeating the word 'said' too many times in a story. Which resource would help most?
options: A dictionary, to find the definition of 'said'
An atlas
A calendar
A thesaurus, for varied synonyms

quiz.q: Why might a dictionary entry include a word's part of speech alongside its definition?
options: Dictionaries never include part of speech
Part of speech has no connection to meaning
This information is unrelated to word usage
Meaning can depend on grammatical use

quiz.q: A student replaces 'big' with 'enormous' using a thesaurus, but the new word doesn't fit the sentence's tone. What does this show?
options: Synonyms can differ in connotation
Word choice never affects tone
Thesauruses should never be used
All synonyms are always interchangeable

quiz.q: Which situation would MOST likely require using a dictionary rather than a thesaurus?
options: Finding a more vivid word for 'walked'
Checking meaning and pronunciation
Avoiding repeating the same word
Making writing more varied

quiz.q: Why is understanding connotation, the feeling associated with a word, important when choosing a synonym?
options: Synonyms can feel emotionally different
Only denotation matters in writing
Connotation has no effect on word choice
All synonyms have identical connotations

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Perimeter and Area of Composite Shapes
subjects.summary: Composite shapes are made of two or more simple shapes; you find their area and perimeter by breaking them into smaller, familiar parts.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Perimeter and Area of Composite Shapes

quiz.q: A composite shape is a 5×4 rectangle with a 2×2 square removed from one corner. What is its area?
options: 16 units²
20 units²
24 units²
12 units²

quiz.q: An L-shaped garden is made of a 6×3 rectangle and a 3×2 rectangle attached to it. What is its total area?
options: 24 units²
18 units²
20 units²
26 units²

quiz.q: To find the perimeter of an L-shaped figure, which approach is correct?
options: Add all the outer edges, notch included
Add the areas of each rectangle
Only measure the top and bottom
Multiply the two largest sides only

quiz.q: A composite area is found by adding a triangle's and a rectangle's areas. Why might a builder need this calculation in real life?
options: Area calculations have no real-world application
Only the perimeter matters in construction
To find how much material is needed
Composite shapes never appear in real buildings

quiz.q: A shape is formed by a 10×6 rectangle with a 4×3 rectangle cut out. What is the remaining area?
options: 48 units²
54 units²
60 units²
42 units²

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Energy Pyramids and Ecosystem Balance
subjects.summary: Energy pyramids show that less energy is available at each higher level of a food chain, which limits how many organisms an ecosystem can support.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Energy Pyramids and Ecosystem Balance

quiz.q: An ecosystem has 10,000 units of energy at the producer level, and only about 10% transfers between levels. What would you expect at the top predator level?
options: Exactly 10,000, since energy never changes
More than 10,000, since energy increases upward
Significantly less than 10,000, since energy decreases at each level
Zero, since predators use no energy

quiz.q: If a disease wiped out most of the producers (plants) in an ecosystem, what would likely happen to the rest of the food chain?
options: Predators would have more food than ever
Consumers would face food shortages
Nothing would change for any other organism
Energy would increase at higher levels

quiz.q: Why is it inefficient, in terms of energy, for humans to eat animals that eat other animals rather than eating plants directly?
options: Energy is lost at each food chain step
There is no energy difference at any level
Eating meat always uses less energy than eating plants
Energy increases the higher up the food chain you eat

quiz.q: A wildlife manager notices a booming deer population and a declining wolf population in the same area. What might this suggest?
options: Wolves have no effect on deer numbers
Fewer predators may be allowing the prey population to grow unchecked
This pattern never occurs in real ecosystems
Deer and wolf populations are always unrelated

quiz.q: Why do scientists study energy pyramids when trying to understand how many organisms an ecosystem can support?
options: Energy pyramids only apply to plants
Energy pyramids have no connection to population size
The pyramid shows how energy availability limits population sizes at each level
All levels of a pyramid can support unlimited organisms

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada's Role in World Organizations
subjects.summary: Canada participates in international organizations like the United Nations to cooperate with other countries on issues like peace, health, and human rights.
subjects.resourceLabel: YouTube: Canada's Role in World Organizations

quiz.q: Why might a country like Canada choose to participate in international organizations rather than act entirely alone?
options: Global issues need cooperation
Canada is legally required to join every organization
International organizations have no benefits
Acting alone is always more effective

quiz.q: How do Canadian peacekeepers contribute to global stability?
options: By creating conflicts abroad
By supporting peace in conflict regions
By only participating in domestic issues
By avoiding all international involvement

quiz.q: Why might climate change be considered a global issue requiring international cooperation rather than a single country's responsibility?
options: Climate change only affects one specific country
International cooperation makes environmental issues worse
Countries cannot influence global environmental issues
Its effects, like rising temperatures, cross national borders and impact the whole planet

quiz.q: What is one likely benefit for Canada of having a voice within an organization like the United Nations?
options: It has no benefit for Canada
It lets Canada shape global decisions
It removes Canada's ability to make its own decisions
It isolates Canada from world events

quiz.q: Why might smaller countries value international organizations like the UN particularly highly?
options: Only large countries benefit from international organizations
Smaller countries have no interest in global organizations
They give small nations a global voice
International organizations only include large countries

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Evaluating Sources for Reliability
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Reading and Media Literacy strands: evaluating a source means checking who wrote it, when it was published, and whether it is supported by evidence, before deciding whether to trust it.

quiz.q: Which is a key question when evaluating a source’s reliability?
options: What colour is the website?
Does it have pictures?
How long is the article?
Who wrote it, and are they a credible authority on the topic?

quiz.q: A reliable source is generally one that ___.
options: Is the first result in any search
Has no author listed at all
Is supported by evidence and comes from a credible author or organization
Contains only opinions with no facts

quiz.q: Why should readers check when a source was published?
options: Dates have no connection to reliability
Older information may be outdated on fast-changing topics
Publication date never matters
Only brand new sources are ever accurate

quiz.q: Which is a warning sign that a source might be unreliable?
options: It cites specific evidence and data
It was reviewed by experts
It presents only one extreme opinion with no evidence
It lists its author clearly

quiz.q: Comparing information across multiple credible sources helps readers ___.
options: Guarantee that one source must be wrong
Avoid learning anything useful
Confirm whether the information is accurate and consistent
Ignore the topic entirely

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Adding and Subtracting Mixed Numbers
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Number strand: adding or subtracting mixed numbers involves combining the whole numbers and the fractions separately, and regrouping when a fraction sum is improper.

quiz.q: What is 2 and 1/4 plus 1 and 2/4?
options: 3 and 1/4
3 and 2/4
4 and 1/4
3 and 3/4

quiz.q: What is 5 and 3/5 minus 2 and 1/5?
options: 3 and 2/5
2 and 2/5
7 and 2/5
3 and 4/5

quiz.q: When adding mixed numbers, you should add the ___ separately.
options: Only the whole numbers
Whole numbers and fractions
Only the fractions
Denominators only

quiz.q: What is 4 and 3/6 minus 1 and 5/6?
options: 2 and 4/6
2 and 2/6
3 and 4/6
3 and 2/6

quiz.q: If a fraction sum becomes improper, such as 1 and 5/4, you should ___.
options: Round down to 1
Ignore the whole number
Leave it as is
Regroup it into 2 and 1/4

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Levers and Mechanical Advantage
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Science Structures and Mechanisms strand: a lever provides mechanical advantage by multiplying the force applied, depending on where the fulcrum is placed relative to the load and effort.

quiz.q: Mechanical advantage refers to ___.
options: The colour of a machine
How many parts a machine has
How a machine multiplies the force applied to it
How heavy a machine is

quiz.q: On a lever, the fixed point it pivots around is called the ___.
options: Fulcrum
Load
Effort
Axle

quiz.q: Moving a lever’s fulcrum closer to the load generally ___ the mechanical advantage.
options: Decreases
Has no effect on
Increases
Eliminates

quiz.q: Which everyday tool uses lever action to provide mechanical advantage?
options: A flat table
A window pane
A brick
A pair of scissors

quiz.q: Why do engineers consider mechanical advantage when designing tools?
options: It always makes tools weaker
It only matters for large machines
Mechanical advantage has no practical use
It helps tools accomplish tasks with less applied effort

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The War of 1812
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Social Studies Heritage and Identity strand: the War of 1812 was fought between the United States and Britain (including its colonies in what is now Canada), with First Nations allies playing a significant role in defending Canadian territory.

quiz.q: The War of 1812 was fought mainly between which two sides?
options: Spain and Portugal
Canada and France
The United States and Britain (including its Canadian colonies)
Canada and Britain

quiz.q: Which group played a significant role in defending Canadian territory during the War of 1812?
options: Astronauts
The Ancient Romans
No one else was involved
First Nations allies

quiz.q: The War of 1812 took place mainly in what is now ___.
options: Australia
Ancient Egypt
Modern-day Canada and parts of the United States
South America

quiz.q: Why is the War of 1812 significant to Canadian history?
options: It occurred in modern times
It took place entirely in Europe
It has no connection to Canadian history
It helped shape early Canadian identity and border relations with the United States

quiz.q: Which historical figure is well known for their role defending Canada during the War of 1812?
options: A fictional superhero
A modern astronaut
A recent prime minister
Laura Secord

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Active and Passive Voice
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Writing strand: in active voice, the subject performs the action (the dog chased the ball), while in passive voice, the subject receives the action (the ball was chased by the dog).

quiz.q: Which sentence is written in active voice?
options: The ball was kicked by Sam.
The ball had been kicked.
Sam kicked the ball.
The ball is being kicked.

quiz.q: Which sentence is written in passive voice?
options: The cat chased the mouse.
The mouse was chased by the cat.
The cat is chasing the mouse.
The cat will chase the mouse.

quiz.q: In active voice, the subject of the sentence ___.
options: Is always missing
Performs the action
Never appears
Receives the action

quiz.q: Why do writers often prefer active voice?
options: Passive voice is always required
Active voice removes the subject entirely
It tends to be clearer and more direct
Active voice is always grammatically incorrect

quiz.q: Rewrite in active voice: The letter was written by Maria.
options: Maria wrote the letter.
The letter is written.
Written was the letter by Maria.
The letter wrote Maria.

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Dividing Fractions by Whole Numbers
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Number strand: dividing a fraction by a whole number means splitting that fraction into that many equal parts, which can be found by multiplying the denominator by the whole number.

quiz.q: What is 1/2 divided by 2?
What is 3/4 divided by 3?
To divide a fraction by a whole number, you can ___.
options: Multiply the numerator by the whole number
Subtract the whole number from the numerator
Add the whole number to the denominator
Multiply the denominator by the whole number

quiz.q: What is 2/3 divided by 2?
What is 5/6 divided by 5?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Density and Buoyancy
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Science Matter and Energy strand: density is how tightly packed an object’s mass is within its volume, and buoyancy is the upward force that determines whether an object floats or sinks in a liquid.

quiz.q: Density is best described as ___.
options: How much mass is packed into a given volume
The colour of an object
How heavy an object feels only
The temperature of an object

quiz.q: An object will generally float in water if its density is ___ than water.
options: Density has no effect on floating
Greater
Less
Exactly equal in every case

quiz.q: Buoyancy refers to ___.
options: The force that pulls objects downward only
The upward force that can cause an object to float
A property of solids only
A type of energy source

quiz.q: Why does a large steel ship float, even though steel is denser than water?
options: Steel always floats regardless of shape
Ships are not actually made of steel
Its overall shape displaces enough water to create sufficient buoyant force
Buoyancy does not apply to ships

quiz.q: Which object would most likely sink in water?
options: A hollow plastic ball
A rock
An inflated balloon
A wooden log

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Rebellions of 1837
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Social Studies Heritage and Identity strand: the Rebellions of 1837 in Upper and Lower Canada were uprisings by reformers seeking more democratic, accountable government, led in part by figures like William Lyon Mackenzie.

quiz.q: The Rebellions of 1837 mainly took place in ___.
options: British Columbia
The Arctic
The United States
Upper and Lower Canada

quiz.q: Reformers involved in the Rebellions of 1837 were seeking ___.
options: No government at all
Less democratic government
More democratic, accountable government
A return to earlier colonial rule with no changes

quiz.q: Which historical figure is associated with the Rebellions of 1837 in Upper Canada?
options: A fictional character
William Lyon Mackenzie
An ancient Roman leader
A modern politician

quiz.q: Why are the Rebellions of 1837 considered important to Canadian political history?
options: They had no lasting effect on Canadian government
They led to no political change whatsoever
They contributed to later reforms toward more responsible government
They occurred after Confederation

quiz.q: The term responsible government refers to ___.
options: A government located outside of Canada
A government with no accountability at all
A government run entirely by one unelected ruler
A government accountable to elected representatives of the people

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Argumentative Essays
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Writing strand: an argumentative essay presents a clear position on an issue and supports it with logical reasons and evidence, while also acknowledging other viewpoints.

quiz.q: An argumentative essay is built around ___.
options: A clear position supported by reasons and evidence
Only the writer’s feelings with no support
A summary with no opinion
A list of unrelated facts

quiz.q: Why is it useful for an argumentative essay to acknowledge other viewpoints?
options: Acknowledging other views weakens an essay
It replaces the need for evidence
Other viewpoints should always be ignored
It shows the writer has considered the issue thoughtfully and strengthens the argument

quiz.q: Evidence in an argumentative essay might include ___.
options: Facts, statistics, or expert opinions
Random unrelated stories
Nothing at all
Only the writer’s favourite colour

quiz.q: A strong argumentative essay typically ends with ___.
options: No conclusion at all
A brand new, unrelated topic
A conclusion that restates the position and key reasons
A list of unrelated questions

quiz.q: Which is an example of a clear argumentative position?
options: Recess happens at school.
Schools should have longer recess because it improves focus and health.
Some schools have recess.
Recess can be long or short.

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Introducing Percentages
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Number strand: percent means out of 100, and percentages can be converted to and from fractions and decimals, such as 50 percent equalling 1/2 or 0.5.

quiz.q: What does 25 percent mean?
options: 25 out of 10
25 out of 1000
25 out of 100
2.5 out of 100

quiz.q: What is 50 percent as a decimal?
What is 3/4 written as a percent?
What is 10 percent of 200?
What is 0.75 written as a percent?

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Nervous System
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Science Human Body Systems strand: the nervous system, made up of the brain, spinal cord, and nerves, controls the body by sending and receiving electrical signals throughout the body.

quiz.q: The nervous system is made up mainly of the ___.
options: Brain, spinal cord, and nerves
Heart and blood vessels only
Stomach and intestines
Lungs and diaphragm

quiz.q: The brain is considered the body’s ___.
options: Storage for food
Digestive organ
Filter for blood
Control centre

quiz.q: Nerves carry information through the body in the form of ___.
options: Digested food
Air only
Bone tissue
Electrical signals

quiz.q: The spinal cord’s main role is to ___.
options: Connect the brain to the rest of the body’s nerves
Pump blood
Digest food
Filter air

quiz.q: Why is the nervous system important for reacting quickly, such as pulling your hand away from something hot?
options: It sends rapid signals between the body and brain to trigger a fast response
Only the digestive system controls reactions
Reactions happen with no signals at all
The nervous system has no role in reactions

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: United Empire Loyalists and the Founding of Ontario
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Social Studies Heritage and Identity strand: United Empire Loyalists were settlers who remained loyal to Britain during the American Revolution and moved north, playing a key role in founding early Ontario communities.

quiz.q: United Empire Loyalists were people who ___.
options: Remained loyal to Britain during the American Revolution
Had no connection to Britain at all
Supported the American Revolution
Immigrated from Asia

quiz.q: After the American Revolution, many Loyalists moved to ___.
options: What is now Ontario and other parts of British North America
South America
Europe permanently
Nowhere, they all stayed in the same place
