Listening to the experiences of residential school survivors
Refusing to speak with anyone affected
Ignoring all historical evidence

quiz.q: Why is learning about the Truth and Reconciliation Commission important for students today?
options: It helps build understanding of this history and supports ongoing reconciliation efforts
This history has no relevance today
It should be avoided in schools entirely
It has no connection to Canadian identity

quiz.q: Reconciliation, in this context, refers to ___.
options: A type of sporting event
Ignoring the effects of past policies
Working to repair relationships and address historical harms
A type of geography lesson only

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Synthesizing Information from Multiple Texts
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Reading strand: synthesizing means combining information from multiple texts or sources to form a more complete understanding of a topic than any single source could provide alone.

quiz.q: Synthesizing information means ___.
options: Reading only a single source and ignoring all others
Combining information from multiple sources into a fuller understanding
Copying one text exactly
Ignoring all sources completely

quiz.q: Why might a reader consult multiple texts on the same topic?
options: One source always provides complete information
To build a more complete and well-rounded understanding
Additional sources never add useful information
Consulting multiple texts is a waste of time

quiz.q: When synthesizing, a reader should look for ___.
options: Nothing related to the sources’ content
Only the exact same information repeated in every source
Random unrelated facts with no connections
Connections, similarities, and differences across sources

quiz.q: Which is an example of synthesizing information?
options: Ignoring every source except one
Reading only the title of one article
Combining facts from two articles to write a well-rounded report
Copying a single paragraph exactly

quiz.q: Why is synthesizing considered an advanced reading skill?
options: It only involves reading a single sentence
It requires understanding, comparing, and combining ideas from multiple sources
It requires no understanding of any text
It has no connection to comprehension

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Review: Fractions, Percent, Integers, and Geometry
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Number and Geometry strands review: this lesson revisits fraction operations, percent, integers, and geometry concepts like circles and volume covered across recent lessons.

quiz.q: What is 1/4 plus 1/4?
What is 20 percent of 50?
Which is colder: -10 degrees or -2 degrees?
options: Cannot be determined
-2 degrees
They are equal
-10 degrees

quiz.q: What is the diameter of a circle with a radius of 6 cm?
options: 6 cm
12 cm
18 cm
3 cm

quiz.q: Why is it useful to review fraction, percent, integer, and geometry skills together?
options: These topics have no connection to each other
Review is never useful in math
These related number and spatial concepts reinforce each other for stronger overall understanding
Each topic must be learned in complete isolation

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Human Body Systems and Forces
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Science review: this lesson revisits the nervous, circulatory, respiratory, skeletal, muscular, and excretory systems alongside forces like friction, air resistance, and mechanical advantage covered recently.

quiz.q: Which system is responsible for sending electrical signals throughout the body?
options: The excretory system
The nervous system
The muscular system
The skeletal system

quiz.q: Which system transports oxygen and nutrients through the body?
options: The respiratory system
The circulatory system
The skeletal system
The excretory system

quiz.q: Which force resists motion between two surfaces in contact?
options: Air resistance
Buoyancy
Magnetism
Friction

quiz.q: Which body system removes waste products from the blood?
options: The respiratory system
The nervous system
The muscular system
The excretory system

quiz.q: Why is it valuable to review body systems and forces together at this point?
options: It helps connect and reinforce related concepts learned across recent lessons
These topics are entirely unrelated to each other
Review provides no benefit in science
Each system must always be studied in isolation

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Review: Canadian Government, Rights, and Recent History
subjects.summary: Ontario Grade 5 Social Studies review: this lesson revisits Canada’s path to independence, the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, the courts, political parties, and reconciliation efforts covered across recent lessons.

quiz.q: Which document, adopted in 1982, includes the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms?
options: The Constitution Act
The Treaty of Paris
The British North America Act only
The Statute of Westminster

quiz.q: Which body in Canada is responsible for interpreting and applying the law?
options: Only the media
The courts
No formal body exists for this
Only municipal councils

quiz.q: The Truth and Reconciliation Commission focused on documenting the history and impact of ___.
options: The Great Depression
The residential school system
The War of 1812
The building of the railway

quiz.q: Political parties in Canada generally represent ___.
options: Identical positions with no real differences
Different ideas about how the country should be governed
Only local municipal issues
No connection to government at all

quiz.q: Why is reviewing recent Canadian government and history topics together useful?
options: Each topic must be studied with no connection to the others
It helps reinforce how these related civic and historical concepts connect
These topics have no meaningful connections
Review is never useful in social studies

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Analyzing an Author’s Style and Word Choice
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: an author’s style is shaped by their word choice, sentence length, and tone, and analyzing it helps readers understand how a writer creates a particular effect.

quiz.q: An author’s style is shaped by their ___.
options: The colour of the book cover
Word choice, sentence length, and tone
A concept unrelated to reading
Only the number of pages in the book

quiz.q: Why might an author use short, choppy sentences in a story?
options: A reason unrelated to author’s style
Short sentences never affect how a story feels
Only to fill space on the page
To create a sense of tension or urgency

quiz.q: What does it mean to analyze an author’s word choice?
options: A concept unrelated to analyzing style
Counting how many words appear on each page
Ignoring the words the author actually used
Considering why the author chose specific words and what effect they create

quiz.q: Why is it useful for readers to notice an author’s tone?
options: It reveals the author’s attitude toward the subject or characters
Tone has no connection to how a text is understood
Tone only matters in poetry, never in prose
A reason unrelated to reading comprehension

quiz.q: Which is an example of analyzing an author’s style?
options: Ignoring how the text is written altogether
A concept unrelated to author’s style
Counting the total number of chapters in a book
Noticing that an author uses a lot of vivid, descriptive language

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Adding and Subtracting Integers
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: integers include positive and negative whole numbers, and adding or subtracting them can be modelled on a number line.

quiz.q: An integer can be ___.
options: A positive or negative whole number
Only a fraction or decimal
Only a positive whole number
A concept unrelated to integers

quiz.q: What is -3 + 5?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is 4 + (-6)?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is -2 - 3?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: On a number line, adding a negative integer means moving ___.
options: Straight up
To the left
A direction unrelated to integers
To the right

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Structures: Types of Bridges and Load Distribution
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: different bridge designs, such as beam, arch, and suspension bridges, distribute the load (weight and forces) in different ways to stay stable.

quiz.q: Load distribution in a bridge refers to how a structure ___.
options: Adds unnecessary weight to itself
A concept unrelated to structures
Spreads out weight and forces to remain stable
Ignores all forces acting on it

quiz.q: A beam bridge is best described as ___.
options: A bridge with no support at all
A concept unrelated to bridge design
A bridge that hangs entirely from cables
A simple, rigid structure supported at both ends

quiz.q: An arch bridge distributes weight by ___.
options: Hanging the roadway from tall towers
A concept unrelated to bridge design
Removing all weight from the structure
Curving the load outward and down into its supports

quiz.q: A suspension bridge uses ___ to help support its load.
options: A concept unrelated to bridge design
No support structures of any kind
Cables and towers
Only a single wooden beam

quiz.q: Why do engineers test different bridge designs before building them?
options: To find out which design distributes load safely and stays stable
Testing designs provides no useful information
A reason unrelated to engineering
Bridges never need to support any weight

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Residential Schools and Their Legacy
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: residential schools were institutions in Canada that separated Indigenous children from their families and cultures, and their legacy continues to affect Indigenous communities today.

quiz.q: Residential schools were institutions that ___.
options: Separated Indigenous children from their families and cultures
A concept unrelated to Canadian history
Celebrated and protected Indigenous languages and traditions
Had no lasting impact on Indigenous communities

quiz.q: Why is it important for Canadians to learn about residential schools?
options: Learning about the past never helps understand the present
A reason unrelated to Canadian history
It helps build understanding of this history and supports reconciliation
This history has no relevance to Canada today

quiz.q: What does “legacy” mean in the context of residential schools?
options: An event that had no effect on the future
A concept unrelated to history
The lasting effects that continue to be felt today
A term unrelated to schools of any kind

quiz.q: Which of these is a way Canadians can learn more about this history?
options: A concept unrelated to reconciliation
Assuming this history has no connection to the present
Ignoring this part of Canadian history entirely
Listening to the experiences shared by residential school survivors

quiz.q: Why is understanding this history connected to the idea of reconciliation?
options: Reconciliation has no connection to understanding history
Acknowledging past harms is an important step toward healing and better relationships
A reason unrelated to Indigenous history
The past has no bearing on relationships in the present

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Parallel Structure in Sentences
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: parallel structure means using the same grammatical pattern for items in a list or comparison, such as “I like reading, writing, and drawing.”

quiz.q: Parallel structure means using ___.
options: A different grammatical pattern for every item in a sentence
A concept unrelated to grammar
The same grammatical pattern for items in a list or comparison
No consistent pattern at all

quiz.q: Which sentence uses correct parallel structure?
options: She likes hiking, swims, and to bike.
She likes hiking, swimming, and biking.
A sentence unrelated to parallel structure
She likes hiking, to swim, and biking.

quiz.q: Which sentence uses correct parallel structure?
options: He wanted to run, jumping, and climb.
He wanted running, to jump, and climbed.
A sentence unrelated to parallel structure
He wanted to run, jump, and climb.

quiz.q: Why is parallel structure important in writing?
options: It only matters in poetry, never in prose
A concept unrelated to clear writing
It has no effect on how a sentence reads
It makes sentences clearer and easier to read

quiz.q: Which phrase breaks parallel structure?
options: A phrase unrelated to parallel structure
“swimming, biking, and running”
“to swim, to bike, and to run”
“to swim, biking, and to run”

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Scale Drawings and Scale Factor
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: a scale drawing represents a real object at a smaller or larger size, and the scale factor tells how much the drawing has been reduced or enlarged.

quiz.q: A scale drawing represents a real object ___.
options: With no connection to the real object at all
At exactly the same size with no changes
A concept unrelated to scale drawings
At a smaller or larger size while keeping proportions the same

quiz.q: If a scale factor is 1:100, one unit on the drawing represents ___ units in real life.
options: A number unrelated to the scale factor

quiz.q: On a map with a scale of 1 cm = 5 km, how many kilometres does 3 cm represent?
options: 8 km
15 km
A distance unrelated to the calculation
5 km

quiz.q: Why are scale drawings useful for architects and mapmakers?
options: They allow large objects or areas to be represented accurately on paper
Scale drawings provide no useful information
A reason unrelated to scale drawings
They are only used for decoration, not measurement

quiz.q: A scale factor greater than 1 means the drawing is ___ the real object.
options: Exactly the same size as
Larger than
A comparison unrelated to scale factor
Smaller than

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Immune System and Fighting Illness
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: the immune system is the body’s defence network, using white blood cells and other tools to fight off germs and illness.

quiz.q: The immune system’s main job is to ___.
options: Defend the body against germs and illness
A concept unrelated to the immune system
Digest food for energy
Pump blood throughout the body

quiz.q: White blood cells help the body by ___.
options: Fighting off harmful germs
Breaking down food in the stomach
Carrying oxygen throughout the body
A function unrelated to white blood cells

quiz.q: Which is an example of something that can trigger an immune response?
options: A concept unrelated to the immune system
Taking a short nap
Drinking a glass of water
A virus entering the body

quiz.q: Why are vaccines useful for the immune system?
options: Vaccines have no connection to the immune system
A reason unrelated to immunity
They help the body learn to recognize and fight specific germs
Vaccines remove the immune system’s ability to function

quiz.q: Why might a fever occur when the body is fighting an infection?
options: A fever has no connection to the immune system
A reason unrelated to illness
It can be part of the immune system’s response to fight off germs
Fevers only occur when a person is completely healthy

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada’s Peacekeeping Role Internationally
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada has historically contributed peacekeeping forces to United Nations missions aimed at maintaining peace and stability in conflict areas around the world.

quiz.q: Peacekeeping generally involves ___.
options: Ignoring conflicts happening around the world
Helping maintain peace and stability in areas affected by conflict
A concept unrelated to international relations
Starting new conflicts between countries

quiz.q: Canada has historically contributed peacekeeping forces to missions organized by ___.
options: No international organization at all
A group unrelated to peacekeeping
The United Nations
Only its own domestic government

quiz.q: Why might a country choose to participate in international peacekeeping?
options: Participating in peacekeeping serves no useful purpose
To help support global stability and assist regions affected by conflict
A reason unrelated to international cooperation
Countries are required to participate with no choice involved

quiz.q: Which of these best describes a peacekeeping mission?
options: A mission with no connection to conflict or stability
International forces helping maintain a ceasefire or stability in a region
A group of soldiers sent to start a new war
A concept unrelated to peacekeeping

quiz.q: Why is Canada’s peacekeeping history often discussed as part of its national identity?
options: It reflects a long-standing value Canada has placed on international cooperation
A reason unrelated to Canadian history
Peacekeeping has no connection to Canadian identity
Canada has never taken part in any international missions

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Writing a Personal Essay
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: a personal essay explores the writer’s own thoughts, experiences, or opinions on a topic, often blending storytelling with reflection.

quiz.q: A personal essay explores ___.
options: A concept unrelated to writing
A topic the writer has no connection to
Only facts with no personal reflection at all
The writer’s own thoughts, experiences, or opinions

quiz.q: A personal essay often blends storytelling with ___.
options: Reflection on what the experience meant to the writer
Instructions for completing a task
No reflection of any kind
A concept unrelated to personal essays

quiz.q: Which is an example of a good topic for a personal essay?
options: A set of directions for baking a cake
A concept unrelated to personal essays
A challenge you overcame and what you learned from it
A topic with no connection to your own life

quiz.q: Why might a writer choose to write in first person for a personal essay?
options: First person makes an essay less personal
A reason unrelated to personal essays
First person is never used in personal essays
It reflects the writer’s own perspective and experience

quiz.q: What is one key difference between a personal essay and a research report?
options: A concept unrelated to these two writing forms
A research report is always written in first person
A personal essay focuses on the writer’s own experience and reflection
A personal essay never includes the writer’s own thoughts

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Surface Area of Rectangular Prisms
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: the surface area of a rectangular prism is the total area of all six faces, found by calculating and adding the area of each face.

quiz.q: The surface area of a rectangular prism is ___.
options: The total area of all six faces added together
A concept unrelated to surface area
The length of just one edge
The volume of the prism

quiz.q: How many faces does a rectangular prism have?
options: A number unrelated to rectangular prisms

quiz.q: What is the area of one face of a rectangular prism measuring 4 by 3?
options: 24 square units
7 square units
A value unrelated to the calculation
12 square units

quiz.q: Surface area is measured in ___.
options: Linear units
Cubic units
A unit unrelated to surface area
Square units

quiz.q: Why might someone need to calculate surface area in real life?
options: To measure how much a box can hold inside it
Surface area has no real-world use
To determine how much material is needed to cover or wrap an object
A reason unrelated to surface area

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Pulleys and Gear Ratios
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: pulleys use a wheel and rope to change the direction or amount of force needed to lift a load, and gear ratios describe how connected gears affect speed and force.

quiz.q: A pulley changes the ___ needed to lift a load.
options: A concept unrelated to pulleys
Weight of the load itself
Direction or amount of force
Colour of the load

quiz.q: A gear ratio describes the relationship between ___.
options: The number of teeth on two connected gears
The colour of two connected gears
A concept unrelated to gears
The temperature of a machine’s parts

quiz.q: Why might a system use multiple pulleys together?
options: To reduce the amount of force needed to lift a heavy load
A reason unrelated to pulleys
Multiple pulleys serve no mechanical purpose
Multiple pulleys always make lifting a load harder

quiz.q: If a small gear turns a much larger gear, the larger gear will generally turn ___.
options: At exactly the same speed
More quickly
More slowly
A description unrelated to gear ratios

quiz.q: Why are pulleys and gears useful in everyday machines?
options: A reason unrelated to simple machines
They make every task more difficult to complete
They have no practical use in real machines
They make lifting, moving, or turning objects easier or more efficient

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Métis Nation and the Red River Resistance
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: the Métis Nation, formed from Indigenous and European ancestry, played a key role in the Red River Resistance, led by Louis Riel, to defend Métis rights and land.

quiz.q: The Métis Nation formed from a blending of ___.
options: Only European settlers with no Indigenous connection
Only Indigenous peoples with no European connection
Indigenous and European ancestry and culture
A concept unrelated to the Métis Nation

quiz.q: The Red River Resistance was primarily focused on ___.
options: Establishing a new international trade agreement
A concept unrelated to the Red River Resistance
Defending Métis rights and land
Building new railways across the country

quiz.q: Who was a key leader of the Red River Resistance?
options: A person unrelated to the Red River Resistance
A leader from a completely different country
A leader with no connection to Métis history
Louis Riel

quiz.q: Why is the Red River Resistance an important event in Canadian history?
options: It highlights the Métis Nation’s efforts to protect their rights and way of life
It had no lasting effect on Canada’s development
This event has no significance in Canadian history
A reason unrelated to Métis history

quiz.q: Why is it valuable for students to learn about the Métis Nation’s history?
options: A reason unrelated to social studies learning
It builds a fuller understanding of the diverse peoples who shaped Canada
The Métis Nation has no connection to Canadian history
Only one group’s history is relevant to understanding Canada

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Vocabulary: Analogies
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: an analogy compares two pairs of words that share a similar relationship, such as “hot is to cold as up is to down.”

quiz.q: An analogy compares ___.
options: Two completely unrelated words with no connection
Only the spelling of two words
A concept unrelated to vocabulary
Two pairs of words that share a similar relationship

quiz.q: Complete the analogy: Bird is to nest as bee is to ___.
options: A word unrelated to the analogy
Sky
Wing
Hive

quiz.q: Complete the analogy: Author is to book as painter is to ___.
options: A word unrelated to the analogy
Museum
Brush
Painting

quiz.q: What relationship does the analogy “up is to down as hot is to cold” show?
options: Synonyms
Opposites
A relationship unrelated to analogies
Items from the same category

quiz.q: Why are analogies a useful vocabulary tool?
options: Analogies only apply to numbers, not words
They help readers understand relationships between words and ideas
Analogies never help with understanding vocabulary
A concept unrelated to vocabulary building

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Probability: Theoretical vs. Experimental
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: theoretical probability predicts an outcome based on possible results, while experimental probability is based on the actual results of repeated trials.

quiz.q: Theoretical probability is based on ___.
options: A random guess with no calculation involved
Only the results of trials that have already happened
A concept unrelated to probability
What is possible, calculated before any trials happen

quiz.q: Experimental probability is based on ___.
options: The actual results of repeated trials
A concept unrelated to probability
A number with no connection to any trials
What is possible before any trials happen

quiz.q: What is the theoretical probability of flipping heads on a fair coin?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: If you flip a coin 20 times and get heads 12 times, what is the experimental probability of heads?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why might experimental probability differ from theoretical probability?
options: Real trials can vary due to chance, especially with a small number of trials
A reason unrelated to probability
Experimental and theoretical probability are always identical
Experimental probability never involves any real trials

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Climate vs. Weather
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: weather describes short-term atmospheric conditions like today’s temperature or rain, while climate describes the average weather patterns of a region over a long period of time.

quiz.q: Weather describes ___.
options: A concept found only in science fiction
The average conditions of a region over many years
Short-term atmospheric conditions, such as today’s temperature
A concept unrelated to weather

quiz.q: Climate describes ___.
options: The average weather patterns of a region over a long period of time
A single day’s temperature and rainfall
Only the weather happening right now
A concept unrelated to climate

quiz.q: Which statement is an example of describing weather?
options: A statement unrelated to weather or climate
This region has cold winters and warm summers on average.
This area has a desert climate.
It is raining outside today.

quiz.q: Which statement is an example of describing climate?
options: This region typically has hot, dry summers.
It is sunny outside right now.
The temperature today is 22 degrees.
A statement unrelated to weather or climate

quiz.q: Why is it important to understand the difference between weather and climate?
options: A reason unrelated to science
This distinction has no practical use
Weather and climate are always exactly the same thing
It helps people make sense of both short-term forecasts and long-term environmental patterns

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada’s Supreme Court and Landmark Cases
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: the Supreme Court of Canada is the highest court in the country, and its landmark cases have shaped Canadian law and rights over time.

quiz.q: The Supreme Court of Canada is ___.
options: The highest court in the country
A concept unrelated to Canadian government
A court found only at the municipal level
A court that only handles local disputes

quiz.q: A landmark case is one that ___.
options: A concept unrelated to the legal system
Has no lasting effect on Canadian law
Sets an important precedent that shapes future law
Is always immediately forgotten after the ruling

quiz.q: Why is the Supreme Court considered the highest court in Canada?
options: Its decisions are final and cannot be appealed to a higher court
Its decisions can always be overturned by any other court
It only handles cases involving traffic violations
A reason unrelated to Canada’s court system

quiz.q: How can a Supreme Court ruling affect all of Canada?
options: Supreme Court rulings only apply to a single province
It can set a legal precedent that applies across the whole country
A reason unrelated to how courts function
Rulings from the Supreme Court have no effect on Canadian law

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to learn about landmark court cases?
options: It helps them understand how laws and rights have developed over time
Court cases have no connection to how rights are protected
A reason unrelated to social studies learning
Landmark cases provide no useful information about Canadian history

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Understanding Foreshadowing
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: foreshadowing is a writing technique where an author hints at events that will happen later in a story.

quiz.q: Foreshadowing is a technique where an author ___.
options: A concept unrelated to reading
Removes all clues about what might happen next
Reveals the entire ending at the very beginning
Hints at events that will happen later in the story

quiz.q: Why might an author use foreshadowing?
options: Foreshadowing never affects how a reader experiences a story
A reason unrelated to storytelling
To confuse readers with no clear purpose
To build suspense and prepare readers for upcoming events

quiz.q: Which is an example of foreshadowing?
options: A concept unrelated to foreshadowing
A character noticing dark storm clouds before a disaster occurs later in the story
A story with absolutely no hints about future events
A character simply describing their breakfast

quiz.q: How is foreshadowing different from a flashback?
options: A flashback hints at future events instead
Foreshadowing hints at future events, while a flashback shows past events
A concept unrelated to these techniques
Foreshadowing and flashback mean exactly the same thing

quiz.q: Why might noticing foreshadowing make reading more enjoyable?
options: It can build anticipation and reward careful readers with connections
Noticing foreshadowing never adds anything to the reading experience
A reason unrelated to reading enjoyment
Foreshadowing always reveals the ending immediately

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Algebraic Expressions: Variables and Terms
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: an algebraic expression uses variables (letters representing unknown numbers) and terms to represent a mathematical relationship, such as “3x + 5.”

quiz.q: A variable in an algebraic expression is ___.
options: A concept unrelated to algebra
A letter that represents an unknown number
A symbol with no mathematical meaning
Always a fixed number that never changes

quiz.q: In the expression “3x + 5,” what is the variable?
options: x
A part of the expression unrelated to variables

quiz.q: In the expression “2y + 7,” what does “2y” represent?
options: The value 27
2 multiplied by the variable y
A part unrelated to the expression
2 added to the variable y

quiz.q: If x = 4, what is the value of 3x + 5?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why are algebraic expressions useful in math?
options: They have no practical use in solving problems
They let us represent unknown or changing quantities in a general way
They can only be used with specific, known numbers
A reason unrelated to algebra

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Life Cycles of Plants and Pollination
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: plants go through a life cycle from seed to mature plant, and pollination -- often carried out by insects or wind -- is a key step that allows plants to reproduce.

quiz.q: Pollination is the process by which ___.
options: A seed is planted in the soil
Pollen is transferred to allow a plant to reproduce
A plant absorbs water through its roots
A concept unrelated to plant life cycles

quiz.q: Which of these commonly helps carry out pollination?
options: Only artificial machines
Bees and other insects
Rocks and soil alone
A concept unrelated to pollination

quiz.q: What is the first stage in a plant’s life cycle?
options: A seed
A fruit
A stage unrelated to plant life cycles
A fully grown, flowering plant

quiz.q: Why is pollination important for many plants?
options: It allows plants to produce seeds and reproduce
Pollination only affects the colour of a plant’s leaves
Pollination has no connection to how plants reproduce
A reason unrelated to plant biology

quiz.q: Wind pollination is common in plants that ___.
options: A description unrelated to pollination
Produce large amounts of light, airborne pollen
Rely entirely on insects for reproduction
Never produce any pollen at all

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Globalization and Its Effects on Canada
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: globalization is the increasing connection between countries through trade, technology, and culture, and it has significant effects on Canada’s economy and society.

quiz.q: Globalization refers to ___.
options: The increasing connection between countries through trade, technology, and culture
A process that only affects a single country
A concept unrelated to international relations
Countries becoming completely isolated from one another

quiz.q: Which is an example of globalization affecting Canada?
options: A concept unrelated to globalization
Canada having no trade relationships with any other country
Canada being completely self-sufficient with no imports
Canadians buying products manufactured in other countries

quiz.q: How has technology contributed to globalization?
options: It has made communication and trade between countries faster and easier
Technology has made international communication impossible
A reason unrelated to globalization
Technology has no connection to how countries interact

quiz.q: Which is a potential effect of globalization on Canada’s economy?
options: A concept unrelated to globalization
A complete end to all trade with other countries
Increased trade opportunities with other countries
No effect on Canada’s economy at all

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to understand globalization?
options: It helps explain how connected Canada is to the rest of the world
A reason unrelated to social studies learning
Globalization has no relevance to understanding Canada today
Canada operates with no connection to other countries

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Figurative Language: Alliteration and Assonance
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: alliteration repeats consonant sounds at the start of nearby words, while assonance repeats vowel sounds within nearby words, both creating rhythm and emphasis.

quiz.q: Alliteration repeats ___.
options: The same word over and over with no variation
A concept unrelated to figurative language
Consonant sounds at the start of nearby words
Vowel sounds within nearby words

quiz.q: Assonance repeats ___.
options: Consonant sounds at the start of nearby words
A concept unrelated to figurative language
Only the final letter of a word
Vowel sounds within nearby words

quiz.q: Which phrase is an example of alliteration?
options: A phrase unrelated to alliteration
The cat sat on the mat.
She walked to the store.
Sally sells seashells by the seashore.

quiz.q: Which phrase is an example of assonance?
options: The dog ran quickly down the street.
A phrase unrelated to assonance
The rain in Spain falls mainly on the plain.
Peter Piper picked a peck.

quiz.q: Why might a poet use alliteration or assonance?
options: A reason unrelated to figurative language
To make a poem harder to read aloud
To create rhythm, emphasis, or a musical quality in the writing
These techniques never affect how a poem sounds

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Comparing and Ordering Integers
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: integers can be compared and ordered on a number line, where numbers further to the right are greater than numbers further to the left.

quiz.q: On a number line, a number further to the right is ___.
options: A concept unrelated to comparing integers
Exactly equal to every other number
Greater than a number to its left
Always smaller than a number to its left

quiz.q: Which integer is greater: -3 or 2?
options: They are equal
A value unrelated to the comparison

quiz.q: Which integer is less: -8 or -2?
options: A value unrelated to the comparison
They are equal

quiz.q: Order these integers from least to greatest: 3, -5, 0, -1.
options: An order unrelated to these integers

quiz.q: Why is understanding negative numbers useful in real life?
options: Negative numbers have no real-world use
Negative numbers are only used in advanced mathematics
A reason unrelated to integers
They can represent things like temperatures below zero or amounts owed

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Static Electricity and Charge
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: static electricity occurs when electric charges build up on the surface of an object, often from friction, and can cause objects to attract or repel each other.

quiz.q: Static electricity often builds up because of ___.
options: A concept unrelated to static electricity
The temperature of the air alone
Friction between two objects
Water flowing through a pipe

quiz.q: Objects with the same type of electric charge will ___ each other.
options: Attract
Have no effect on
Repel
An interaction unrelated to charge

quiz.q: Objects with opposite electric charges will ___ each other.
options: Attract
An interaction unrelated to charge
Repel
Have no effect on

quiz.q: Which is an everyday example of static electricity?
options: Water boiling in a kettle
A balloon sticking to a wall after being rubbed on hair
A concept unrelated to static electricity
A magnet attracting metal from across a room

quiz.q: Why might your hair stand up after rubbing a balloon on it?
options: The balloon removes all charge from the hair
A reason unrelated to static electricity
Hair naturally stands up with no connection to electric charge
The balloon transfers a static charge that causes the hair strands to repel each other

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Population Distribution and Density in Canada
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada’s population is unevenly distributed, with most people living in southern regions near the border, resulting in varying population density across the country.

quiz.q: Population density refers to ___.
options: A concept unrelated to population
The total land area of a country
The number of people living in a given area
The number of cities in a country

quiz.q: Most of Canada’s population lives ___.
options: Equally spread across every region of the country
A location unrelated to population distribution
In southern regions, close to the border with the United States
In the far northern Arctic regions

quiz.q: Why might a region with a mild climate and good farmland have a higher population density?
options: Climate and farmland have no effect on where people choose to live
It can better support agriculture and comfortable living conditions
Population is distributed with no connection to geography
A reason unrelated to population distribution

quiz.q: Which of these would likely have a lower population density?
options: A concept unrelated to population density
A large southern city
A densely built urban downtown
A remote northern territory

quiz.q: Why is it useful to study population distribution across Canada?
options: A reason unrelated to geography
Every region of Canada has an identical population
Population distribution provides no useful information
It helps explain patterns in where services, cities, and resources are located

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Writing a Literary Response
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: a literary response shares the writer’s thoughts and reactions to a text, supported by specific evidence and examples from the reading.

quiz.q: A literary response shares ___.
options: The writer’s thoughts and reactions to a text, supported by evidence
A concept unrelated to writing
Only a summary of the plot with no personal reaction
A completely new, unrelated story

quiz.q: Why is it important to include evidence in a literary response?
options: A reason unrelated to writing a literary response
Evidence is never needed when responding to a text
Evidence makes a response less convincing
It supports the writer’s ideas with specific details from the text

quiz.q: Which is an example of evidence a writer might use in a literary response?
options: A concept unrelated to literary responses
A specific quote or event from the story
The writer’s opinion with no connection to the text
A completely unrelated fact about a different book

quiz.q: What might a literary response include besides a summary of events?
options: The writer’s personal reaction and interpretation of the text
Only a list of characters with no further detail
A concept unrelated to literary responses
Instructions for how to read the book

quiz.q: Why might two readers write very different literary responses to the same book?
options: Literary responses never include any personal interpretation
Readers can have different reactions, interpretations, and connections to a text
All readers are required to respond to a text in exactly the same way
A reason unrelated to literary responses

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Converting Between Fractions, Decimals, and Percents
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: fractions, decimals, and percents are different ways to represent the same value, and converting between them uses division, multiplication, or place value.

quiz.q: What is 1/2 written as a decimal?
options: A value unrelated to the conversion

quiz.q: What is 0.75 written as a percent?
options: A value unrelated to the conversion

quiz.q: What is 3/4 written as a percent?
options: A value unrelated to the conversion

quiz.q: To convert a decimal to a percent, you ___.
options: A method unrelated to converting decimals
Add 100 to the decimal
Multiply by 100
Divide by 100

quiz.q: What is 25% written as a fraction in lowest terms?
options: A fraction unrelated to the conversion

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Natural Disasters and Their Causes
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: natural disasters such as earthquakes, floods, and tornadoes are caused by natural processes in the Earth’s systems, including shifting tectonic plates and extreme weather.

quiz.q: An earthquake is often caused by ___.
options: A sudden change in air temperature
Shifting tectonic plates beneath the Earth’s surface
A concept unrelated to earthquakes
The movement of ocean currents alone

quiz.q: A flood can be caused by ___.
options: A lack of rainfall over a long period
Excessive rainfall or rapid snowmelt
A sudden drop in temperature
A concept unrelated to floods

quiz.q: A tornado forms when ___.
options: Warm and cold air masses collide, creating strong rotating winds
The ground shifts suddenly beneath the surface
Ocean water rises unusually high
A concept unrelated to tornadoes

quiz.q: Why do scientists study natural disasters?
options: A reason unrelated to science
Natural disasters cannot be studied or understood
Studying natural disasters provides no useful information
To better predict them and help keep communities safe

quiz.q: Which of these is a way communities can prepare for natural disasters?
options: Ignoring the possibility of a natural disaster entirely
Creating emergency plans and warning systems
A concept unrelated to disaster preparedness
Assuming natural disasters never occur

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada’s Relationship with the United States
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada and the United States share the world’s longest international border and have close economic, cultural, and political ties.

quiz.q: Canada and the United States share ___.
options: The world’s longest international border
A concept unrelated to their relationship
No border at all
A border that is closed to all trade

quiz.q: Which is an example of the close economic ties between Canada and the United States?
options: Canada trading only with countries outside North America
A complete absence of any trade between the two countries
A concept unrelated to their relationship
A large amount of trade in goods and services between the two countries

quiz.q: Why might close cultural ties exist between Canada and the United States?
options: The two countries share similar media, language, and geographic closeness
Canada and the United States have never interacted culturally
A reason unrelated to their relationship
The two countries have no shared culture or media of any kind

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of political cooperation between the two countries?
options: Having completely separate, unconnected governments with no interaction
Working together on shared border and trade agreements
A concept unrelated to their relationship
Refusing to communicate on any shared issues

quiz.q: Why is Canada’s relationship with the United States significant for Canadians?
options: Canada and the United States operate with no connection to one another
A reason unrelated to Canadian geography
It affects trade, travel, and many parts of daily life due to their close connection
This relationship has no effect on Canadians’ daily lives

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Evaluating Arguments and Counterarguments
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: evaluating an argument means judging whether the reasons and evidence support the claim, while a counterargument presents an opposing point of view.

quiz.q: Evaluating an argument means judging whether ___.
options: The argument is written in complete sentences
The text is a certain length
A concept unrelated to reading
The reasons and evidence actually support the claim

quiz.q: A counterargument presents ___.
options: A concept unrelated to argumentation
A summary of the entire text
The exact same point of view as the main argument
An opposing point of view to the main argument

quiz.q: Why might a writer include a counterargument in a persuasive text?
options: To show they have considered other perspectives and strengthen their own argument
Counterarguments always weaken a writer’s position
A reason unrelated to persuasive writing
Counterarguments are never included in persuasive writing

quiz.q: What should a reader look for when evaluating whether an argument is strong?
options: Whether the argument uses the most exciting language
A concept unrelated to evaluating arguments
The number of paragraphs in the text
Solid evidence and logical reasoning that supports the claim

quiz.q: Which is an example of evaluating an argument critically?
options: Believing every claim in a text without question
Checking whether the evidence used actually supports the claim being made
A concept unrelated to critical reading
Ignoring the reasons given for a claim entirely

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Line Plots and Stem-and-Leaf Plots
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: a line plot displays data using marks above a number line, while a stem-and-leaf plot organizes numerical data by splitting each value into a “stem” and a “leaf.”

quiz.q: A line plot displays data using ___.
options: Bars of different heights only
Slices of a circle
A concept unrelated to line plots
Marks placed above a number line

quiz.q: In a stem-and-leaf plot, the “stem” usually represents ___.
options: The exact value of a single data point
The total number of data points
The leading digit(s) of a number
A concept unrelated to stem-and-leaf plots

quiz.q: In a stem-and-leaf plot, the “leaf” usually represents ___.
options: The total sum of all data points
The last digit of a number
A concept unrelated to stem-and-leaf plots
The leading digit(s) of a number

quiz.q: Why might someone use a line plot to display data?
options: Line plots only work with non-numerical data
It shows how often each value occurs in a simple, visual way
A reason unrelated to graphing data
Line plots cannot display any type of data

quiz.q: In the stem-and-leaf entry “3 | 4,” what value does this represent?
options: A value unrelated to the plot

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Structures: Materials Engineering and Testing
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: engineers test different materials for strength, flexibility, and durability to determine the best choice for building a stable structure.

quiz.q: Materials engineering involves testing materials for qualities such as ___.
options: Their price alone, with no other testing
Strength, flexibility, and durability
Only their colour and appearance
A concept unrelated to engineering

quiz.q: Why might an engineer test a material’s flexibility before using it in a structure?
options: Flexibility has no connection to how a structure performs
To see whether it can bend without breaking under stress
Flexibility testing is never done in engineering
A reason unrelated to materials engineering

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of testing a material’s strength?
options: Measuring the temperature of the room
Painting a beam a different colour
A concept unrelated to materials testing
Applying weight to a beam to see how much it can hold before bending

quiz.q: Why is durability an important quality for building materials?
options: Materials never need to withstand any wear over time
Durable materials can withstand wear and last longer over time
A reason unrelated to materials engineering
Durability has no connection to how well a structure lasts

quiz.q: Why do engineers compare multiple materials before choosing one for a project?
options: A reason unrelated to engineering design
Only one material has ever been used to build anything
Comparing materials provides no useful information
To select the material best suited to the structure’s needs and conditions

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Land Acknowledgements and Treaty Territories
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: a land acknowledgement recognizes the Indigenous peoples who have traditionally lived on and cared for a particular territory, often defined by historic treaties.

quiz.q: A land acknowledgement recognizes ___.
options: A country’s entire history, with no focus on any specific group
The Indigenous peoples who have traditionally lived on a territory
A concept unrelated to Indigenous history
A modern boundary with no historical significance

quiz.q: A treaty territory is often defined by ___.
options: A concept unrelated to treaties
A boundary drawn with no historical basis
A historic agreement between Indigenous nations and the government
A region that has never had any agreements in place

quiz.q: Why might a school or organization begin an event with a land acknowledgement?
options: To ignore the history of the land entirely
A reason unrelated to Indigenous history
To show respect for the Indigenous peoples connected to that land
Land acknowledgements have no purpose or meaning

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to learn about the treaty territory they live on?
options: A reason unrelated to social studies learning
This knowledge has no relevance to understanding local history
It builds understanding of the land’s history and the people connected to it
Treaty territories have no connection to where people live today

quiz.q: What is one purpose of historic treaties between Indigenous nations and governments?
options: Treaties exist with no real purpose or effect
A reason unrelated to treaties
Treaties have never included any agreements about land
To establish agreements about land, rights, and relationships

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Writing an Effective Conclusion
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: an effective conclusion sums up the main ideas of a piece of writing and leaves the reader with a final thought, without simply repeating the introduction word for word.

quiz.q: An effective conclusion should ___.
options: Introduce a completely new topic with no connection to the writing
A concept unrelated to writing
Sum up the main ideas and leave the reader with a final thought
Repeat the introduction word for word

quiz.q: Why should a conclusion avoid simply repeating the introduction?
options: Conclusions are never meant to add anything new
A strong conclusion should add a sense of closure or new insight
Repeating the introduction always makes a conclusion stronger
A reason unrelated to writing conclusions

quiz.q: Which is an example of a strong closing thought in a conclusion?
options: A random change of topic
A concept unrelated to conclusions
A reflection on why the topic matters to the reader
A brand new fact with no connection to the essay

quiz.q: Why is a strong conclusion important in a piece of writing?
options: It leaves a lasting impression and reinforces the writer’s main point
A conclusion has no effect on how a piece of writing is remembered
A reason unrelated to writing structure
Conclusions are an optional part of writing that can be skipped

quiz.q: Which of these belongs in a conclusion rather than an introduction?
options: A hook meant to grab the reader’s attention at the start
The very first sentence that introduces the topic
A final thought that ties the main ideas together
A concept unrelated to conclusions

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Multiplying and Dividing Integers
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: when multiplying or dividing integers, two numbers with the same sign give a positive result, while two numbers with different signs give a negative result.

quiz.q: What is -3 × 4?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is -6 × -2?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is -10 ÷ 2?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is -12 ÷ -4?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: When multiplying two integers with different signs, the result is ___.
options: Negative
Always zero
Positive
A result unrelated to integer multiplication

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Carbon Cycle
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: the carbon cycle describes how carbon moves between the atmosphere, living things, and the Earth, through processes like photosynthesis, respiration, and decomposition.

quiz.q: The carbon cycle describes how carbon moves between ___.
options: Only the ocean, with no other locations involved
A concept unrelated to the carbon cycle
Nowhere at all, since carbon never moves
The atmosphere, living things, and the Earth

quiz.q: During photosynthesis, plants take in ___ from the atmosphere.
options: A gas unrelated to photosynthesis
Nitrogen only
Oxygen only
Carbon dioxide

quiz.q: During respiration, living things release ___ back into the atmosphere.
options: Carbon dioxide
Nitrogen only
Oxygen only
A gas unrelated to respiration

quiz.q: Decomposition contributes to the carbon cycle by ___.
options: Having no connection to carbon at all
Removing all carbon permanently from the environment
Releasing carbon stored in dead plants and animals back into the environment
A process unrelated to the carbon cycle

quiz.q: Why is understanding the carbon cycle important?
options: Carbon never plays a role in supporting life on Earth
It helps explain how carbon supports life and connects to environmental issues like climate change
A reason unrelated to science
The carbon cycle has no connection to living things or the environment

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Municipal vs. Provincial Elections
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: municipal elections choose local leaders like mayors and councillors, while provincial elections choose representatives who make decisions for the whole province.

quiz.q: A municipal election chooses leaders who make decisions for ___.
options: Every province in Canada at once
The entire country
A concept unrelated to elections
A local community or city

quiz.q: A provincial election chooses representatives who make decisions for ___.
options: A single neighbourhood only
The whole province
Every country in the world
A concept unrelated to elections

quiz.q: Which of these would most likely be decided in a municipal election?
options: Who will serve as the city’s mayor
A concept unrelated to municipal elections
Who will serve as the country’s prime minister
Who will represent Canada internationally

quiz.q: Which of these would most likely be decided in a provincial election?
options: Who will lead a local school council
A concept unrelated to provincial elections
Who will be the mayor of a single town
Who will represent a riding in the provincial legislature

quiz.q: Why is it useful to understand the difference between municipal and provincial elections?
options: Elections have no connection to how decisions are made
These two types of elections are always identical in every way
It helps citizens understand which level of government handles which responsibilities
A reason unrelated to understanding government

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Semicolons and Colons
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: a semicolon can join two related independent clauses, while a colon is often used to introduce a list, explanation, or quotation.

quiz.q: A semicolon can be used to ___.
options: Join two closely related independent clauses
A concept unrelated to punctuation
Separate every word in a sentence
Replace a period at the end of every sentence

quiz.q: A colon is often used to ___.
options: Replace a comma in every sentence
End a question
Introduce a list, explanation, or quotation
A concept unrelated to punctuation

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly uses a semicolon?
options: I love reading, it helps me relax.
I love reading; it helps me relax.
A sentence unrelated to semicolon use
I love reading; And it helps me relax.

quiz.q: Which sentence correctly uses a colon?
options: A sentence unrelated to colon use
Bring these items, a pencil, a notebook, and a ruler.
Bring these items: a pencil, a notebook, and a ruler.
Bring these items; a pencil, a notebook, and a ruler.

quiz.q: Why might a writer choose a semicolon instead of starting a new sentence?
options: Semicolons are never used to connect ideas
A reason unrelated to punctuation
To show that two ideas are closely connected
A semicolon always separates completely unrelated ideas

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Review: Integers, Algebra, and Data Displays
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: this review lesson revisits key ideas from Days 51-60, including integers, scale drawings, surface area, algebraic expressions, and stem-and-leaf plots.

quiz.q: An integer can be ___.
options: Only a positive whole number
A concept unrelated to integers
A positive or negative whole number
Only a fraction or decimal

quiz.q: A variable in an algebraic expression is ___.
options: A symbol with no mathematical meaning
A letter that represents an unknown number
Always a fixed number that never changes
A concept unrelated to algebra

quiz.q: The surface area of a rectangular prism is ___.
options: The volume of the prism
The total area of all six faces added together
A concept unrelated to surface area
The length of just one edge

quiz.q: In a stem-and-leaf plot, the “leaf” usually represents ___.
options: The last digit of a number
The leading digit(s) of a number
A concept unrelated to stem-and-leaf plots
The total sum of all data points

quiz.q: Why is it useful to review integers, algebra, and data displays together?
options: It reinforces how these math concepts connect and build on one another
Review never helps strengthen understanding of a subject
A reason unrelated to reviewing math
These topics have no connection to each other

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Body Systems, Structures, and Earth Processes
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: this review lesson revisits key ideas from Days 51-60, including the immune system, bridges, static electricity, plant pollination, and natural disasters.

quiz.q: The immune system’s main job is to ___.
options: Pump blood throughout the body
Digest food for energy
Defend the body against germs and illness
A concept unrelated to the immune system

quiz.q: Load distribution in a bridge refers to how a structure ___.
options: Ignores all forces acting on it
A concept unrelated to structures
Adds unnecessary weight to itself
Spreads out weight and forces to remain stable

quiz.q: Objects with the same type of electric charge will ___ each other.
options: Repel
An interaction unrelated to charge
Have no effect on
Attract

quiz.q: Pollination is the process by which ___.
options: Pollen is transferred to allow a plant to reproduce
A seed is planted in the soil
A concept unrelated to plant life cycles
A plant absorbs water through its roots

quiz.q: Why is it useful to review body systems, structures, and Earth processes together?
options: Each topic must be studied with no connection to the others
These topics have no connection to each other
It reinforces how these interconnected science concepts relate to one another
Review is never useful in science

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Culminating Task: A Timeline of Canadian Milestones
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: this culminating task asks students to apply Days 51-60 learning about residential schools, peacekeeping, the Métis Nation, and Canadian government to build a timeline of significant milestones.

quiz.q: Why might a student include residential schools on a timeline of Canadian milestones?
options: It represents a significant and difficult part of Canada’s history that continues to affect communities today
Timelines should only include positive events
Residential schools have no connection to Canadian history
A reason unrelated to this unit

quiz.q: Why might a student include the Red River Resistance on a timeline of Canadian milestones?
options: It reflects an important moment when the Métis Nation defended their rights and land
Only events from the last ten years belong on a timeline
A reason unrelated to this unit
This event has no significance in Canadian history

quiz.q: Why might a student include Canada’s peacekeeping missions on a timeline of Canadian milestones?
options: A reason unrelated to this unit
They reflect Canada’s long-standing role in supporting international stability
Peacekeeping has never been part of Canada’s history
Peacekeeping missions have no connection to Canadian history

quiz.q: Why might a student include a Supreme Court landmark case on a timeline of Canadian milestones?
options: The Supreme Court has never influenced Canadian law
A reason unrelated to this unit
Court cases have no lasting effect on Canadian history
Landmark cases can shape Canadian law and rights for the whole country

quiz.q: Why is building a timeline a valuable way to end this set of Social Studies lessons?
options: Timelines never help show connections between historical events
Organizing events chronologically provides no educational benefit
It helps students see how different events connect and build Canada’s history over time
A reason unrelated to social studies learning

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Identifying Bias in a Text
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: bias occurs when a text presents information in a one-sided way, favouring a particular opinion instead of presenting facts fairly.

quiz.q: Bias in a text means the writing ___.
options: Presents information in a one-sided, unfair way
A concept unrelated to reading
Contains only verified factual statements
Presents every side of an issue equally

quiz.q: Which is a clue that a text might be biased?
options: A clue unrelated to identifying bias
It uses emotional language to persuade rather than inform
It cites where each piece of information came from
It includes a balanced mix of evidence from multiple sources

quiz.q: Why is it useful for readers to notice bias in a text?
options: A reason unrelated to critical reading
Noticing bias never changes how a reader understands a text
It helps readers judge how trustworthy or one-sided the information is
Bias has no effect on how information is presented

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of biased language?
options: Describing an idea using only measured, neutral facts
Listing evidence for and against an idea equally
Describing an idea as “obviously the only sensible choice”
A phrase unrelated to biased language

quiz.q: How can a reader check whether a text is biased?
options: Reading only the title and nothing else
A method unrelated to identifying bias
Assuming every text is completely fair with no need to check
Comparing it with other sources to see if the information is one-sided

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Adding and Subtracting Decimals
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: adding and subtracting decimals requires lining up the decimal points and place values before combining or comparing the digits.

quiz.q: When adding decimals, it is important to first ___.
options: Ignore the decimal points entirely
A step unrelated to adding decimals
Round every number to a whole number
Line up the decimal points

quiz.q: What is 4.5 + 3.2?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is 6.8 - 2.3?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation
A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why might someone need to add decimals in real life?
options: To total up amounts of money that include cents
Adding decimals never applies to real-life situations
A reason unrelated to decimals
Decimals are only used in scientific formulas

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Magnetism and Magnetic Forces
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: magnets create an invisible force field that attracts certain metals and can either pull objects together or push them apart, depending on their poles.

quiz.q: A magnet has two poles, called the ___.
options: Positive pole and negative pole
North pole and south pole
Hot pole and cold pole
A concept unrelated to magnetism

quiz.q: Which of these materials is most strongly attracted to a magnet?
options: A material unrelated to magnetism
Iron
Wood
Plastic

quiz.q: What happens when two like poles of a magnet are brought together?
options: They always attract, or pull together
A concept unrelated to magnetism
They repel, or push apart
They have no effect on each other

quiz.q: What happens when opposite poles of two magnets are brought together?
options: A concept unrelated to magnetism
They attract, or pull together
They have no effect on each other
They repel, or push apart

quiz.q: Which of these is an everyday use of magnets?
options: Measuring the temperature outside
Boiling water for tea
Holding notes on a refrigerator door
A use unrelated to magnetism

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Confederation: The Birth of Canada in 1867
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Confederation in 1867 united several British colonies into the Dominion of Canada, creating a new country with its own federal government.

quiz.q: Confederation in 1867 united several British colonies to form ___.
options: A concept unrelated to Canadian history
The Dominion of Canada
A country called New France
A colony still ruled directly by Britain

quiz.q: Which four provinces joined together at Confederation in 1867?
options: A group unrelated to Confederation
British Columbia, Alberta, Manitoba, and Yukon
Only Ontario and Quebec
Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick

quiz.q: Why did colonies consider joining together at Confederation?
options: A reason unrelated to Confederation
The colonies were forced to join by another country
To gain strength through unity and build a stronger shared economy and defence
Joining together offered no benefits to the colonies

quiz.q: What is the significance of July 1, 1867 in Canadian history?
options: It marks the day Canada became a country through Confederation
It has no connection to Canadian history
It marks the day Canada gained full independence from Britain
A date unrelated to Confederation

quiz.q: Why is Confederation an important milestone for students to study?
options: Confederation had no lasting impact on Canada
It only affected one small region of the country
It marks the founding of Canada as a nation and shaped its government
A reason unrelated to Canadian history

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Prepositional Phrases
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: a prepositional phrase begins with a preposition, such as “in,” “on,” or “under,” and ends with a noun or pronoun, adding detail about location, time, or direction.

quiz.q: A prepositional phrase begins with a ___.
options: Verb
Noun only, with no preposition
Preposition
A part of speech unrelated to prepositional phrases

quiz.q: Which of these is a prepositional phrase?
options: Quickly ran
A phrase unrelated to prepositions
Under the table
The happy dog

quiz.q: In the sentence “The cat slept on the couch,” what is the prepositional phrase?
options: A phrase unrelated to this sentence
The cat slept
Slept on
On the couch

quiz.q: Prepositional phrases often add detail about ___.
options: A concept unrelated to prepositional phrases
The subject’s exact age
Location, time, or direction
Only the main verb of a sentence

quiz.q: Which word is a common preposition?
options: A word unrelated to prepositions
Happiness
Quickly
Beside

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Prime Factorization
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: prime factorization breaks a number down into the prime numbers that multiply together to make it, often shown using a factor tree.

quiz.q: Prime factorization breaks a number down into ___.
options: A list of all its factors, prime or not
A concept unrelated to factorization
The prime numbers that multiply together to make it
Only its largest factor

quiz.q: What is the prime factorization of 12?
options: 2 x 2 x 3
A factorization unrelated to 12

quiz.q: What is the prime factorization of 20?
options: A factorization unrelated to 20
2 x 2 x 5

quiz.q: A factor tree is a tool used to ___.
options: List multiples of a number
Add two numbers together
A tool unrelated to factorization
Break a number down into its prime factors visually

quiz.q: Why is prime factorization a useful math skill?
options: A reason unrelated to factorization
It has no connection to other math skills
It only applies to even numbers
It helps with finding common factors and simplifying fractions

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Layers of the Earth
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: the Earth is made up of several layers -- the crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core -- each with different properties and depths.

quiz.q: The outermost layer of the Earth is called the ___.
options: Crust
Mantle
A layer unrelated to Earth’s structure
Inner core

quiz.q: Which layer of the Earth is closest to the centre?
options: Crust
A layer unrelated to Earth’s structure
Inner core
Mantle

quiz.q: The mantle is located ___.
options: Above the crust
Between the crust and the outer core
A location unrelated to Earth’s structure
Outside the Earth’s atmosphere

quiz.q: Which layer of the Earth is believed to be extremely hot and mostly solid metal?
options: Crust
Inner core
A layer unrelated to Earth’s structure
Mantle

quiz.q: Why do scientists study the layers of the Earth?
options: To better understand processes like earthquakes and volcanic activity
Studying Earth’s layers provides no useful information
A reason unrelated to Earth science
The layers of the Earth have no connection to natural events

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canadian Inventions and Innovators
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canadians have contributed many important inventions and innovations, from basketball to insulin, that have had a lasting impact on the world.

quiz.q: Which of these was invented by a Canadian?
options: Basketball
The bicycle
The telephone book
A concept unrelated to Canadian inventions

quiz.q: Why is it important to study Canadian inventors and innovators?
options: It highlights the contributions Canadians have made to the world
Canadian inventors have made no notable contributions
A reason unrelated to Canadian history
Only inventions from other countries matter

quiz.q: Insulin, a treatment for diabetes, was discovered by a research team working in ___.
options: A country unrelated to this discovery
A country that has never contributed to medicine
Only outside of North America
Canada

quiz.q: Which of these best describes an innovator?
options: A concept unrelated to innovation
Someone with no interest in solving problems
Someone who creates a new idea, product, or way of doing something
Someone who only repeats ideas that already exist

quiz.q: Why might Canadian inventions be a source of national pride?
options: A reason unrelated to Canadian identity
National pride has no connection to accomplishments
They show how Canadians have contributed valuable ideas that benefit others
Inventions have no connection to how a country sees itself

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Writing a Book Review
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: a book review summarizes a text and shares the writer’s opinion of it, supported by specific reasons and examples from the book.

quiz.q: A book review typically includes a summary and ___.
options: The writer’s opinion, supported by reasons and examples
A completely different story with no connection to the book
Only a list of characters with no opinion at all
A concept unrelated to book reviews

quiz.q: Why should a book review include specific examples from the text?
options: Examples make a review harder to understand
Examples are never needed in a book review
They support the writer’s opinion and make the review more convincing
A reason unrelated to writing a review

quiz.q: Which is an example of an opinion a writer might include in a book review?
options: A concept unrelated to book reviews
The pacing of the story felt slow in the middle chapters
The book has exactly two hundred pages
The book was published by a certain company

quiz.q: What is one purpose of writing a book review?
options: To retell the entire story without offering any opinion
To copy the book’s summary from its back cover
To help other readers decide whether they might enjoy the book
A purpose unrelated to book reviews

quiz.q: Why might two readers write very different reviews of the same book?
options: All readers are required to feel the same way about every book
Book reviews never include a personal opinion
A reason unrelated to book reviews
Readers can have different reactions and opinions about the same text

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Introduction to Exponents
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: an exponent shows how many times a number, called the base, is multiplied by itself, such as 2 cubed meaning 2 x 2 x 2.

quiz.q: An exponent tells you ___.
options: How many times a number is multiplied by itself
How many times a number is divided by itself
The sum of a number added to itself
A concept unrelated to exponents

quiz.q: What does 2 to the power of 3 (2 cubed) mean?
options: 2 x 2 x 2
A value unrelated to this expression

quiz.q: What is the value of 2 to the power of 3?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is the value of 5 to the power of 2 (5 squared)?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: In the expression 4 to the power of 2, what is the base?
options: A number unrelated to this expression

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Volcanoes: Formation and Types
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: volcanoes form when molten rock, gas, and ash escape from beneath the Earth’s surface, and different volcano types are shaped by how they erupt.

quiz.q: A volcano forms when molten rock, called magma, escapes from ___.
options: The Earth’s atmosphere
Beneath the Earth’s surface
A concept unrelated to volcanoes
Only the ocean floor

quiz.q: Magma that reaches the Earth’s surface is called ___.
options: Groundwater
Sediment
Lava
A term unrelated to volcanoes

quiz.q: A shield volcano is typically shaped by ___.
options: Slow-flowing lava that spreads out widely
Cold temperatures with no eruption at all
A concept unrelated to volcano formation
Only ash with no lava involved

quiz.q: Why do some volcanoes erupt explosively?
options: Explosive eruptions never involve pressure of any kind
A reason unrelated to volcanic activity
Built-up pressure from trapped gas is released suddenly
Volcanoes only erupt when the weather is cold

quiz.q: Why do scientists monitor active volcanoes closely?
options: To help predict eruptions and protect nearby communities
A reason unrelated to science
Monitoring volcanoes provides no useful information
Volcanoes never pose any risk to nearby areas

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada’s Economy: Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Industries
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada’s economy includes primary industries that gather raw materials, secondary industries that manufacture goods, and tertiary industries that provide services.

quiz.q: A primary industry is one that ___.
options: Only sells finished products in stores
Provides services like teaching or banking
Gathers raw materials, such as mining or farming
A concept unrelated to industries

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of a secondary industry?
options: A farm that grows wheat
A concept unrelated to secondary industries
A factory that manufactures cars from raw materials
A bank that offers financial services

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of a tertiary industry?
options: A mine that extracts minerals from the ground
A hospital that provides healthcare services
A forest where logging takes place
A concept unrelated to tertiary industries

quiz.q: Why does Canada’s economy rely on all three types of industries?
options: The three types of industries have no connection to each other
A reason unrelated to Canada’s economy
Only one type of industry is actually needed
Each type of industry plays a different role in producing goods and services

quiz.q: Why is fishing considered a primary industry in Canada?
options: Fishing always involves manufacturing finished products
Fishing is classified only as a service industry
It involves gathering a natural resource directly from the environment
A reason unrelated to Canada’s economy

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Vocabulary: Prefixes and Suffixes
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: a prefix is added to the beginning of a word to change its meaning, while a suffix is added to the end, and both help readers figure out unfamiliar words.

quiz.q: A prefix is added to the ___ of a word.
options: Beginning
A position unrelated to prefixes
Middle
End

quiz.q: A suffix is added to the ___ of a word.
options: End
Beginning
A position unrelated to suffixes
Middle

quiz.q: What does the prefix “un-” usually mean, as in “unhappy”?
options: Before
A meaning unrelated to this prefix
Not
Again

quiz.q: What does the suffix “-ful” usually mean, as in “helpful”?
options: Before
Without
A meaning unrelated to this suffix
Full of

quiz.q: Why are prefixes and suffixes useful when reading unfamiliar words?
options: They never provide any clues about meaning
They only affect how a word is spelled, not its meaning
They can give clues about a word’s meaning
A reason unrelated to vocabulary

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Divisibility Rules
