subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: divisibility rules are quick tests that show whether a number can be divided evenly by another number, without doing long division.

quiz.q: A number is divisible by 2 if it ___.
options: A rule unrelated to divisibility by 2
Ends in an even digit
Is greater than 100
Ends in an odd digit

quiz.q: A number is divisible by 5 if it ___.
options: A rule unrelated to divisibility by 5
Ends in an even digit
Ends in 0 or 5
Is a prime number

quiz.q: A number is divisible by 3 if ___.
options: It ends in the digit 3
The sum of its digits is divisible by 3
A rule unrelated to divisibility by 3
It is an even number

quiz.q: Is 42 divisible by 3? Use the digit-sum rule to check.
options: Yes, because 4 + 2 = 6, which is divisible by 3
No, because 42 is an odd number
Yes, because 42 ends in 2
A conclusion unrelated to the digit-sum rule

quiz.q: Why are divisibility rules useful?
options: They make it impossible to check divisibility
They help quickly check if a number can be divided evenly without long division
They only work for the number 10
A reason unrelated to divisibility rules

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Types of Pollution and Their Impact
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: pollution includes air, water, and land pollution caused by human activity, and each type can harm living things and the environment.

quiz.q: Air pollution can be caused by ___.
options: A concept unrelated to air pollution
Sunlight reaching the Earth’s surface
Rainfall over a forest
Smoke and exhaust from vehicles and factories

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of water pollution?
options: Fish swimming in clean water
Rain falling into a lake
A concept unrelated to water pollution
Chemicals or waste being released into a river

quiz.q: Land pollution often occurs when ___.
options: Farmers plant new crops in a field
Garbage or harmful substances are dumped onto the ground
A concept unrelated to land pollution
Leaves fall naturally from trees

quiz.q: Why can pollution be harmful to living things?
options: Pollution never has any effect on living things
A reason unrelated to pollution
It can damage habitats and make air, water, or soil unsafe
Pollution only affects things that are not alive

quiz.q: Which of these actions could help reduce pollution?
options: Dumping more garbage into rivers
Reducing waste and recycling materials
A concept unrelated to reducing pollution
Burning more fuel unnecessarily

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Taxes and Government Services
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: taxes are payments collected by governments from citizens and businesses, used to fund public services such as schools, roads, and healthcare.

quiz.q: Taxes are payments collected by governments to fund ___.
options: Only the personal expenses of government leaders
Nothing, since taxes serve no real purpose
Public services such as schools, roads, and healthcare
A concept unrelated to taxes

quiz.q: Which of these is typically funded through taxes?
options: A concept unrelated to public services
Public schools
A family’s personal savings account
A privately owned business

quiz.q: Why might governments at different levels, such as municipal and federal, both collect taxes?
options: Taxes are collected with no connection to government services
Each level of government funds different public services and responsibilities
Only one level of government is ever allowed to collect taxes
A reason unrelated to government funding

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of a public service funded by taxes?
options: A private vacation for one family
A personal savings account
A concept unrelated to tax-funded services
Public libraries

quiz.q: Why is it useful for citizens to understand how taxes are used?
options: A reason unrelated to citizenship
Understanding taxes provides no useful information
It helps them understand how public services are funded and maintained
Taxes have no connection to the services citizens use

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Understanding Flashback
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: a flashback is a writing technique where the story pauses to show an event that happened earlier, before returning to the present moment of the story.

quiz.q: A flashback shows an event that happened ___.
options: At exactly the same moment as the current scene
Earlier in time, before the present moment of the story
A concept unrelated to reading
Later in time, after the story has ended

quiz.q: Why might an author use a flashback?
options: A reason unrelated to storytelling
To give readers important background information about a character or event
To confuse readers with no clear purpose
Flashbacks never provide any useful information to readers

quiz.q: Which is an example of a flashback?
options: A concept unrelated to flashback
A character suddenly remembering a moment from their childhood
A story with no reference to any past events
A character planning something that will happen tomorrow

quiz.q: How is a flashback different from foreshadowing?
options: A flashback shows past events, while foreshadowing hints at future events
Foreshadowing always shows events from the past
A concept unrelated to these techniques
A flashback and foreshadowing mean exactly the same thing

quiz.q: What often signals to a reader that a flashback is beginning?
options: A reason unrelated to flashback
A story ending abruptly with no explanation
A phrase or clue indicating a shift to an earlier time
Flashbacks are never signalled in any way

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Square Numbers and Square Roots
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: a square number is the result of multiplying a whole number by itself, and its square root is the number that was multiplied to produce it.

quiz.q: A square number is the result of ___.
options: Dividing a whole number by itself
Adding a whole number to itself
Multiplying a whole number by itself
A concept unrelated to square numbers

quiz.q: What is 4 squared (4 x 4)?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is the square root of 25?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is the square root of 49?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why are square numbers often shown using a grid or array?
options: Grids have no connection to square numbers
Square numbers can only be shown using a number line
A reason unrelated to square numbers
Because a square number can form a perfectly square grid of dots

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Animal Life Cycles and Metamorphosis
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: many animals go through distinct life cycle stages, and some, such as butterflies and frogs, undergo metamorphosis, a dramatic change in body form.

quiz.q: Metamorphosis is a process in which an animal ___.
options: Stays exactly the same throughout its entire life
Loses all of its offspring immediately after birth
A concept unrelated to animal life cycles
Changes dramatically in body form as it grows

quiz.q: Which of these animals undergoes complete metamorphosis?
options: A human
A butterfly
A dog
A concept unrelated to metamorphosis

quiz.q: What is the correct order of a butterfly’s life cycle?
options: Adult, egg, pupa, larva
Egg, larva, pupa, adult
Pupa, adult, egg, larva
An order unrelated to a butterfly’s life cycle

quiz.q: A tadpole changing into a frog is an example of ___.
options: Hibernation
Metamorphosis
A concept unrelated to amphibians
A life cycle stage that never changes

quiz.q: Why is metamorphosis an important adaptation for some animals?
options: Metamorphosis has no connection to how an animal survives
It can allow the animal to live in different environments or eat different foods at each stage
Metamorphosis prevents an animal from ever changing
A reason unrelated to animal life cycles

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Agriculture and Farming Regions of Canada
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada’s different regions support different types of farming, shaped by climate, soil, and land, from prairie grain farms to Ontario’s fruit orchards.

quiz.q: The Prairie provinces are well known for growing ___.
options: Grain crops such as wheat
Only tropical fruit
A concept unrelated to Prairie agriculture
No crops at all

quiz.q: Why might southern Ontario be suitable for growing fruit such as peaches and grapes?
options: Southern Ontario has no farmland at all
A reason unrelated to agriculture
It has a milder climate and fertile soil suited to fruit growing
Climate and soil have no connection to farming

quiz.q: Which factor most affects what crops can be grown in a Canadian region?
options: A factor unrelated to agriculture
The colour of the provincial flag
The size of the region’s cities
Climate and soil conditions

quiz.q: Why is agriculture an important part of Canada’s economy?
options: Agriculture has no connection to Canada’s economy
A reason unrelated to farming
It provides food and supports jobs across many regions
Farming no longer takes place anywhere in Canada

quiz.q: Why might farming be more limited in Canada’s northern territories?
options: Colder climates and shorter growing seasons make large-scale farming difficult
The north has the best possible climate for farming
A reason unrelated to Canadian geography
Farming is equally easy in every region of Canada

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Figurative Language: Personification and Metaphor
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: personification gives human qualities to something non-human, while a metaphor directly compares two unlike things without using “like” or “as.”

quiz.q: Personification gives ___.
options: A concept unrelated to figurative language
No special qualities to anything at all
Animal qualities to a human being
Human qualities to something non-human

quiz.q: A metaphor compares two unlike things ___.
options: Directly, without using “like” or “as”
Only by using the word “like”
Only by using the word “as”
A concept unrelated to metaphors

quiz.q: Which sentence is an example of personification?
options: The wind whispered through the trees.
A sentence unrelated to personification
The wind blew at ten kilometres per hour.
The tree has green leaves.

quiz.q: Which sentence is an example of a metaphor?
options: Her smile appeared briefly.
Her smile was sunshine on a cloudy day.
Her smile was as bright as sunshine.
A sentence unrelated to metaphors

quiz.q: Why might a writer use personification or metaphor in a story?
options: These techniques never affect how a reader pictures a scene
To create a vivid image or add deeper meaning to the writing
To make the writing more difficult to understand for no reason
A reason unrelated to figurative language

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Data Management: Pictographs
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: a pictograph displays data using pictures or symbols, where each symbol represents a set number of items, along with a key explaining its value.

quiz.q: A pictograph displays data using ___.
options: Pictures or symbols, along with a key
Only bars of different heights
Slices of a circle
A concept unrelated to pictographs

quiz.q: What is the purpose of the key in a pictograph?
options: It explains how many items each symbol represents
It has no useful purpose in a pictograph
It shows the title of the graph only
A concept unrelated to pictographs

quiz.q: If a key shows that one symbol equals 5 items, and a row has 3 symbols, how many items does that represent?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why might a half symbol appear in a pictograph?
options: To represent exactly double the symbol’s usual value
To represent an amount that is half of the symbol’s usual value
Half symbols are never used in pictographs
A reason unrelated to pictographs

quiz.q: Why are pictographs useful for displaying data?
options: Pictographs cannot be used to compare any data
A reason unrelated to pictographs
Pictographs only work with negative numbers
They present data in a simple, visual way that is easy to compare

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Nutrition and the Food Groups
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: a balanced diet includes a variety of foods from different groups, such as vegetables and fruits, grains, and protein foods, each providing different nutrients the body needs.

quiz.q: A balanced diet includes foods from ___.
options: No specific groups at all
A variety of different food groups
Only a single food group
A concept unrelated to nutrition

quiz.q: Which of these is considered a protein food?
options: A concept unrelated to protein foods
Bread
Rice
Fish

quiz.q: Why does the body need nutrients from vegetables and fruits?
options: They are needed only for their colour
They provide important vitamins, minerals, and fibre
A reason unrelated to nutrition
Vegetables and fruits provide no nutrients at all

quiz.q: What is one benefit of eating a variety of foods rather than just one type?
options: It helps the body get all the different nutrients it needs
Eating a variety of foods provides no benefit
Variety only affects how food tastes, not health
A reason unrelated to nutrition

quiz.q: Why might drinking enough water be considered part of good nutrition?
options: The body does not need water to stay healthy
Water helps the body function properly and stay hydrated
Water has no connection to how the body functions
A reason unrelated to nutrition

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada’s Role in International Alliances (NATO)
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada is a member of international alliances, such as NATO, that bring countries together to support mutual defence and cooperation.

quiz.q: NATO is an alliance that brings countries together to support ___.
options: Trade disputes between member countries
Mutual defence and cooperation
A concept unrelated to international alliances
Complete isolation from other countries

quiz.q: Why might Canada choose to join an international alliance like NATO?
options: A reason unrelated to international relations
Joining an alliance offers no benefits to a country
To strengthen security and cooperation with other countries
Canada is required to join with no choice involved

quiz.q: Which of these best describes how alliance members support one another?
options: A concept unrelated to alliances
They compete against each other militarily
They refuse to communicate with one another
They cooperate on shared defence and security goals

quiz.q: Why might belonging to an international alliance be considered part of Canada’s foreign policy?
options: It reflects how Canada works with other countries on global issues
Canada has never taken part in any international alliance
A reason unrelated to Canadian government
Alliances have no connection to a country’s foreign policy

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to learn about international alliances?
options: International alliances have no relevance to students
Countries never work together on shared goals
A reason unrelated to social studies learning
It helps them understand how countries cooperate on global security and issues

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Writing Effective Introductions
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: an effective introduction grabs the reader’s attention, introduces the topic, and often previews the main ideas that will follow.

quiz.q: An effective introduction should ___.
options: Avoid mentioning the topic at all
Summarize the entire piece of writing word for word
Grab the reader’s attention and introduce the topic
A concept unrelated to writing

quiz.q: Which is an example of a strong opening hook in an introduction?
options: A random sentence with no connection to the topic
An interesting question or surprising fact related to the topic
A concept unrelated to introductions
A list of every source used in the writing

quiz.q: Why might a writer preview the main ideas in an introduction?
options: It helps the reader know what to expect from the rest of the writing
A reason unrelated to writing introductions
Introductions should never mention any main ideas
Previewing ideas always confuses the reader

quiz.q: Which of these would likely make a weak introduction?
options: A concept unrelated to introductions
Starting with “This essay is about a topic.” and nothing else
Starting with an interesting question about the topic
Starting with a surprising, relevant fact

quiz.q: Why is the introduction often considered an important part of a piece of writing?
options: The introduction has no effect on how a reader experiences the writing
Introductions are an optional part of writing that can be skipped
A reason unrelated to writing structure
It creates the reader’s first impression and sets up the rest of the writing

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Estimating Sums and Differences
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: estimating sums and differences involves rounding numbers before adding or subtracting to quickly find an approximate answer.

quiz.q: Estimating a sum or difference involves ___.
options: Finding the exact answer using a calculator
Rounding numbers before adding or subtracting
A concept unrelated to estimation
Ignoring the numbers in the problem entirely

quiz.q: Estimate the sum of 48 + 31 by rounding to the nearest ten.
options: A value unrelated to the estimation

quiz.q: Estimate the difference of 92 - 47 by rounding to the nearest ten.
options: A value unrelated to the estimation

quiz.q: Why might someone estimate before solving a math problem?
options: A reason unrelated to estimation
Estimating always gives the exact same result as the real answer
Estimating never helps with checking an answer
To quickly check whether their exact answer seems reasonable

quiz.q: Which is a real-life situation where estimating a sum could be useful?
options: Estimating never applies to real-life situations
Quickly checking if you have enough money for groceries
Estimating is only used in advanced scientific research
A reason unrelated to estimation

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Skin and Its Functions
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: the skin is the body’s largest organ, protecting internal tissues, helping regulate body temperature, and providing the sense of touch.

quiz.q: The skin is considered the body’s ___.
options: Largest organ
A concept unrelated to the skin
Only a covering with no other function
Smallest organ

quiz.q: Which of these is a function of the skin?
options: Pumping blood throughout the body
A function unrelated to the skin
Protecting the body from germs and injury
Digesting food for energy

quiz.q: How does the skin help regulate body temperature?
options: By preventing the body from ever losing heat
By sweating to cool the body down when it is hot
The skin has no connection to body temperature
A reason unrelated to the skin

quiz.q: Which sense is closely connected to the skin?
options: Taste
A sense unrelated to the skin
Touch
Hearing

quiz.q: Why is it important to protect skin from sun damage?
options: Sun exposure has no effect on the skin
A reason unrelated to skin health
The skin can be harmed by too much exposure to ultraviolet light
Skin cannot be damaged by sunlight

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada’s Provincial and Territorial Capitals
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: each of Canada’s provinces and territories has its own capital city, which is home to that region’s government.

quiz.q: The capital city of Ontario is ___.
options: Ottawa
A city unrelated to Ontario
Hamilton
Toronto

quiz.q: The capital city of British Columbia is ___.
options: A city unrelated to British Columbia
Vancouver
Kelowna
Victoria

quiz.q: What is typically located in a province’s or territory’s capital city?
options: That region’s government buildings and legislature
A concept unrelated to capital cities
The capital of a different province entirely
Only farmland with no government presence

quiz.q: Which of these is the capital of Canada as a whole country?
options: A city unrelated to Canada’s federal capital
Toronto
Ottawa
Montreal

quiz.q: Why is it useful to know the capital cities of Canada’s provinces and territories?
options: It helps build understanding of where regional governments are based
A reason unrelated to Canadian geography
Every province and territory shares the exact same capital
Capital cities have no connection to how a region is governed

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Direct and Indirect Speech
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: direct speech repeats a speaker’s exact words, often in quotation marks, while indirect speech reports what someone said without quoting them exactly.

quiz.q: Direct speech repeats a speaker’s ___.
options: Words translated into a different language
General idea, without quoting them exactly
A concept unrelated to grammar
Exact words, often in quotation marks

quiz.q: Indirect speech reports what someone said ___.
options: Using their exact words in quotation marks
Only in the form of a question
A concept unrelated to grammar
Without quoting them exactly

quiz.q: Which sentence uses direct speech?
options: A sentence unrelated to direct speech
Maya mentioned something about a library once.
Maya said that she was going to the library.
Maya said, “I am going to the library.”

quiz.q: Which sentence uses indirect speech?
options: Jordan shouted, “I am here early!”
A sentence unrelated to indirect speech
Jordan said, “I will arrive early.”
Jordan said that he would arrive early.

quiz.q: Why might a writer choose indirect speech instead of direct speech?
options: A reason unrelated to grammar
Indirect speech is never used in writing
To summarize what someone said without quoting their exact words
Indirect speech always requires quotation marks

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Congruent and Similar Shapes
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: congruent shapes are identical in size and shape, while similar shapes have the same shape but can differ in size, with matching angles and proportional sides.

quiz.q: Congruent shapes are ___.
options: Identical in both size and shape
The same shape but always different sizes
Never the same in any way
A concept unrelated to congruent shapes

quiz.q: Similar shapes have ___.
options: Completely different shapes and angles
The same shape, with matching angles and proportional sides
A concept unrelated to similar shapes
No matching angles or sides at all

quiz.q: If two triangles are congruent, their corresponding sides are ___.
options: Only equal if the triangles are also similar
Always different in length
Exactly equal in length
A description unrelated to congruent triangles

quiz.q: If two rectangles are similar, one rectangle is ___ the other.
options: Never related in shape to
A description unrelated to similar shapes
Always exactly the same size as
A scaled-up or scaled-down version of

quiz.q: Why might architects use similar shapes when designing a building?
options: A reason unrelated to similar shapes
Architects never use scale models of any kind
Similar shapes have no use in architecture
To create a scaled model that keeps the same proportions as the final building

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Gravity and Its Effects
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: gravity is a force that pulls objects toward each other, and on Earth it pulls objects toward the ground, giving them weight.

quiz.q: Gravity is a force that ___.
options: Pulls objects toward each other
Pushes objects away from each other
A concept unrelated to gravity
Has no effect on objects at all

quiz.q: On Earth, gravity pulls objects toward ___.
options: The sky above
A direction unrelated to gravity
Outer space
The ground

quiz.q: Why do objects fall when dropped?
options: Objects fall with no connection to gravity
Objects only fall because of wind
A reason unrelated to gravity
Earth’s gravity pulls them downward

quiz.q: An object’s weight is affected by ___.
options: The strength of the gravity pulling on it
The temperature of the object
The colour of the object
A factor unrelated to weight

quiz.q: Why would an astronaut weigh less on the Moon than on Earth?
options: The Moon has weaker gravity than Earth
Gravity is exactly the same on the Moon and Earth
The Moon has stronger gravity than Earth
A reason unrelated to gravity

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Consumer Rights and Responsibilities
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: consumers have rights, such as receiving accurate information about products, along with responsibilities, such as spending money wisely.

quiz.q: A consumer right includes receiving ___.
options: Accurate information about a product before buying it
No information about a product at all
False advertising with no consequences
A concept unrelated to consumer rights

quiz.q: Which of these is a consumer responsibility?
options: Comparing prices and making informed spending decisions
Ignoring the cost of a product entirely
Spending money without any thought or planning
A concept unrelated to consumer responsibilities

quiz.q: Why might it be important for advertisements to be truthful?
options: Advertisements have no effect on what consumers decide to buy
Consumers rely on accurate information to make good decisions
A reason unrelated to consumer rights
Truthfulness in advertising is never required

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of being a responsible consumer?
options: A concept unrelated to consumer responsibility
Buying items with no consideration of cost
Saving money for something important instead of spending it right away
Ignoring how much money is available before spending

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to learn about consumer rights and responsibilities?
options: It helps prepare them to make informed decisions as future consumers
Consumers have no rights or responsibilities of any kind
This knowledge has no connection to everyday life
A reason unrelated to social studies learning

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Drawing Conclusions
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: drawing a conclusion means combining evidence from the text with what you already know to form a logical judgment about something the author does not state directly.

quiz.q: Drawing a conclusion means combining text evidence with ___.
options: A concept unrelated to reading
What you already know to form a logical judgment
A random guess with no evidence involved
Only information that is stated directly in the text

quiz.q: Which is an example of drawing a conclusion?
options: Ignoring all details in the text
A concept unrelated to drawing conclusions
Copying a sentence directly from the text
Deciding a character is nervous because of how they are described acting

quiz.q: Why is it useful for readers to draw conclusions while reading?
options: Conclusions can only be drawn after finishing an entire book
A reason unrelated to reading comprehension
Drawing conclusions never adds anything to understanding a text
It helps them understand ideas the author implies but does not state directly

quiz.q: What should a reader use as support when drawing a conclusion?
options: A concept unrelated to drawing conclusions
Only the title of the text
A conclusion with no connection to the text at all
Specific evidence and details found in the text

quiz.q: How is drawing a conclusion different from simply summarizing a text?
options: Drawing a conclusion and summarizing mean exactly the same thing
Summarizing always requires forming a judgment about the text
Drawing a conclusion involves interpreting evidence, not just restating events
A concept unrelated to reading skills

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Elapsed Time and Time Zones
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: elapsed time is the amount of time that passes between a start time and an end time, and time zones account for differences in time across regions.

quiz.q: Elapsed time is the amount of time that ___.
options: Is shown on a single clock at one moment
Never changes no matter the situation
Passes between a start time and an end time
A concept unrelated to elapsed time

quiz.q: If a movie starts at 2:15 p.m. and ends at 4:00 p.m., how much time has elapsed?
options: 1 hour 45 minutes
1 hour 15 minutes
2 hours 15 minutes
A duration unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: A time zone is a region that ___.
options: Shares the same standard time
A concept unrelated to time zones
Has no connection to time at all
Never experiences any change in time

quiz.q: Why does Canada have more than one time zone?
options: Time zones have no connection to a country’s size
A reason unrelated to time zones
The country spans a wide distance from east to west
Canada has only one time zone across the entire country

quiz.q: If it is 3:00 p.m. in one time zone and a neighbouring zone is one hour behind, what time is it there?
options: A time unrelated to the calculation

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: The Human Eye and Vision
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: the human eye detects light and sends signals to the brain, which interprets them as images, allowing us to see the world around us.

quiz.q: The human eye allows us to see by detecting ___.
options: Air pressure
Sound waves
A concept unrelated to vision
Light

quiz.q: Which part of the eye controls how much light enters?
options: The pupil
The eyelash
A part unrelated to vision
The eyebrow

quiz.q: After light enters the eye, signals are sent to the ___ to be interpreted.
options: A part unrelated to vision
Lungs
Stomach
Brain

quiz.q: Why might the pupil become smaller in bright light?
options: A reason unrelated to vision
The pupil always stays exactly the same size
Bright light has no effect on the pupil
To limit the amount of light entering the eye

quiz.q: Why is protecting your eyes important?
options: The eyes are sensitive organs needed for the sense of sight
The eyes have no important function in the body
A reason unrelated to eye safety
Eyes cannot be damaged in any way

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada’s National Symbols and Their History
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada’s national symbols, such as the flag, coat of arms, and national anthem, represent the country’s identity and history.

quiz.q: Canada’s current national flag, featuring a red maple leaf, was officially adopted in ___.
options: A year unrelated to Canada’s flag history

quiz.q: What does the maple leaf on the Canadian flag commonly represent?
options: Another country’s national identity
A symbol with no connection to Canada
A concept unrelated to Canadian symbols
Canadian identity and natural heritage

quiz.q: Which of these is considered an official national symbol of Canada?
options: A flag belonging to a different country
A symbol unrelated to Canadian identity
An object with no historical significance
The coat of arms

quiz.q: Why do countries often adopt national symbols?
options: National symbols serve no real purpose
To represent shared identity, values, and history
Symbols are chosen with no meaning behind them
A reason unrelated to national identity

quiz.q: Why might learning about the history of national symbols be valuable?
options: A reason unrelated to social studies learning
It helps explain how a country’s identity has developed over time
Symbols never change or have any background story
National symbols have no connection to a country’s history

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Review: Bias, Prepositions, Flashback, and Figurative Language
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: this review lesson revisits key ideas from Days 61-69, including identifying bias, prepositional phrases, flashback, and personification and metaphor.

quiz.q: Bias in a text means the writing ___.
options: Presents information in a one-sided, unfair way
A concept unrelated to reading
Presents every side of an issue equally
Contains only verified factual statements

quiz.q: A prepositional phrase begins with a ___.
options: A part of speech unrelated to prepositional phrases
Verb
Noun only, with no preposition
Preposition

quiz.q: A flashback shows an event that happened ___.
options: At exactly the same moment as the current scene
A concept unrelated to reading
Earlier in time, before the present moment of the story
Later in time, after the story has ended

quiz.q: Personification gives ___.
options: No special qualities to anything at all
A concept unrelated to figurative language
Human qualities to something non-human
Animal qualities to a human being

quiz.q: Why is it useful to review reading and grammar skills like these together?
options: These skills have no connection to each other
It reinforces how different language skills support clear reading and writing
A reason unrelated to reviewing language
Review never helps strengthen understanding of a subject

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Review: Decimals, Exponents, Factors, and Shapes
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: this review lesson revisits key ideas from Days 61-69, including adding and subtracting decimals, exponents, prime factorization, and congruent and similar shapes.

quiz.q: When adding decimals, it is important to first ___.
options: Round every number to a whole number
Line up the decimal points
A step unrelated to adding decimals
Ignore the decimal points entirely

quiz.q: What does 2 to the power of 3 (2 cubed) mean?
options: A value unrelated to this expression
2 x 2 x 2

quiz.q: Prime factorization breaks a number down into ___.
options: Only its largest factor
A list of all its factors, prime or not
A concept unrelated to factorization
The prime numbers that multiply together to make it

quiz.q: Congruent shapes are ___.
options: The same shape but always different sizes
Never the same in any way
Identical in both size and shape
A concept unrelated to congruent shapes

quiz.q: Why is it useful to review decimals, exponents, factors, and shapes together?
options: Review never helps strengthen understanding of a subject
It reinforces how these math concepts connect and build on one another
These topics have no connection to each other
A reason unrelated to reviewing math

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Review: Magnetism, Earth Layers, Volcanoes, and the Human Body
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: this review lesson revisits key ideas from Days 61-69, including magnetism, the layers of the Earth, volcanoes, and the skin and human eye.

quiz.q: A magnet has two poles, called the ___.
options: North pole and south pole
A concept unrelated to magnetism
Positive pole and negative pole
Hot pole and cold pole

quiz.q: The outermost layer of the Earth is called the ___.
options: Inner core
Crust
Mantle
A layer unrelated to Earth’s structure

quiz.q: Magma that reaches the Earth’s surface is called ___.
options: A term unrelated to volcanoes
Groundwater
Sediment
Lava

quiz.q: The skin is considered the body’s ___.
options: Largest organ
Only a covering with no other function
Smallest organ
A concept unrelated to the skin

quiz.q: Why is it useful to review magnetism, Earth science, and body systems together?
options: It reinforces how these interconnected science concepts relate to one another
These topics have no connection to each other
Review is never useful in science
Each topic must be studied with no connection to the others

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Review: Confederation, Economy, Capitals, and Canadian Identity
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: this review lesson revisits key ideas from Days 61-69, including Confederation, Canada’s economy, provincial capitals, and national symbols.

quiz.q: Confederation in 1867 united several British colonies to form ___.
options: A concept unrelated to Canadian history
A colony still ruled directly by Britain
A country called New France
The Dominion of Canada

quiz.q: A primary industry is one that ___.
options: Provides services like teaching or banking
Gathers raw materials, such as mining or farming
Only sells finished products in stores
A concept unrelated to industries

quiz.q: The capital city of Ontario is ___.
options: Ottawa
A city unrelated to Ontario
Toronto
Hamilton

quiz.q: What does the maple leaf on the Canadian flag commonly represent?
options: Another country’s national identity
Canadian identity and natural heritage
A symbol with no connection to Canada
A concept unrelated to Canadian symbols

quiz.q: Why is it useful to review Confederation, the economy, capitals, and national identity together?
options: Review never helps strengthen understanding of a subject
It reinforces how these Social Studies concepts connect to build understanding of Canada
A reason unrelated to reviewing social studies
These topics have no connection to each other

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Understanding Irony
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: irony occurs when there is a difference between what is expected to happen and what actually happens, or between what is said and what is truly meant.

quiz.q: Irony occurs when there is a difference between what is expected and ___.
options: What always happens exactly as planned
A synonym for exactly what is expected
What actually happens
A concept unrelated to irony

quiz.q: Which is an example of irony?
options: A concept unrelated to irony
A fire station burning down
A weather report that is always accurate
A fire station opening on time

quiz.q: Verbal irony occurs when a speaker says ___.
options: Exactly what they truly mean
A concept unrelated to verbal irony
The opposite of what they truly mean
Nothing at all

quiz.q: Why might an author use irony in a story?
options: Irony never affects how a reader experiences a story
A reason unrelated to irony
To make the plot completely predictable
To create humour or emphasize a surprising contrast

quiz.q: How is irony different from simply describing an unexpected event?
options: Irony only occurs in nonfiction texts
Irony and unexpected events are exactly the same thing
Irony highlights a meaningful contrast between expectation and reality
A concept unrelated to irony

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Multiplying Two-Digit Numbers
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: multiplying two two-digit numbers involves breaking the numbers into tens and ones, multiplying each part, and adding the partial products together.

quiz.q: When multiplying two two-digit numbers using the standard algorithm, each digit of one number is multiplied by ___.
options: Each digit of the other number
Only the first digit of the same number
Only the ones digit of the other number
A concept unrelated to multiplication

quiz.q: What is 23 x 14?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is 32 x 21?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What are the partial products of 12 x 13 when broken into tens and ones (10+2) x (10+3)?
options: 12 and 13 added together
A concept unrelated to partial products
Only one product with no breakdown

quiz.q: Why might breaking a multiplication problem into tens and ones make it easier to solve?
options: It changes the answer to the problem
A reason unrelated to multiplication strategies
Breaking numbers apart never makes multiplication easier
It turns one large multiplication into smaller, simpler steps

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Heat Transfer: Conduction, Convection, and Radiation
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: heat energy moves from warmer objects to cooler ones through conduction, convection, and radiation, each involving a different method of transfer.

quiz.q: Conduction is the transfer of heat through ___.
options: Direct contact between objects
A concept unrelated to heat transfer
Empty space with no matter involved
Sound waves travelling through air

quiz.q: Convection is the transfer of heat through the movement of ___.
options: A solid object only
Light rays only
A liquid or a gas
A concept unrelated to convection

quiz.q: Radiation is the transfer of heat through ___.
options: Only cold objects
A concept unrelated to radiation
Waves that can travel through empty space
Direct contact between two solids only

quiz.q: Which is an example of conduction?
options: Heat moving from a hot stove into a metal pot touching it
A concept unrelated to conduction
Heat from the sun reaching Earth through space
Warm air rising in a room

quiz.q: Why does heat generally move from a warmer object to a cooler one?
options: Cooler objects always transfer heat to warmer ones
Heat naturally flows toward areas with less thermal energy until they balance out
A reason unrelated to heat transfer
Heat never moves between objects of different temperatures

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Klondike Gold Rush
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: the Klondike Gold Rush of the late 1890s drew thousands of prospectors to the Yukon in search of gold, shaping the growth and history of northern Canada.

quiz.q: The Klondike Gold Rush drew thousands of prospectors to the ___.
options: Prairies
Atlantic coast
Yukon
A region unrelated to the Klondike Gold Rush

quiz.q: What resource were prospectors searching for during the Klondike Gold Rush?
options: Gold
Coal
A resource unrelated to the Klondike Gold Rush
Oil

quiz.q: The Klondike Gold Rush mainly took place during which decade?
options: The 1890s
A decade unrelated to the Klondike Gold Rush
The 1950s
The 1700s

quiz.q: How did the Klondike Gold Rush affect the growth of towns in the Yukon?
options: The Gold Rush had no effect on any towns in the Yukon
It caused towns such as Dawson City to grow quickly as prospectors arrived
A reason unrelated to the Klondike Gold Rush
It caused every town in the Yukon to shrink and disappear

quiz.q: Why is the Klondike Gold Rush an important event in Canadian history?
options: It shaped the settlement and development of northern Canada
The event had no lasting impact on Canadian history
It only affected countries outside of Canada
A reason unrelated to Canadian history

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Grammar: Subject-Verb Agreement
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: subject-verb agreement means a singular subject takes a singular verb, and a plural subject takes a plural verb, so the two match in a sentence.

quiz.q: Subject-verb agreement means the subject and verb in a sentence must ___.
options: A concept unrelated to subject-verb agreement
Always be in the past tense
Always start with a capital letter
Match in number, singular or plural

quiz.q: Which sentence shows correct subject-verb agreement?
options: A sentence unrelated to subject-verb agreement
The dog run in the park.
The dogs runs in the park.
The dogs run in the park.

quiz.q: In the sentence “She ___ to school every day,” which verb correctly completes it?
options: Walk
Walking
Walks
A word unrelated to this sentence

quiz.q: Why is subject-verb agreement important in writing?
options: A reason unrelated to grammar
Verbs never need to match their subject
Subject-verb agreement has no effect on how a sentence reads
It helps sentences read clearly and sound grammatically correct

quiz.q: Which subject requires a plural verb?
options: A subject unrelated to subject-verb agreement
One student
The children
The child

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Long Division: Interpreting Remainders
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: when a division problem does not divide evenly, the leftover amount is called the remainder, and how it is interpreted depends on the real-world context of the problem.

quiz.q: A remainder is the amount left over when a number ___.
options: Is multiplied by another number
Does not divide evenly into equal groups
A concept unrelated to remainders
Divides evenly with nothing left over

quiz.q: What is 17 divided by 5, expressed with a remainder?
options: 4 remainder 1
A value unrelated to the calculation
3 remainder 5
3 remainder 2

quiz.q: If 23 stickers are shared equally among 4 friends, how many whole stickers does each friend get, and how many are left over?
options: 6 stickers each, with 1 left over
5 stickers each, with 3 left over
A result unrelated to the calculation
4 stickers each, with 5 left over

quiz.q: If 26 students need to travel in vans that hold 6 students each, how many vans are needed to fit everyone, including the leftover students?
options: 6 vans
A number unrelated to the calculation
4 vans
5 vans

quiz.q: Why might a remainder sometimes need to be rounded up in a real-world problem, such as the vans example?
options: Because the leftover people or items still need to be accounted for
A reason unrelated to interpreting remainders
Rounding up always gives an incorrect answer
Remainders should always be ignored in real-world problems

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Simple Machines: The Wheel and Axle and the Inclined Plane
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: a wheel and axle reduces the force needed to move an object by rotating together, while an inclined plane is a sloped surface that reduces the force needed to raise an object.

quiz.q: A wheel and axle reduces the force needed to move an object by ___.
options: Cutting the object into smaller pieces
Staying completely still
A concept unrelated to the wheel and axle
Rotating together as a single unit

quiz.q: An inclined plane is best described as a ___.
options: Wheel that spins around an axle
Sloped surface that makes it easier to raise a load
A concept unrelated to inclined planes
Flat surface with no slope at all

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of a wheel and axle?
options: A pair of scissors
A concept unrelated to the wheel and axle
A ramp leading up to a doorway
A doorknob

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of an inclined plane?
options: A concept unrelated to inclined planes
A pair of pliers
A ramp used to load a truck
A steering wheel on a car

quiz.q: Why might a ramp make it easier to move a heavy object to a higher place?
options: Ramps have no effect on the force needed to move an object
A ramp always requires more force than lifting straight up
It spreads the work over a longer distance, reducing the force needed at any moment
A reason unrelated to inclined planes

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Bilingualism and Canada’s Official Languages
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada has two official languages, English and French, and government services at the federal level are available in both languages.

quiz.q: Canada’s two official languages are ___.
options: French and Mandarin
A pair of languages unrelated to Canada’s official languages
English and French
English and Spanish

quiz.q: Federal government services in Canada must be available in ___.
options: Both official languages
A concept unrelated to bilingualism
No particular language
Only one official language

quiz.q: Which Canadian province is officially bilingual?
options: A province unrelated to official bilingualism
British Columbia
New Brunswick
Alberta

quiz.q: Why might a country choose to have more than one official language?
options: To reflect and respect the languages and history of its diverse population
Having more than one official language offers no benefit to a country
A reason unrelated to bilingualism
Countries are never allowed to have more than one official language

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to learn about Canada’s official languages?
options: A reason unrelated to social studies learning
Canada has never had more than one language spoken
It helps them understand an important part of Canadian identity and government
Official languages have no connection to Canadian identity

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Writing a Diary Entry from a Character’s Perspective
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: a diary entry written from a character’s perspective uses first-person voice to express that character’s thoughts, feelings, and experiences about events in a story.

quiz.q: A diary entry written from a character’s perspective is written in ___.
options: First-person voice
A concept unrelated to diary entries
Third-person voice
A voice with no clear perspective at all

quiz.q: What might a diary entry from a character’s perspective include?
options: Information the character could not possibly know
Only a list of facts with no personal feelings included
The character’s private thoughts and feelings about events
A concept unrelated to diary entries

quiz.q: Why is writing in first person useful for a diary entry?
options: It allows the writer to express a character’s personal thoughts directly
First person always describes events from an outside narrator
A reason unrelated to writing a diary entry
First person can never be used for diary entries

quiz.q: Which is an example of a sentence that might appear in a character’s diary entry?
options: Today I felt nervous walking into the new school for the first time.
A sentence unrelated to diary entries
The character walked into the new school on Monday morning.
The story takes place in a small town.

quiz.q: Why might writing a diary entry from a character’s point of view help a reader understand a story better?
options: A reason unrelated to writing diary entries
It offers insight into the character’s inner thoughts and emotions
Diary entries never help a reader understand a character better
Diary entries only describe events with no personal feelings

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Comparing and Ordering Decimals
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: comparing and ordering decimals involves lining up the decimal points and comparing digits from left to right, starting with the largest place value.

quiz.q: When comparing decimals, it helps to first ___.
options: Line up the decimal points
Ignore the decimal points completely
Round every decimal to a whole number
A step unrelated to comparing decimals

quiz.q: Which decimal is greater: 0.45 or 0.5?
options: Both are exactly equal
A value unrelated to the comparison

quiz.q: Which list of decimals is ordered from least to greatest?
options: A list unrelated to the comparison

quiz.q: Which decimal is smaller: 0.6 or 0.09?
options: A value unrelated to the comparison
Both are exactly equal

quiz.q: Why might someone need to compare decimals in real life?
options: To compare prices or measurements that include cents or fractional units
A reason unrelated to decimals
Comparing decimals never applies to real-life situations
Decimals are only used in advanced scientific research

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Electrical Conductors and Insulators
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: an electrical conductor allows electric current to pass through it easily, such as metal, while an insulator resists the flow of electric current, such as rubber or plastic.

quiz.q: An electrical conductor is a material that ___.
options: Allows electric current to pass through it easily
Has no connection to electricity at all
Blocks electric current from passing through
A concept unrelated to conductors

quiz.q: An electrical insulator is a material that ___.
options: Allows electric current to flow through it easily
Resists the flow of electric current
Always generates its own electricity
A concept unrelated to insulators

quiz.q: Which of these materials is a good electrical conductor?
options: Wood
Copper
A material unrelated to electrical conductivity
Rubber

quiz.q: Which of these materials is a good electrical insulator?
options: Plastic
Aluminum
Copper
A material unrelated to electrical insulation

quiz.q: Why are electrical cords often covered in a material like rubber or plastic?
options: Rubber and plastic make the wire conduct more electricity
To insulate the wire and protect people from electric shock
A reason unrelated to electrical safety
Covering a cord has no effect on electrical safety

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Role of the Governor General
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: the Governor General is the representative of the Crown in Canada, performing ceremonial duties and formally approving new laws passed by Parliament.

quiz.q: The Governor General is the representative of ___ in Canada.
options: A political party
A foreign government
A concept unrelated to the Governor General
The Crown

quiz.q: One duty of the Governor General is to ___.
options: Vote in every federal election
A concept unrelated to the Governor General
Formally approve new laws passed by Parliament
Lead a political party in Parliament

quiz.q: Which of these best describes the Governor General’s role?
options: The elected leader of the government
Largely ceremonial, representing the Crown at official events
A role identical to the Prime Minister’s
A role with no connection to Canadian government

quiz.q: Who recommends the appointment of the Governor General?
options: The Prime Minister
A concept unrelated to this appointment
A foreign country’s leader
A vote from all Canadian citizens

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to learn about the role of the Governor General?
options: A reason unrelated to social studies learning
The Governor General has no connection to Canadian government
It helps them understand Canada’s system of government and its ties to the Crown
The role of Governor General no longer exists

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Vocabulary: Homophones and Homographs
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: homophones are words that sound alike but have different spellings and meanings, while homographs are spelled the same but can have different meanings and sometimes different pronunciations.

quiz.q: Homophones are words that ___.
options: A concept unrelated to homophones
Sound alike but have different spellings and meanings
Are spelled exactly the same and mean the same thing
Always begin with the same letter

quiz.q: Which pair of words are homophones?
options: Happy and sad
Run and running
Their and there
A pair of words unrelated to homophones

quiz.q: Homographs are words that ___.
options: Are spelled the same but can have different meanings
A concept unrelated to homographs
Always sound completely different from each other
Are always opposite in meaning

quiz.q: Which sentence uses the word “bow” as a homograph, where its meaning depends on context?
options: He read the entire book in one day.
She ran quickly to the store.
A sentence unrelated to homographs
She wore a bow in her hair, then took a bow after the play.

quiz.q: Why is it important for readers to understand homophones and homographs?
options: A reason unrelated to vocabulary
To choose the correct spelling and meaning based on context
These words never cause any confusion for readers
Homophones and homographs mean exactly the same thing

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Least Common Multiple and Greatest Common Factor
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: the least common multiple is the smallest number that two numbers both divide into evenly, while the greatest common factor is the largest number that divides evenly into both numbers.

quiz.q: The least common multiple of two numbers is the smallest number that ___.
options: A concept unrelated to least common multiple
Is smaller than both numbers
Both numbers divide into evenly
Only one of the numbers divides into evenly

quiz.q: What is the least common multiple of 4 and 6?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: The greatest common factor of two numbers is the largest number that ___.
options: Divides evenly into both numbers
Is greater than both numbers
A concept unrelated to greatest common factor
Cannot divide into either number

quiz.q: What is the greatest common factor of 12 and 18?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why might the greatest common factor be useful when simplifying a fraction?
options: It shows the largest number that can divide both the numerator and denominator evenly
The greatest common factor has no connection to simplifying fractions
It can only be used with even numbers
A reason unrelated to this math skill

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Vertebrates and Invertebrates
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: vertebrates are animals with a backbone, such as fish and mammals, while invertebrates are animals without a backbone, such as insects and worms.

quiz.q: A vertebrate is an animal that has a ___.
options: Set of wings
A concept unrelated to vertebrates
Shell only
Backbone

quiz.q: An invertebrate is an animal that ___.
options: Always has a backbone
Cannot move at all
A concept unrelated to invertebrates
Does not have a backbone

quiz.q: Which of these animals is a vertebrate?
options: A fish
A concept unrelated to vertebrates
A worm
An insect

quiz.q: Which of these animals is an invertebrate?
options: A spider
A concept unrelated to invertebrates
A reptile
A bird

quiz.q: Why might scientists group animals into vertebrates and invertebrates?
options: A reason unrelated to classifying animals
It helps organize and compare animals based on a shared physical feature
This grouping provides no useful information about animals
All animals belong to exactly the same group

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Canada’s Currency and Its Symbols
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: Canada’s currency includes coins and bills featuring symbols such as the loon on the one-dollar coin, reflecting the country’s history, wildlife, and identity.

quiz.q: Canada’s one-dollar coin is nicknamed the ___ because of the bird pictured on it.
options: A name unrelated to Canadian currency
Loonie
Toonie
Beaver dollar

quiz.q: Canadian currency includes coins and ___.
options: A concept unrelated to Canadian currency
Paper or polymer bills
Items with no value at all
Only gold bars

quiz.q: Why might Canadian currency feature images such as wildlife or historical figures?
options: Currency images have no connection to a country’s identity
To reflect the country’s history, culture, and identity
Currency designs are chosen completely at random
A reason unrelated to Canadian currency

quiz.q: What is the name of Canada’s central bank, responsible for issuing currency?
options: The Bank of Canada
A bank located in another country
A bank unrelated to Canadian currency
A private company with no government connection

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to learn about the symbols on Canadian currency?
options: Canadian currency has never featured any symbols
Currency symbols have no meaning behind them
A reason unrelated to social studies learning
It helps them understand how currency reflects national identity and history

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Main Idea and Supporting Details
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: the main idea is the central point a text is making, while supporting details are the specific facts, examples, or reasons that explain and back up that main idea.

quiz.q: The main idea of a text is ___.
options: A minor detail mentioned only once
The central point the text is making
A concept unrelated to reading comprehension
The very last sentence of the text, always

quiz.q: Supporting details in a text ___.
options: A concept unrelated to supporting details
Provide facts or examples that explain the main idea
Always contradict the main idea
Have no connection to the main idea at all

quiz.q: Which is an example of a supporting detail for the main idea “Recycling helps protect the environment”?
options: A sentence with no connection to recycling
A concept unrelated to supporting details
The weather was sunny on Tuesday
Recycling paper reduces the number of trees that need to be cut down

quiz.q: Why is it useful for readers to identify the main idea of a text?
options: The main idea is always stated in the title only
Identifying the main idea never helps with understanding a text
It helps them understand the central point the author is making
A reason unrelated to reading comprehension

quiz.q: How can a reader use supporting details to find the main idea?
options: A reason unrelated to identifying the main idea
Supporting details never provide any clues about the main idea
By ignoring all the details in the text
By noticing what several details have in common and what point they support

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Data Management: Surveys, Tally Charts, and Frequency Tables
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: a survey collects information by asking people questions, and the results can be recorded using tally charts and organized into frequency tables before being displayed as a graph.

quiz.q: A survey collects information by ___.
options: A concept unrelated to surveys
Reading a story from beginning to end
Measuring temperature with a thermometer
Asking people questions

quiz.q: A tally chart is used to ___.
options: Draw a picture of the data collected
Record how many times each response occurs
A concept unrelated to tally charts
Show only the final average of the data

quiz.q: A frequency table organizes data by showing ___.
options: Only the largest value collected
A single result with no other information
How often each value or category occurs
A concept unrelated to frequency tables

quiz.q: If a tally chart shows 10 marks grouped in fives plus 2 more marks for a category, how many responses does that represent?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why might someone organize survey data into a frequency table before making a graph?
options: It makes the data easier to read and turn into a graph
Organizing data into a table never helps with making a graph
A reason unrelated to data management
Frequency tables can only be used with numbers larger than 100

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Soil Composition and Formation
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: soil forms over long periods of time as rock breaks down through weathering and mixes with decayed plant and animal matter, called humus.

quiz.q: Soil forms as rock breaks down through a process called ___.
options: A concept unrelated to soil formation
Evaporation
Photosynthesis
Weathering

quiz.q: Humus, an important part of soil, is made up of ___.
options: Pure water with no other materials
Decayed plant and animal matter
A concept unrelated to soil composition
Melted rock from a volcano

quiz.q: Why does soil formation typically take a very long time?
options: Soil forms instantly with no time required at all
Weathering has no connection to how soil forms
A reason unrelated to soil formation
Rock breaks down gradually through weathering over many years

quiz.q: Which of these is a component commonly found in soil?
options: Pure oxygen gas
A component unrelated to soil
Minerals from broken-down rock
Only liquid water with nothing else mixed in

quiz.q: Why is healthy soil important for growing plants?
options: It provides nutrients, water, and support for plant roots
Plants can grow equally well with no soil at all
Soil has no connection to how plants grow
A reason unrelated to soil health

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The History of Transportation in Canada
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: transportation in Canada has changed over time, from canoes and horse-drawn wagons to railways, automobiles, and airplanes, shaping how people and goods move across the country.

quiz.q: Before railways and cars, Indigenous peoples and early settlers in Canada often travelled by ___.
options: Canoe
Subway
A method unrelated to early transportation
Airplane

quiz.q: How did railways change transportation in Canada?
options: A reason unrelated to Canadian transportation history
Railways had no effect on how goods were moved
They allowed people and goods to travel long distances more quickly
Railways made travel across Canada impossible

quiz.q: Which mode of transportation became common in Canada during the twentieth century, changing how people travelled locally?
options: A mode unrelated to twentieth-century transportation
The horse-drawn wagon, exclusively
The canoe
The automobile

quiz.q: Why is transportation important to Canada’s development as a country?
options: Transportation has no connection to how a country develops
It connects communities and helps move people and goods across long distances
A reason unrelated to Canadian history
Canada has never needed methods of transportation

quiz.q: Why might studying the history of transportation help students understand Canada’s growth?
options: A reason unrelated to social studies learning
Transportation methods have never changed throughout Canadian history
The history of transportation has no connection to a country’s growth
It shows how new technology changed how people lived, worked, and travelled

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Figurative Language: Imagery
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: imagery is descriptive language that appeals to the five senses, helping readers picture, hear, smell, taste, or feel what is happening in a text.

quiz.q: Imagery is descriptive language that appeals to ___.
options: The five senses
Only numbers and statistics
A concept unrelated to imagery
Only the sense of sight

quiz.q: Which sentence is a strong example of imagery?
options: It was a certain time of year.
The season was autumn.
A sentence unrelated to imagery
The crisp autumn air smelled of woodsmoke and fallen leaves.

quiz.q: Why might a writer use imagery in a story?
options: To help readers vividly picture or experience a scene
To make a text shorter and less detailed
A reason unrelated to imagery
Imagery never helps a reader picture a scene

quiz.q: Which sense does the phrase “the rough bark of the old oak tree” appeal to?
options: Hearing
A sense unrelated to this phrase
Taste
Touch

quiz.q: How is imagery different from simply stating a fact?
options: Facts always include vivid sensory description
Imagery and stating a fact are exactly the same thing
Imagery uses descriptive, sensory language rather than a plain statement
A concept unrelated to imagery

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Multiplying Decimals by Powers of Ten
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: multiplying a decimal by a power of ten, such as 10, 100, or 1000, moves the decimal point to the right the same number of places as there are zeros.

quiz.q: When multiplying a decimal by 10, the decimal point moves ___.
options: One place to the right
One place to the left
A direction unrelated to this rule
Two places to the left

quiz.q: What is 3.4 x 10?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is 2.75 x 100?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is 0.6 x 1000?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why is it useful to know the pattern for multiplying decimals by powers of ten?
options: A reason unrelated to this math skill
This pattern never applies to multiplying decimals
It allows for quick mental math without needing long multiplication
It only works with whole numbers, not decimals

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Sound: Pitch and Frequency
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: pitch is how high or low a sound seems, and it is determined by frequency, the number of sound wave vibrations that occur each second.

quiz.q: Pitch describes how ___ a sound seems.
options: High or low
Long or short
A concept unrelated to pitch
Loud or quiet

quiz.q: Frequency refers to ___.
options: The loudness of a sound only
The number of sound wave vibrations occurring each second
A concept unrelated to frequency
The colour of a sound wave

quiz.q: A sound with a higher frequency has a ___ pitch.
options: Lower
Completely silent
Higher
A description unrelated to pitch

quiz.q: Which of these is likely to produce a higher-pitched sound?
options: A small, tightly stretched guitar string
A large, loosely stretched guitar string
An object that is not vibrating at all
A concept unrelated to pitch

quiz.q: Why might a shorter vibrating object generally produce a higher pitch than a longer one?
options: It vibrates faster, producing a higher frequency
Shorter objects always vibrate more slowly than longer ones
The length of an object never affects the pitch it produces
A reason unrelated to pitch and frequency

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Provincial Legislatures and the Role of the Premier
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: each Canadian province has its own legislature that makes provincial laws, led by a premier, the head of the provincial government.

quiz.q: A provincial legislature is responsible for making laws at the ___ level.
options: Provincial
International
A level unrelated to provincial legislatures
Federal

quiz.q: The head of a provincial government is called the ___.
options: A title unrelated to provincial government
Governor General
Premier
Prime Minister

quiz.q: Which of these is typically a responsibility handled at the provincial level in Canada?
options: Education and healthcare
National defence
A responsibility unrelated to provincial government
Printing national currency

quiz.q: How does the role of a premier differ from the role of the Prime Minister?
options: A concept unrelated to Canadian government
A premier leads a provincial government, while the Prime Minister leads the federal government
A premier and the Prime Minister have exactly the same role
The Prime Minister only leads one province

quiz.q: Why does Canada have both federal and provincial levels of government?
options: Having multiple levels of government serves no purpose
A reason unrelated to Canadian government
To divide responsibilities between national and regional issues
Only one level of government exists in Canada

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Writing: Procedural Writing (How-To Instructions)
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: procedural writing gives step-by-step instructions in a clear sequence, often using numbered steps and precise, action-oriented language so a reader can complete a task.

quiz.q: Procedural writing gives instructions in ___.
options: A clear, step-by-step sequence
A single long paragraph with no steps
A random order with no particular sequence
A concept unrelated to procedural writing

quiz.q: Why do procedural texts often use numbered steps?
options: To show the reader the exact order actions should be completed
Numbered steps make instructions harder to follow
A reason unrelated to procedural writing
Numbers are never used in procedural writing

quiz.q: Which sentence is an example of procedural writing?
options: The bread smelled wonderful as it baked.
A sentence unrelated to procedural writing
The bakery was famous for its fresh bread.
First, pour the flour into a large bowl.

quiz.q: Why is precise, action-oriented language important in procedural writing?
options: Precise language makes instructions more confusing
A reason unrelated to procedural writing
It helps the reader understand exactly what action to take
Procedural writing should always be vague

quiz.q: Which of these is a common example of procedural writing?
options: A diary entry about a school day
A concept unrelated to procedural writing
A poem describing a sunset
A recipe explaining how to bake a cake

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Comparing and Ordering Fractions
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: comparing and ordering fractions involves finding a common denominator or using benchmarks, such as one-half, to determine which fraction is greater or smaller.

quiz.q: One way to compare two fractions with different denominators is to first find a ___.
options: Common numerator only
Whole number with no fraction involved
A concept unrelated to comparing fractions
Common denominator

quiz.q: Which fraction is greater: 3/4 or 1/2?
options: Both are exactly equal
A value unrelated to the comparison

quiz.q: Which fraction is smaller: 2/5 or 3/5?
options: A value unrelated to the comparison
Both are exactly equal

quiz.q: Which list of fractions is ordered from least to greatest?
options: A list unrelated to the comparison

quiz.q: Why might using a benchmark such as one-half help when comparing fractions?
options: It gives a quick reference point to judge whether a fraction is greater or less than half
One-half can only be used when comparing whole numbers
Benchmarks never help when comparing fractions
A reason unrelated to comparing fractions

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Types of Clouds and Weather Prediction
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: different cloud types, such as cumulus, stratus, and cirrus, form in different conditions and can give clues about upcoming weather.

quiz.q: Cumulus clouds are typically described as ___.
options: A description unrelated to cumulus clouds
Flat sheets covering the entire sky
Thin and wispy
Puffy and cotton-like

quiz.q: Stratus clouds typically form as ___.
options: Bright, colourful patches
Flat, grey layers covering much of the sky
A description unrelated to stratus clouds
Tall, narrow columns reaching into space

quiz.q: Cirrus clouds are typically found ___.
options: Very close to the ground
High in the atmosphere and appear thin and wispy
Only during a thunderstorm
A description unrelated to cirrus clouds

quiz.q: Which cloud type is often associated with an approaching storm?
options: A cloud type unrelated to storms
Dark, towering cumulonimbus clouds
Clear skies with no clouds at all
Thin, wispy cirrus clouds

quiz.q: Why might observing clouds help people predict upcoming weather?
options: Clouds have no connection to weather conditions
Different cloud types are linked to different weather conditions
Weather can never be predicted by observing the sky
A reason unrelated to weather prediction

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: The Fishing Industry in Atlantic Canada
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Social Studies strand: the Atlantic provinces have long relied on the fishing industry, harvesting species such as cod and lobster from the ocean, which has shaped the region’s economy and communities.

quiz.q: The fishing industry has long been an important part of the economy in ___.
options: Atlantic Canada
The Prairie provinces
Northern Ontario
A region unrelated to the fishing industry

quiz.q: Which of these is a species commonly harvested by the Atlantic fishing industry?
options: Lobster
Wheat
Corn
A species unrelated to Atlantic fishing

quiz.q: Why might a decline in fish populations, such as cod, seriously affect Atlantic coastal communities?
options: A reason unrelated to the fishing industry
A decline in fish populations has no effect on coastal communities
Coastal communities never rely on fishing for their economy
Many jobs and local economies depend on the fishing industry

quiz.q: The fishing industry is considered part of which type of industry?
options: A concept unrelated to fishing
A tertiary industry that only provides services
A secondary industry that manufactures goods
A primary industry that gathers a natural resource

quiz.q: Why is it useful for students to learn about the fishing industry in Atlantic Canada?
options: The fishing industry has no connection to Atlantic Canada
A reason unrelated to social studies learning
Fishing has never taken place in Atlantic Canada
It helps them understand how geography shapes a region’s economy and communities

subjects.subject: Language
subjects.title: Reading: Skimming and Scanning for Information
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Language strand: skimming means quickly reading a text to get a general idea of its content, while scanning means quickly looking through a text to find specific information.

quiz.q: Skimming a text means reading it quickly to ___.
options: Find one specific fact only
Get a general idea of its content
A concept unrelated to skimming
Memorize every single word

quiz.q: Scanning a text means looking through it quickly to ___.
options: Read the text extremely slowly
Understand every detail in depth
A concept unrelated to scanning
Find specific information

quiz.q: Which situation is a good example of scanning?
options: Memorizing an entire poem word for word
Reading a novel slowly from cover to cover
Looking through a phone book to find one specific name
A concept unrelated to scanning

quiz.q: Why might a reader skim a chapter before reading it in detail?
options: A reason unrelated to skimming
Skimming always replaces the need to read a text closely
To get a general sense of what the chapter is about before reading closely
Skimming never helps a reader prepare to read a text

quiz.q: Why are skimming and scanning useful research skills?
options: A reason unrelated to research skills
They help readers find and understand information quickly and efficiently
Skimming and scanning mean exactly the same thing
These skills never help with finding information quickly

subjects.subject: Math
subjects.title: Rounding Whole Numbers to a Given Place Value
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Math strand: rounding a whole number to a given place value involves looking at the digit to the right of that place to decide whether to round up or keep the digit the same.

quiz.q: When rounding a number, you look at the digit ___ the place value you are rounding to.
options: Immediately to the left of
A position unrelated to rounding
Immediately to the right of
Two places to the left of

quiz.q: What is 452 rounded to the nearest ten?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is 3,675 rounded to the nearest hundred?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: What is 8,249 rounded to the nearest thousand?
options: A value unrelated to the calculation

quiz.q: Why might someone round a large number instead of using its exact value?
options: A reason unrelated to rounding
Rounding a number always makes a problem harder to solve
To make the number easier to work with or estimate quickly
Rounding is only useful for very small numbers

subjects.subject: Science
subjects.title: Insects and Their Characteristics
subjects.summary: Grade 5 Science strand: insects are a group of invertebrates with three main body parts -- the head, thorax, and abdomen -- along with six legs and, in most species, wings.

quiz.q: An insect’s body is divided into three main parts: the head, thorax, and ___.
options: Shell
Abdomen
Backbone
A body part unrelated to insects

quiz.q: How many legs does a typical insect have?
options: Four
A number unrelated to insects
Six
Eight

quiz.q: Which of these is classified as an insect?
options: A concept unrelated to insects
A worm
A spider
An ant

quiz.q: Why are spiders not classified as insects, even though both are invertebrates?
options: Spiders have eight legs and two main body parts, unlike an insect’s six legs and three body parts
Spiders and insects have exactly the same body structure
Spiders are actually a type of vertebrate
A reason unrelated to classifying insects

quiz.q: Why might scientists study the characteristics that define an insect?
options: All invertebrates are classified as insects
It helps them accurately classify and compare different invertebrate species
Studying insect characteristics provides no useful scientific information
A reason unrelated to classifying living things

subjects.subject: SocialStudies
subjects.title: Charities and Non-Profit Organizations in Canada
